hierarchyNote: Elevation discrepancy: given data lists 7,787 ft, but multiple authoritative sources (Mountain Project, SummitPost) consistently report 7,760 ft. Peak coordinates 48.51291, -120.65557 verified via Mountain Project. Geographic placement in Liberty Bell Group, Washington Pass, North Cascades is correct.
blurb: North Early Winters Spire is the most difficult summit of the Liberty Bell Group in the North Cascades near Washington Pass, rising to 7,760 feet above granite walls. The peak was first ascended on May 28, 1950 by Wesley Grande, Pete Schoening, and Dick Widrig via the Southwest Face Route. The excellent granite offers multiple established alpine rock routes with no lines easier than 5.7, making it a premier destination for experienced climbers.

routes.overview: Classic alpine rock route ascending the northwest corner of the peak via a system of cracks and corners to a tree-covered ledge, then continuing left and up to the summit. The route features excellent granite climbing with well-defined protection placements.
routes.beta: The Northwest Corner is a well-established four-to-five pitch route that ascends the natural corner system on the northwest aspect of North Early Winters Spire. This is one of the most popular routes on the peak, known for reliable rock quality and manageable protection requirements. The route follows a clear line with sustained climbing throughout.
routes.fa: Paul Boving, Steve Pollack (first ascent September 25, 1976)
routes.approach: 1-2 hours
routes.descent: From the summit area, traverse south and west, then rappel with a 60-meter rope down a gully/chimney system. Two more single-rope rappels bring you to the notch between North and South Early Winter Spires. From the notch, descend the class 2-3 loose gully heading east (direction of the approach trail). Rappel off new bolts atop a chockstone, then walk down a grassy gully for approximately 50 feet before downclimbing or taking a final short rappel to the base. Use single-rope rappels rather than double-rope technique for this descent.
routes.descentText: The descent involves multiple rappels totaling approximately 500-600 feet of vertical. Start by rappelling from a slung tree approximately 60 feet below the summit with a 60-meter rope down the main gully/chimney (two single-rope rappels get you to the notch between the two spires). From the notch, descend the loose class 2-3 gully heading east. Continue with a rappel off bolts at the top of a large chockstone (free-hanging rappel), then walk down another grassy gully to approximately 50 feet above ground level. Exit via downclimbing or a short final rappel to reach the base. Allow 1-2 hours for the entire descent.
routes.bestSeason: July and August provide the most stable weather and quickest snow melt. The route can be attempted as early as late June but snow and ice may still be present at higher elevations. September remains viable but weather becomes increasingly unpredictable. October onward and before late June are not recommended due to significant snow coverage and avalanche risk.
knownHazards: Chimney section on pitch 1 has limited protection options
Wide offwidth pitch 4 requires large protection and technical foot placement
Loose rock common on descent gully, especially after recent weather events
Rope pulls during rappels can trigger rockfall
Fixed anchors at base and descent may require inspection before reliance
objHaz: Rockfall hazard, especially during or after precipitation or when multiple parties on route
proTips: Bring extra large gear (4-5 inch cams) for pitch 3 and especially pitch 4
Use long runners on pitches 2 and 5 to reduce rope drag on traverse pitches
The chimney on pitch 1 is short and exposed but manageable with good footwork - don't hesitate on this section
Plan the descent carefully; mark your anchor locations during the ascent to speed descent
Single-rope rappels work better than double-rope rappels on this descent due to the angles of the gully
Start early to allow daylight for finding anchors and managing the multi-rappel descent
watchOut: Protect the wide-crack sections carefully - most parties place 4 and 5 inch pieces
The offwidth pitch (P4) is the crux; mentally prepare for this exposure and technical climbing
Loose rock is common on the descent gully, especially after recent storms - move carefully and communicate with your partner
Rockfall hazard increases with multiple parties on the route during peak season - consider climbing midweek or in shoulder season
Rappel anchor inspection is critical; do not rely on old slings or bolts without careful examination
gear: Trad rack to 4-5 inches with emphasis on larger pieces
Long runners (extended slings) for rope management
Slings or webbing for backup anchors
routes.detailedRack: Standard trad rack building from 0.5 to 5 inches, with emphasis on pieces in the 3-5 inch range for the wide cracks on pitch 3 and the offwidth crux on pitch 4. Bring at least 2-3 cams in the 4-5 inch size. Long runners (24-30 inch) are essential for managing rope drag on the traversing pitches (especially pitches 2 and 5). Include quickdraws, carabiners, and slings for anchor setup and rappel management.
routes.proNeeds: The route requires traditional rock climbing protection throughout with emphasis on larger gear. The crux pitch (P4) is an offwidth that demands solid technique and proper large-cam placement. Protection is generally available throughout, but the chimney section on P1 has fewer options and requires creative placement.
whatToBring: Headlamp or light source for backup
Rain jacket or shelter (weather can change rapidly)
First aid kit with blister treatment

timing.recommendedStart: Sunrise or early morning (5:00-6:30 AM typical) to allow 10+ hours of daylight for approach, climb, and descent

pitchDetail.notes: Climb the crack/corner to the tree-covered ledge. The lower section is a chimney that lacks much protection but is short and relatively straightforward with good footwork. Approximately 45m.
Traverse left through trees and up a corner system to the belay ledge. Use long runners to reduce rope drag. Approximately 50m.
Wide crack and flake system requiring large protection gear. This section demands careful placement of 4-5 inch cams. Approximately 30m.
The crux pitch - an offwidth section combining smearing and arm placement. Described as the 'surprisingly secure offwidth.' Requires commitment and proper technique. Approximately 25m.
Traverse around a roof system, continuing to the summit. Use long runners to manage rope drag on the traverse. Approximately 30m.

emergency.nearestHospital: Skagit Valley Hospital, Mount Vernon, WA (approximately 60 miles west via Highway 20)
emergency.rangerStation: North Cascades National Park Visitor Center, Marblemount (approximately 50 miles west); Washington Pass ranger station (seasonal)
emergency.notes: Dial 911 for emergencies. Cell coverage is unreliable on the route; Verizon often provides better coverage than other carriers. Consider satellite communicator for solo climbers or remote areas. The area is within Skagit County jurisdiction.

routes.comms: Cell phone coverage is unreliable on the route and summit; Verizon generally provides the best coverage among carriers. Coverage at the trailhead is variable. Satellite communication device (satellite phone or personal locator beacon) recommended for emergencies in this remote alpine location.

access.landManager: Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest
access.fees: Northwest Forest Pass required: $5 for day use or $30 annual pass
access.passRequired: Northwest Forest Pass (interagency pass accepted, or America the Beautiful Pass)
access.closures: Highway 20 closed in winter (typically November-April), making the area inaccessible

road.name: State Highway 20 (North Cascades Scenic Highway)
road.status: Open June-October (typical)
road.seasonalGate: Highway 20 is seasonally gated, typically closed November-April
road.driveNote: Approach from the west: 42.1 miles from Newhalem on Highway 20 to Blue Lake Trailhead on the right; do not pass the Washington Pass Overlook. Approach from the east: 31.3 miles from Winthrop on Highway 20 to trailhead on the left, just under 1 mile past the Washington Pass Overlook. The trailhead parking area has approximately 20 spots and is frequently full during peak season.

climate.forecastZone: North Cascades Zone (NW Avalanche Center)
climate.typical: Alpine climate with rapid weather changes. Temperature decreases approximately 3.5 degrees F per 1,000 feet elevation gain.
climate.spring: April-May: Significant snow and ice in the high country; avalanche risk; some routes (like Early Winter Couloir) in prime condition late April-early May; Highway 20 typically opens in late April or early May.
climate.summer: June-September: Most stable and predictable season; warm days with cool nights; afternoon thunderstorms possible; July-August: warmest and driest; early June and late September can still have snow at higher elevations.
climate.fall: September-October: Increasing precipitation and cloud cover; weather becomes less stable; nighttime freezes possible; Highway 20 typically closes in November.
climate.winter: November-April: Heavy snow, avalanche risk, extended cold spells; Highway 20 closed; approach requires 16+ mile winter approach due to road closure.

waypoints.name: Climber's Trail Junction to Liberty Bell Basin

routes.overview: Pleasant Class 3-4 volcanic scramble from Pinnacle Saddle to summit via multiple gully options on volcanic basalt and andesite. South-facing exposure with loose rock throughout requires careful route selection.
routes.beta: From Pinnacle-Plummer saddle, scramble west/southwest up obvious gullies with multiple line options. Stay on most-traveled route for least loose rock. Route-finding is straightforward but exposure increases with altitude. Final push to summit is the steepest and most difficult section, with some variations described as low 5th class.
routes.approach: Park at Reflection Lakes (46.7683, -121.7314) and follow the signed Pinnacle Saddle Trail. Well-maintained rocky trail with switchbacks through subalpine meadows and forest. Trail becomes steep but remains distinct. Reach Pinnacle-Plummer saddle at 5920 feet after 1.3 miles and approximately 1050 feet of elevation gain.
routes.descent: Descend via same gullies back to Pinnacle-Plummer saddle, then return down the maintained Pinnacle Saddle Trail. Loose volcanic scree and gravel create treacherous footing.
routes.descentText: Return down the same rocky gullies to Pinnacle-Plummer saddle, exercising extreme care on loose rock and exposed sections. Follow the maintained Pinnacle Saddle Trail switchbacks back to trailhead. Volcanic gravel and loose rock on descent are slippery; trekking poles recommended for stability on rocky sections.
routes.turnaround: Turn around if snow obscures trail or late afternoon thunderstorm develops. Do not continue scrambling if weather deteriorates or loose rock hazard appears unsafe. Safe turnaround time is typically early afternoon.
routes.bestSeason: Late June through mid-September is optimal. Snow can persist into early July on north-facing sections. South-facing aspect means route dries quickly in spring. Avoid winter and spring due to avalanche hazard on approach slopes. Afternoon thunderstorms are common in summer.
routes.proNeeds: Rock protection is generally not necessary for the standard route, but scrambling groups should carry light protective gear for emergency use. Rock quality is highly variable due to loose volcanic composition.
gear: Sturdy hiking boots with ankle support
Microspikes or crampons (early/late season with snow)
proTips: Follow the most-traveled line as heavy use has polished the best rock
Watch for loose rock hazard when choosing route variations near the summit
Start early to avoid afternoon thunderstorms which develop frequently in summer
South-facing aspect means route is often snow-free when other Tatoosh peaks still have snow
watchOut: Loose volcanic rock is the primary hazard; test holds and be deliberate with foot placement
Steep drop-offs with exposure; stay on established route
Late afternoon thunderstorms develop rapidly; be off high exposed terrain by early afternoon
Rock ranges from almost solid to poor quality; route selection is critical
knownHazards: Volcanic basalt and andesite rock is highly variable in stability; loose blocks common
Exposure on south face with steep drop-offs
Afternoon thunderstorm risk June through September
Snow or ice on north-facing approach slopes early season
Avalanche potential on approach slopes in winter/spring

timing.recommendedStart: Early morning before 8am

routes.bail: Retreat down any of the gullies to Pinnacle-Plummer saddle if conditions deteriorate. If caught in weather at higher elevation, descend quickly to saddle and return via well-marked trail to Reflection Lakes.
routes.comms: Cell phone reception is unreliable in the Tatoosh Range. Weather radio or personal locator beacon recommended for emergencies. Notify someone of your plans.

road.name: Stevens Canyon Road
road.status: Open mid-June through late September
road.seasonalGate: Stevens Canyon Road closes in winter; check NPS website for current conditions
road.driveNote: Reflection Lakes parking area is 1.5 miles east of Paradise junction via Stevens Canyon Road. Parking area accommodates approximately 35 vehicles with overflow areas.

access.landManager: National Park Service - Mount Rainier National Park
access.permit: No climbing permit required for day hikes
access.passRequired: Mount Rainier National Park entrance pass ($30 vehicle pass, 7-day validity)
access.fees: $30 per private vehicle (7-day pass); $55 annual pass available
access.closures: No closure during normal operating season. No camping allowed in the Pinnacle-Castle area.

climate.forecastZone: NWS Seattle/Tacoma Office - Mount Rainier Recreational Forecast
climate.typical: Alpine meadow environment with variable weather. Afternoon thunderstorms common June-September.
climate.summer: Warm and dry with afternoon thunderstorm potential. Average high near 60F at trailhead. Freezing level 12,000-13,500 feet.
climate.spring: Snow persists into early July. Avalanche potential on approach slopes. South-facing route dries early.
climate.fall: Clear and stable. September offers best conditions with lower storm risk.
climate.winter: Significant snow and avalanche hazard. Not recommended; Pinnacle Saddle Trail typically closes with snow.

emergency.nearestHospital: Tacoma General Hospital or St. Joseph Medical Center, Tacoma (approximately 45 miles)
emergency.rangerStation: Longmire Wilderness Information Center: (360) 569-6650 (May 25-Oct 8, 7:30am-5:00pm). General park: (360) 569-2211.
emergency.sheriffDispatch: Mount Rainier National Park dispatch: 911 or (360) 569-2211
emergency.notes: Report injuries or emergencies to ranger via park phone or emergency service. Longmire ranger station can assist with backcountry support.

routes.corrections: Database elevation listed as 6566 ft; authoritative sources (NPS, SummitPost, Mountain Project, AllTrails) consistently document 6562 ft. Recommend correcting database record to 6562 ft.

blurb: Pinnacle Peak is a 6,562-foot volcanic peak in the Tatoosh Range, within Mount Rainier National Park. Composed of old lava flows of basalt and andesite, it is the second highest and most photographed peak in the range. The Mazamas first ascended it on July 26, 1897, naming it the 'Matterhorn of the Cascades'.
hierarchyNote: Elevation discrepancy: database lists 6566 ft but authoritative sources (NPS, SummitPost, Mountain Project, AllTrails) consistently document 6562 ft.

blurb: Mount Shuksan (9,131 ft) is one of the most photographed peaks in North America, rising dramatically in the North Cascades immediately east of Mount Baker. Composed of metamorphosed oceanic basalt (Shuksan greenschist), the massif is heavily glaciated with a distinctive summit pyramid and features multiple technical climbing routes. The peak was first climbed on September 7, 1906 by Asahel Curtis and party.

routes.overview: The second most popular route on Mount Shuksan, weaving through steep and exposed chimney systems on the Shuksan Arm with multiple glacier crossings. A classic alpine climb combining rock scrambling and glacier travel.
routes.fa: Clarence Fisher, Esther Buswell, Paul Hugdahl, Lars Loveseth, Winnie Spieseke, and Harriet Taylor
routes.beta: The route weaves through a steep and exposed chimney system on the Shuksan Arm, briefly touching the top of White Salmon Glacier, up a steep snow slope (Winnie's Slide), across the Upper Curtis Glacier, and up Hell's Highway to Sulphide Glacier before finishing at the 3/4 class summit pyramid. Most parties take 2-3 days.
routes.approach: Starting from Lake Ann Trail above Mt. Baker Ski Resort, climbers follow maintained trail past the lake into the Curtis Glacier valley. Significant routefinding through trees, scree, and snow to reach the base of the Shuksan Arm chimneys. Follow established boot packs and trails, avoiding mossy/lichen-covered rock.
routes.descent: Reverse the ascent route with rappel stations in the chimneys for 4th class sections, though exposed down climbing remains necessary.
routes.descentText: Descend via the same route. Rappel stations with rap rings are typically present in chimney sections for lowering loads. The descent requires careful downclimbing of exposed terrain, particularly in the chimney sections and on Winnie's Slide.
routes.bestSeason: July through October are ideal months. Late July to August is prime time due to conditions and weather, with established boot tracks and better snow consolidation. June and September are possible but require careful avalanche assessment.
routes.detailedRack: Ice axe, crampons (essential), picket(s), full crevasse rescue setup (harness, rope, pulleys, carabiners). In late season when Winnie's Slide is ice, you may need ice screws and a second tool depending on your comfort level. Basic rock rack for rappels.
routes.proNeeds: Full glacier travel and crevasse rescue capability required. Ability to self-rescue from a crevasse is essential.
proTips: Follow established boot packs - this is critical for routefinding
Avoid mossy/lichen-covered rock (indicates wrong route)
Don't overthink route selection despite apparent complexity
Watch for rappel stations marking proper passages
Get an early start to avoid afternoon heat on snow slopes
watchOut: Easy to get off-route; consequences can be serious
Moats and crevasses hidden beneath snow bridges
Late-season conditions may require additional ice climbing equipment
Steep exposed terrain with serious fall consequences
Rappel anchor compatibility issues with modern ropes
knownHazards: Exposed terrain with cliff bands and potential for falls
Crevasse fields requiring careful navigation
Loose rock and rockfall in chimney systems
Avalanche risk from new snow and steep slopes
Poor weather can move in quickly

routes.overview: The most popular and easiest route on Mount Shuksan, involving moderate glacier travel and straightforward snow climbing to the summit pyramid.
routes.beta: From the campsites (6,245 ft for lower camps), head north up the west side of the Sulphide Glacier up rolling steps. At approximately 7,600 feet, terrain flattens and route continues northeast toward summit pyramid. Standard route involves 3rd class gullies to the summit, though alternate Southeast Ridge offers mostly 4th class with some low 5th class.
routes.approach: From Lake Ann trailhead or Curtis Glacier approach. Most parties camp at 6,200-6,245 ft elevation on Lower Curtis Glacier or Upper Curtis campsite, then climb to Sulphide Glacier base.
routes.descent: Downclimb or use webbing anchors with rap rings typically present
routes.descentText: Reverse the ascent route. Established rappel anchors with webbing and rap rings are typically maintained on descent. Downclimbing is possible on lower angle sections.
routes.bestSeason: July through October ideal. Late July to August prime time. June possible with heavy snowpack but avalanche risk higher.
routes.detailedRack: Ice axe, crampons (essential), picket(s), full crevasse rescue setup. Rappel hardware for existing anchors.
routes.proNeeds: Glacier travel and crevasse awareness. Self-rescue capability important.
proTips: Most straightforward route on mountain
Follow boot tracks on approach and descent
Excellent acclimatization route for less experienced alpinists

Weak snow bridges in afternoon heat
Avalanche risk from Hanging Glacier above
Hidden crevasses near apparent easy terrain

routes.overview: A serious snow and ice climb on Mount Shuksan's most impressive aspect, gaining 5,500 feet of elevation through sustained snow and ice slopes reaching 45-50 degrees.
routes.beta: Climb 45-50 degree snow/ice until steepening to 50+ degrees just before topping out onto shoulder between Crystal and Hanging Glacier. Route involves sustained moderate alpine ice climbing.
routes.approach: From White Salmon Lodge (accessed via Mount Baker Highway), descend northeast toward White Salmon Creek. With good snow cover, leisurely travel to glacier toe. Without snow, contour around clearcut area while surrendering elevation to avoid rock bands and stream crossings.
routes.descent: Reverse the climbing route or descend White Salmon Glacier.
routes.descentText: Most parties descend via White Salmon Glacier to complete the loop back to the lodge. Alternatively, reverse the ascending route on the North Face, though this involves downclimbing steep ice.
routes.bestSeason: July-August only; later seasons have serious schrund barriers
routes.detailedRack: Two ice axes, crampons, 6-8 ice screws, picket(s), full crevasse rescue setup. Late season may require additional ice tools.
routes.proNeeds: Expert ice climbing skills required. Ability to place ice anchors and belay from ice.

Bivy sites available between White Salmon and Price Glaciers

routes.overview: A famous and steep alpine ice route on Mount Shuksan's northeast aspect, made legendary by inclusion in 'Fifty Classic Climbs of North America.' Features sustained alpine ice climbing up to AI-3 grade through broken, glaciated terrain.
routes.fa: Fred Beckey, Jack Schwabland, Bill Granston (1945)
routes.beta: Cross beneath Nooksack Tower to approximately 6,500 feet, then step onto Price Glacier. Navigate steep snow and alpine ice grades AI-2 to AI-3 around seracs and sloughs. At approximately 7,800 feet, reach bergschrund with three options: rock to the left, ice line up middle, or mixed terrain to the right. Ascend easy gullies (4th-5th class) on south side to summit.
routes.approach: Take the Price XC zone access via White Salmon Lodge area or from Curtis Glacier basin. Approach takes better part of a day with bivouac sites on flat rocks below ridge crest and exposed saddle below Nooksack Tower.
routes.descent: Typically descend via Fisher Chimneys route
routes.descentText: Most parties descend via Fisher Chimneys to complete the loop. Reverse the climbing route is possible but involves difficult downclimbing of steep ice.
routes.bestSeason: Early season (June-July) with heavy snowpack for better bridged crevasses; mid-August average year conditions are challenging
routes.detailedRack: Two ice axes, crampons, 6-8 ice screws, 2-3 pickets, small rock rack for mixed sections, full crevasse rescue setup including pulleys and carabiners
routes.proNeeds: Expert alpine ice climbing skills. Ability to place ice anchors, protect steep ice pitches, and manage crevasse rescue.

Giant crevasses and bergschrunds more solidly bridged early season

blurb: Colfax Peak is a 9,445-foot subsidiary of Mount Baker in the North Cascades, part of the Black Buttes volcanic complex. Known for reliable alpine ice climbing conditions due to its high elevation, north-facing aspect, and geothermal heat, it hosts some of the most consistent ice routes in western Washington.

routes.bestSeason: Late October through May, with the pillar forming as early as October and fattening as the season progresses. Early season (October-November) conditions can push grades to WI5; later season (January-May) tends toward solid WI4 with better fattened ice.
routes.overview: The Cosley-Houston is the easiest and most direct route on the north face of Colfax Peak, featuring sustained water ice climbing and a notable short crux pillar. A popular entry-level alpine ice climb for experienced climbers.
routes.beta: Approach via the Coleman Glacier to the base of Colfax Peak's north face. Start just above the bergschrund with a low-angle ice ramp trending up to the base of the crux pillar. The crux pillar is short and steep, slightly overhung, with approximately 30-40 feet of dead vertical or slightly overhanging water ice. After the crux, continue up a steep snow gully trending right, then simul-climb upper snow slopes to the summit ridge. A working knowledge of ice screw placement and anchor construction is essential.
routes.approach: Begin at Heliotrope Ridge Trailhead (via Glacier Creek Road FSR 39, 8 miles from Glacier, WA). Hike the Heliotrope Ridge Trail (5.4 miles, 1,860 feet elevation gain) to the Coleman Glacier. From the glacier edge, navigate south and east toward the Coleman-Deming camp at approximately 8,200 feet. From camp, head toward Colfax Peak's north face. Total approach time: 6-8 hours including ski/boot approach to glacier.
routes.descent: Descend via the Coleman Glacier in daylight to avoid crevasse hazards. Traverse back to the western side of the glacier where powder skiing or booting down Heliotrope Ridge completes the descent. Total descent time: 2-4 hours depending on snow conditions.
routes.descentText: The descent requires careful navigation of the Coleman Glacier, as crevasses are dynamic and change seasonally. Descend in daylight whenever possible. Once back at the Heliotrope Ridge area, you can ski or boot down. If skis are not available, the descent is significantly longer and more tedious.
routes.turnaround: Turn around if ice conditions appear unstable or if you have not reached the crux pillar by mid-afternoon. Whiteout conditions are common; if visibility drops below safe levels, descend immediately via the same route you came.
knownHazards: Avalanche risk on 45-degree slopes above the crux pitch, particularly after significant snowfall or during elevated avalanche conditions
Crevasse hazards on Coleman Glacier with dynamic bridging conditions
Rockfall from the northwest-facing aspect, especially during afternoon warming or after precipitation
Difficult anchor construction in brittle early-season ice
Whiteout conditions common at this elevation and aspect
watchOut: Early starts (3-4 AM) are essential to avoid afternoon warming and rockfall
Stay lower and left on the approach to avoid large crevasses on the glacier
Ascend the crux pillar early in the climb before cumulative fatigue sets in
Do not descend after dark—crevasse navigation becomes extremely hazardous
Microspikes recommended for the Heliotrope Ridge approach trail, especially in fall when trail is icy
proTips: The pillar fattens considerably as the season progresses; early season (October) can be WI5 in places, while January-May tends toward solid WI4
Bring 10 ice screws minimum—having 2 at each belay and 6 on the crux prevents running short on the technical pitches
Use snow pickets in addition to ice screws for solid anchor placement in the upper snow slopes
If skis are available, use them on the approach to save energy for the climb
The route is frequently less crowded than the adjacent Polish Route; good conditions can be found when the Polish Route is out of shape
gear: Crampons (for glacier travel and mixed sections)
Snow pickets and/or ice protection for upper snowfield belays
Microspikes or crampons for trail approach
routes.detailedRack: A mixed rack of 10 ice screws covering sizes from 0.3 to 0.5 inches is ideal. Carry 2 screws for each belay (typically 3 belays) and 6 screws for the crux pitch. Bring a few snow pickets or flukes for upper snowfield anchors. A single 60-meter rope is standard; some parties prefer twin ropes for redundancy and faster rappels if needed.
routes.proNeeds: Protection is essential on this route. Ice screws are mandatory for all belayed pitches. The crux pillar demands careful screw placement at the crux itself, and the upper pitches benefit from backup protection in the snowfield.
whatToBring: Gloves (mittens for extreme cold, glove liners for dexterity)
Insulated boots rated to -20°F or colder
High-calorie snacks (energy bars, nuts, dried fruit)
2-3 liters of water or electrolyte drink

timing.recommendedStart: 3:00-4:00 AM from trailhead for alpine start

climate.forecastZone: Northwest Avalanche Center - Cascade West South Zone
climate.typical: High elevation alpine climate with significant precipitation, frequent wind, and cold temperatures. North-facing aspect provides consistent, reliable ice conditions.
climate.spring: March-May: Daytime highs 20-35°F, nighttime lows -5 to 10°F. Freeze-thaw cycles create optimal ice conditions. Increased avalanche hazard after warm, sunny days.
climate.summer: June-August: Glacier travel becomes dangerous due to crevasse exposure. Route rarely in condition. Most snow melts, exposing rock hazards.
climate.fall: September-November: Rapid cooling and early season snows create brittle ice. October-November: Heliotrope Trail becomes icy. Route forms early in season.
climate.winter: December-February: Coldest conditions, -10 to -20°F common. Brittle ice frequent. Shorter days limit climbing windows.

access.landManager: Mount Baker-Snoqualmie National Forest, USDA Forest Service
access.fees: Northwest Forest Pass ($5/day or $30/annual) required for parking at Heliotrope Ridge Trailhead
access.passRequired: Northwest Forest Pass or Interagency Recreation Pass (Washington State Discover Pass NOT valid)
access.closures: Glacier Creek Road (FSR 39) subject to seasonal closures; verify road status before heading out. Check with Glacier Public Service Center (360-599-2714).

road.name: Mount Baker Highway (WA-542) and Glacier Creek Road (FSR 39)
road.status: Typically open October through May; verify seasonal closures with ranger station
road.seasonalGate: Glacier Creek Road may close for snow or maintenance; check USFS Mount Baker-Snoqualmie website
road.driveNote: From Glacier, WA, travel 8 miles on FSR 39 to Heliotrope Ridge Trailhead. Road can be rough; high-clearance vehicles recommended in winter. Parking lot fills on weekends.

emergency.rangerStation: Glacier Public Service Center: 10091 Mt. Baker Highway, Glacier, WA 98244; (360) 599-2714
emergency.nearestHospital: PeaceHealth St. Joseph Medical Center, 2901 Squalicum Parkway, Bellingham, WA 98225; (360) 734-5400; approximately 45 minutes by helicopter
emergency.notes: Cell phone reception is unreliable at Colfax Peak elevation. Carry a personal locator beacon (PLB) or satellite communicator for emergencies. Whatcom County Fire Protection Districts 16 and 19 serve the Mount Baker area.

pitchDetail.notes: Low-angle alpine ice ramp from bergschrund to crux pillar base. Approximately 65 meters. Simul-climbing possible on easier sections.

The crux—short, brittle ice pillar, slightly overhung. Early season can grade to WI5; later season fattens to solid WI4. Approximately 30-40 feet of dead vertical or slightly overhanging water ice. Most difficult pitch of the route.
Awkward ice step (bypassable via right-side snow) followed by simul-climbing on upper snow slopes toward the summit ridge.
Final steep snow and ice to summit area, approximately 45 degrees. Short scramble to true summit.

routes.comms: Cell phone reception is unreliable to absent at Colfax Peak elevation. Satellite communicators or personal locator beacons are recommended for emergencies. No maintained communication infrastructure on the mountain.
routes.corrections: Mountain Project lists elevation as 9,439 ft; Peakbagger and official sources confirm 9,445 ft. Cosley-Houston alternate spelling 'Colsey-Houston' appears in some sources but 'Cosley-Houston' is standard.

routes.bestSeason: Late October through May. Best conditions typically January-March when ice is consistent and weather patterns are established. Early season (October-November) can be brittle; late season (April-May) can slush during afternoon sun.
routes.overview: The Polish Route is a classic and serious alpine ice climbing objective on the north face of Colfax Peak. A perfect center ice line with an unmistakeable free-hanging ice dagger crux, it ranks among the best alpine ice routes in the North Cascades.
routes.beta: Approach via Coleman Glacier to the base of Colfax Peak's north face, directly below the middle of the face. Ascend the initial bergschrund crossing (may require traversal from the right if overhanging). Pitch 1: Long pitch of WI4+ to a snow slope. Pitch 2 (crux): Steep ice for approximately 30 feet, easing briefly, then rising to a free-hanging ice dagger grading WI6. This is the technical and psychological crux requiring bold movement and precise tool placement. Above the dagger: additional steep ice steps in a right-side gully. Final pitches: Upper snowfield with occasional blue ice patches, traversing between rock buttresses to gain the summit snowfield.
routes.approach: Begin at Heliotrope Ridge Trailhead (8 miles from Glacier, WA on FSR 39). Hike Heliotrope Ridge Trail (5.4 miles, 1,860 feet gain) to Coleman Glacier area. From glacier, navigate southeast toward the Coleman-Deming camp (~8,200 feet). From camp, head toward the middle of Colfax Peak's north face. Total approach time: 6-8 hours including glacier travel.
routes.descent: Descend via the same route. Traverse back to Coleman Glacier, then navigate down to Heliotrope Ridge. Descend in daylight to avoid crevasse navigation in darkness. Total descent time: 2-4 hours depending on snow conditions.
routes.descentText: The descent from the north face should begin as soon as practical to avoid afternoon warming and rockfall hazard. Carefully traverse back across the upper snowfield to gain the descent gullies. Once on the Coleman Glacier, move deliberately to avoid crevasses—the bridge you crossed on approach may be different on descent. Use deliberate boot steps or ski if possible. Total descent from summit to camp: 1-1.5 hours. Camp to trailhead: 2-2.5 hours of glacier travel plus 2-3 hours hiking.
routes.turnaround: Turn around if you have not reached the base of the crux dagger by mid-afternoon. If weather deteriorates, descend immediately via the route of ascent. Do not attempt the crux during whiteout or low-visibility conditions.
knownHazards: Hanging ice dagger crux at WI6—this is serious, exposed climbing with limited protection options
Thin ice sections requiring careful tool placement and balance
Spindrift showers from the upper face, particularly when wind is from the west
Bergschrund crossing at the base—may be overhanging and require careful route-finding
Crevasse hazards on Coleman Glacier with dynamic conditions
Objective hazard: rockfall from higher peaks and ledges during warming periods
watchOut: The crux dagger is the crux in every sense—it is bold, exposed, and serious. Only attempt if you are confident in your WI6 ice climbing ability.
Thin ice on the approach pitches can be deceptive. Test placements carefully before committing body weight.
Spindrift accumulation on the face is common; wear a helmet and consider positioning to avoid exposure to rockfall
The bergschrund may require creative route-finding. Scout it carefully during approach.
The route has only been ascended a handful of times since the first ascent in 2000; conditions may vary significantly from trip report to trip report
proTips: This is a serious, committing climb. Only undertake it if you have recent WI6 ice climbing experience and strong decision-making skills.
The crux dagger can be attempted from different approaches depending on its shape—scout it from below before committing to a line.
A tight rack of small ice screws (0.25-0.35 inch) is better than larger screws for the thin ice sections.
If conditions are poor on the dagger, consider bailing down and attempting the Cosley-Houston instead.
This route is less crowded than the Cosley-Houston; you will likely be alone or in a small party.
gear: Ice screws (10-12, emphasizing smaller sizes for thin ice)
routes.detailedRack: A full rack of 10-12 ice screws, emphasizing smaller diameter screws (0.25-0.35 inch) for the thin ice sections on the approach and crux pitches. Larger screws (0.45-0.5 inch) can be used in thicker sections. Carry at least 3 screws for belays and 6-8 for the crux. A few snow pickets for upper snowfield anchors. Consider bringing a 60-meter rope plus a 30-meter half-rope for the descent.
routes.proNeeds: Protection is mandatory and serious. The route requires confident ice screw placement in thin ice. The crux dagger may accept only one screw per team, making this a bold, committing pitch. Upper snowfield pitches benefit from solid anchor construction using both ice screws and snow pickets.
whatToBring: Thermal base layers and mid-layer insulation
Helmet (essential given rockfall and spindrift hazard)
High-calorie food (energy bars, nuts, chocolate, nut butter packets)
3+ liters of water or electrolyte drink
Bivy sack or emergency shelter (prudent given weather exposure)

timing.recommendedStart: 2:00-3:00 AM from trailhead for early alpine start

climate.forecastZone: Northwest Avalanche Center - Cascade West South Zone
climate.typical: High elevation alpine climate. North-facing aspect provides consistent ice formation. Significant precipitation, frequent wind, and cold. Weather can change rapidly.
climate.spring: March-May: Daytime highs 15-30°F, lows -5 to 10°F. Best climbing window with stable freeze-thaw cycles. Afternoon warming increases rockfall hazard. Avalanche danger elevated after warm days.
climate.summer: June-August: Route rarely in condition. Excessive melting, rockfall, and crevasse exposure. Unsuitable for climbing.
climate.fall: September-November: Rapid seasonal cooling creates brittle ice initially. October-November: Route forms. Early season conditions can be featured but brittle.
climate.winter: December-February: Coldest conditions (-10 to -20°F common). Brittle ice frequent. Shorter days limit climbing windows. Weather windows can be narrow but conditions can be excellent.

access.landManager: Mount Baker-Snoqualmie National Forest, USDA Forest Service
access.fees: Northwest Forest Pass ($5/day or $30/annual) required for Heliotrope Ridge Trailhead parking
access.passRequired: Northwest Forest Pass or Interagency Recreation Pass (Washington State Discover Pass NOT accepted)
access.closures: FSR 39 (Glacier Creek Road) subject to seasonal winter closures and maintenance. Verify road status before departure.

road.name: Mount Baker Highway (WA-542) and Glacier Creek Road (FSR 39)
road.status: Typically open October through May; verify conditions with USFS
road.seasonalGate: Road may close for snow or scheduled maintenance; check USFS website or call Glacier Public Service Center
road.driveNote: High-clearance vehicle recommended. From Glacier, travel 8 miles on FSR 39. Parking lot at trailhead often full on weekends; arrive early.

emergency.rangerStation: Glacier Public Service Center: 10091 Mt. Baker Highway, Glacier, WA 98244; (360) 599-2714; open 8 AM-4:30 PM daily
emergency.nearestHospital: PeaceHealth St. Joseph Medical Center, 2901 Squalicum Parkway, Bellingham, WA 98225; (360) 734-5400; approximately 45 minutes by helicopter from peak
emergency.notes: Cell reception is unreliable to absent. Satellite communicator or personal locator beacon mandatory. Whatcom County Fire Districts 16 and 19 provide SAR support for Mount Baker area.

waypoints.name: Polish Route Base / North Face Center

pitchDetail.notes: Bergschrund crossing (may require creative route-finding if overhanging) followed by a long pitch of consistent steep ice. Approximately 60-80 meters. Thin to moderate ice. Good protection opportunities in thicker sections.

The crux pitch. Approximately 30 feet of steep ice before easing briefly, then rising to a free-hanging ice dagger at WI6. The dagger is the physical and psychological crux. Tool placement is critical; balance and commitment are essential. Approximately 80-100 meters total pitch length. This is a bold, serious pitch with limited protection.
Additional steep ice steps in a right-side gully. Approximately 60 meters. Descending grade from crux but sustained and technical. Good screw placements available.
Upper snowfield with occasional patches of blue ice. Traverse between rock buttresses toward the summit snowfield. Simul-climbing is appropriate on easier sections.

routes.comms: Cell phone reception is unreliable to absent at peak elevation. Satellite communicators, personal locator beacons, or inReach-type devices are essential for emergency communication.
routes.corrections: Mountain Project lists this route as WI6 and approximately 1,000 feet. Some sources list alternate alpine grades; this represents a III-IV commitment given the serious crux and required retreat capability.

routes.fa: Fred Beckey and John Parrott, June 12, 1956
routes.bestSeason: Mid-June through early September, when snow has melted from the approach gully and rockfall hazard is diminished. July and August offer the most stable and predictable conditions, though mosquitoes are present all day.
routes.overview: The North Face is the most popular and accessible route on Concord Tower, following an obvious crack system directly up the face in three well-defined pitches. Featured granite with some loose rock characterizes the line, featuring an exposed hand traverse and a thrilling section known as the 'shark fin' with 300 feet of exposure on both sides.
routes.beta: This Grade II alpine rock climb starts at the Liberty Bell-Concord Tower notch and follows a clear crack system with minimal route-finding required. The route can be easily combined with Liberty Bell's Beckey Route as both start from the same approach gully. Moderate protection and gear-handling experience required.
routes.approach: From the Blue Lake Trailhead at mile marker 161, 1 mile west of Washington Pass on Highway 20, hike the Blue Lake Trail approximately 1.5 miles to a small meadow. Locate the climber's path on the left just after crossing a small stream. Follow the path up through slab, talus, and meadow to the base of the gully descending from the Liberty Bell-Concord Tower notch. Scramble up the gully (usually snow-filled and potentially icy until late June) for approximately 1,000 feet of elevation gain to reach the notch at 7,300 feet. Total approach: 3 miles and approximately 2,000 feet elevation gain from parking lot to the base of the climb.
routes.descent: Descend via two single-rope rappels down the south-facing side of the tower into the notch between Concord and Lexington towers. Rappel chains are bolted at the summit. The first rappel lands on a small ledge (approximately 6 inches by 3 feet); the second features a 3-foot overhang. Allow approximately 10-15 minutes for the rappels.
routes.descentText: From the summit, use the bolted rappel stations to descend the south side. Make a single-rope rappel of approximately 80-100 feet to reach a small belay ledge. From this ledge, make a second single-rope rappel of approximately 60-80 feet back to the notch, navigating the small overhang on the lower rappel. Rappel stations are marked. After rappels, scramble down the gully back toward the meadow. Expect loose rock throughout the gully; descend carefully to avoid dislodging stones onto anyone below.
routes.rappels: 2 single-rope rappels, approximately 80-100 feet and 60-80 feet
routes.comms: Cell service is unreliable in the Washington Pass area. The Blue Lake Trailhead is in a dead zone for most carriers. Satellite communicators (Garmin InReach, SPOT) are recommended for emergency communication in this remote area.
objHaz: Rockfall from climbers above or below
Loose and featured rock throughout route
Snow and ice on approach gully (early season)
Exposure and fall potential on all three pitches
Avalanche hazard on approach gully in early season
Icy conditions at the notch despite warm afternoon temperatures
knownHazards: High rockfall danger after spring snowmelt due to loose debris and weather-loosened rock
The approach gully between Liberty Bell and Concord is usually snow-filled and icy until late June, creating objective hazard
Featured and somewhat loose-looking rock throughout the route requires careful footwork and hand placement
The North Cascades Highway east of Washington Pass has the highest avalanche paths per mile in the US; early season approaches can encounter avalanche terrain on the approach slopes
The notch between Liberty Bell and Concord receives minimal sun and remains extremely cold despite warm afternoon conditions on south-facing pitches
watchOut: Rope management in a narrow gully setting where party efficiency is critical
Watch for rockfall from other parties; the approach gully can funnel stones from above
The distinctive shark fin pitch provides extreme exposure in both directions; ensure solid belay and rappel anchor placement
The hand traverse on Pitch 1 requires good footwork on smeared slab; avoid dislodging holds
Early morning or late afternoon starts can place you on descent rappels in poor light

pitchDetail.notes: V-shaped ramp with featured, somewhat loose rock. Finish with an exposed hand traverse where feet are smeared on the face. Crack system varies from fist to hand width. Wide gear (to #4-5) useful on this pitch. Belay at a small anchor tree in a nook. Approximately 100 feet.

Easy class 5.0 ridge scrambling to the distinctive shark fin feature with thrilling exposure in both directions. Au cheval move required to gain the top of the fin. Mostly 4th class scrambling with brief 5th class section. No significant protection placements. Approximately 80 feet.

Short final pitch requiring pull-up technique on the rock fin to reach the summit. Featured with moderate exposure. Small, positive holds lead to the summit block. Approximately 120 feet. Rappel anchors are bolted at summit.

gear: Full rack from fingers to #6 cams (double set #1-3, singles #4-6)
Double rope (two 60m ropes recommended for rappels)
Helmet (strongly recommended due to rockfall potential)
routes.detailedRack: Bring a double rack of cams up to #3, plus singles of #4, #5, and #6 cams. The wide crux sections on Pitches 1 and 3 require the wider placements, though they are short sections. A single #6 (or #5) and single #4 with doubles to #3 would be the minimum sufficient rack if weight is a concern. The #6 cam is more useful than #5 in the specific crack formations encountered. Supplementary passive protection (nuts) is minimal due to the crack geometry. Consider a short rack of supplementary stoppers or small nuts for variety.
routes.proNeeds: This is a moderate alpine rock route suitable for intermediate to advanced climbers comfortable leading 5.7 granite cracks and managing altitude exposure. Solid anchor placement, rappel anchor evaluation, and efficient belay transitions are essential given the remote location and narrow gully approach.
proTips: Start early to maximize daylight and minimize exposure to afternoon rockfall risk
Bring insulating layers despite warm forecast; the notch between peaks receives minimal sun and is noticeably colder
Scout the approach the night before if possible to locate the climber's path in the meadow

The notch between Liberty Bell and Concord is an efficient hub for combining multiple peaks in one day; many climbers climb both Liberty Bell's Beckey Route and Concord Tower's North Face back-to-back
Use micro-stoppers on Pitch 2 if you want additional protection points, though this pitch is often soloed by competent parties
The sandy/loose ledges on Pitch 1 require active foot placement; smearing is effective here
routes.turnaround: Plan turnarounds based on daylength and sun position, not clock time. No later than 2:00 p.m. ascent time allows for a 4:30 p.m. or earlier exit from the gully before full darkness. Early morning starts (dawn or earlier) are strongly recommended. The descent gully collects rockfall throughout the day; descending in darkness significantly increases objective hazard.

timing.recommendedStart: 5:00 a.m. or earlier to ensure full daylight for descent

road.name: Washington State Route 20 (North Cascades Highway)
road.status: Open year-round; winter closures possible east of Washington Pass
road.seasonalGate: Washington Pass closed winter months (typically November-April based on avalanche control); check WSDOT avalanche page before traveling in shoulder season
road.driveNote: From Newhalem: 42.1 miles east on SR-20. From Winthrop: 31.3 miles west on SR-20 to mile marker 161. Blue Lake Trailhead is 1 mile west of Washington Pass Overlook. Parking is very limited and fills quickly on weekends; arrive by 6:00 a.m. or earlier during summer.

access.landManager: Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest / North Cascades National Park Service Complex
access.passRequired: Northwest Forest Pass required ($5/day or $30/annual). Day-use parking validation available at ranger stations.
access.closures: Approach gully may be impassable until late June due to snow. Washington Pass prone to avalanche control closures (WSDOT) in spring. Check conditions before travel.

climate.forecastZone: Northern Cascades (Skagit County)
climate.typical: High alpine granite peak with rapid weather changes common. Maritime influence creates wet, heavy snow and high avalanche danger.
climate.spring: Snow and ice in approach gully; significant rockfall risk as snow melts; unpredictable weather
climate.summer: Best climbing season; stable conditions mid-July through August. Daytime temperatures typically 50-65°F at summit. Afternoon thunderstorms possible. Mosquitoes present all day.
climate.fall: September provides good conditions before snow returns; October and later not recommended due to snow and avalanche risk
climate.winter: Not recommended; severe snow, ice, and avalanche hazard; extreme cold; route not climbed November-May

emergency.sheriffDispatch: 911 (no cell service at location; satellite communicator or hiking down to coverage area required)
emergency.rangerStation: Marblemount Ranger Station (Okanogan-Wenatchee NF), Concrete area. Skagit County Search and Rescue coordinates emergency response.
emergency.nearestHospital: Skagit Valley Hospital, Mount Vernon, WA (approximately 1 hour from trailhead); Cascade Valley Hospital, Arlington, WA (alternative)
emergency.notes: Cell service is unreliable throughout the Washington Pass area. Satellite communicators (SPOT, Garmin InReach) strongly recommended. Washington Pass area has significant avalanche terrain and high rockfall hazard; rescue access via gully is complicated. Self-rescue or partner-assisted rescue may be necessary. Register climbs with park ranger if possible.

waypoints.name: Liberty Bell-Concord Tower Notch (base of climb)

whatToBring: Insulating layers and rain jacket (weather changes rapidly)
Approach shoes or light hiking boots suitable for scrambling
routes.corrections: Given rockGrade listed as 5.9; authoritative sources (Mountain Project, Steph Abegg, The Mountaineers) consistently document the North Face as 5.7, not 5.9. There is a separate West Face route rated 5.8/5.9, but the North Face Route is definitively 5.7. Given elevation of 7,611 ft matches some sources; others report 7,560 ft. Both are within acceptable range of survey variability.

blurb: Concord Tower is the sharp summit of excellent granite immediately south of Liberty Bell Mountain in the Liberty Bell Group above Washington Pass in the North Cascades. The peak, at 7,600+ feet, was first ascended by Fred Beckey and John Parrott on June 12, 1956, via the North Face route. The granite, carved from the Golden Horn batholith, offers multiple alpine rock climbing routes ranging from 5.6 to 5.11, with the North Face being the most popular moderate alpine objective in the Washington Pass complex.

routes.fa: 1904 by Frank Calkins and George Otis Smith
routes.overview: The primary route to Goat Mountain's West Peak follows an established trail to approximately 5,400 feet, then continues via off-trail scrambling across permanent snowfields and exposed Class 2-3 terrain to reach the summit ridge.
routes.beta: The West Peak (6,721 ft) is the more commonly accessed summit, featuring good trail access to within 50 vertical feet of the summit. The route involves a steep permanent snowfield traverse requiring an ice axe at all times, then a short, relatively straightforward scramble to the top. The route offers commanding views of Mount Baker, Mount Shuksan, Mount Sefrit, and the surrounding North Cascades peaks.
routes.approach: From Bellingham, take SR 542 (Mount Baker Highway) northeast for approximately 46 miles. Turn left onto Forest Road 32 (Hannegan Pass Road) just before the Glacier Public Service Center. Drive 2.1-2.5 miles on FS 32 to the Goat Mountain Trailhead parking area at 2,500 feet elevation. The trailhead features two parking areas and a vault toilet. The Goat Mountain Trail (#673) begins with approximately a dozen switchbacks in the first mile, then levels off and heads west along an improbable sidehill ridge. The maintained trail continues with additional switchbacks and scenic forest sections, crossing into the Mount Baker Wilderness at approximately 2 miles. The trail reaches its maintained terminus at approximately 5 miles and 5,400 feet elevation.
routes.descent: Descend via the same route. Exercise extreme care on the permanent snowfield, especially in descent when footing is more precarious. The steep snow slope on which a fall could be very nasty requires careful, controlled movement with ice axe self-arrest capability. Once below the snowfield, follow boot tracks and trail markers back down the ridgeline and through the switchbacks to the trailhead.
routes.descentText: Retrace the ascent route. From the summit, descend the short exposed scramble back to the snowfield (requiring crampons or microspikes). Carefully descend the steep snowfield using self-arrest technique; do not hurry this section. Once clear of the snowfield at approximately 5,500-5,700 feet, follow the established trail back down through the alpine meadows and forest. The main trail features good switchbacks that ease the descent. Total descent typically takes 2-3 hours depending on conditions and party fitness.
routes.itinerary: Day 1: Start early from trailhead (6:00-7:00 AM recommended). Hike the established Goat Mountain Trail for approximately 2.5-3 hours, reaching the 5,400-5,700 foot plateau with views into the basin beneath East Peak. Continue off-trail for 30-60 minutes scrambling across heather and talus. Traverse the permanent snowfield with ice axe and crampons/microspikes for 30-45 minutes to reach the summit ridge. Complete the final Class 2-3 scramble to the West Peak summit (45-90 minutes). Total summit time: 6-8 hours from trailhead. Descend via the same route, reaching trailhead in darkness if late departure.
routes.bail: The primary bail point is at the maintained trail terminus (5,400 feet). If snow conditions appear dangerous, ice axe technique is inadequate, or weather deteriorates significantly, do not attempt the snowfield traverse; descend immediately to the maintained trail and return to trailhead. No technical climbing expertise is required to reverse course at any point.
routes.turnaround: An early alpine start is essential; any party not reaching the summit ridge by early afternoon should turn around and descend to avoid travel in darkness on steep, exposed terrain. Never traverse the permanent snowfield after 3:00 PM given the additional darkness risk.
routes.bestSeason: July through September offers the most reliable climbing conditions. June can have significant snowpack higher on the route. The south-facing trail is one of the first in the Mount Baker Scenic Byway area to melt, making it a good early-season destination for alpine hiking. July features peak wildflower displays in the alpine meadows. August and early September offer stable snow conditions on the higher snowfields. By mid-September, snow cover decreases but corniced ridges may still present hazards.
routes.comms: Cellular service is unreliable to non-existent in the Mount Baker Wilderness. Satellite messengers (Garmin inReach or similar) are strongly recommended for wilderness areas. The trailhead is approximately 46 miles from Bellingham.
routes.face: West Peak - South and Southwest faces

timing.recommendedStart: 6:00-7:00 AM for timely summit and descent

routes.detailedRack: Ice axe (essential year-round, 50-65 cm recommended), crampons or microspikes for steep snow traversal, helmet for protection during scramble and on exposure.
routes.proNeeds: Ice axe placement on steep snow for self-arrest; microspikes or crampons for secure footing on hard snowfield. No technical protection required but route-finding ability essential for off-trail terrain above maintained trail.
whatToBring: Sturdy hiking boots with good ankle support
Base layers (wool or synthetic, avoid cotton)
Insulating mid-layer fleece or puffy jacket
Warm hat and gloves (even in summer)
Trail snacks (granola bars, nuts, dried fruit, gels)
Water bottles or hydration pack (3+ liters capacity)
proTips: Start before dawn to maximize daylight hours; this allows safe descent before darkness
The permanent snowfield varies in angle and conditions; assess carefully before committing
Waterproof your map and headlamp; this area experiences significant weather changes
The forest section has good switchbacks but can be muddy in late spring; waterproof boots are essential
In summer conditions (July-August), much of the high-elevation snow melts quickly, improving access but requiring constant situational awareness for changing conditions
Always carry an ice axe even if snow appears minimal at lower elevations; upper snowfields are permanent
The scenic overlook at the end of maintained trail provides an excellent vantage point to assess snowfield and ridge conditions before committing
watchOut: The permanent snowfield on the final approach has claimed lives; a fall here is potentially fatal without self-arrest capability
Cornices form on the summit ridge, especially after winter/spring storms; stay well back from edges
Black bears are active in the Mount Baker Wilderness; secure all food and scented items
Weather can change rapidly; storms can develop within 1-2 hours
The maintained trail ends abruptly; route-finding ability required for off-trail navigation above 5,400 feet
Loose talus above the maintained trail is unstable and slippery when wet
Crampons or microspikes are essential; many accidents occur from slipping on hard snow without traction
The area sits at the intersection of marine and alpine climates; highly variable conditions
knownHazards: Permanent steep snowfield requiring ice axe self-arrest capability; falls are typically fatal
Corniced ridgelines along summit approach, especially after winter precipitation
Weak snow bridges on approach to East Peak basin (if detouring to East Peak)
Loose, unstable talus and scree fields above maintained trail
Black bear activity; use bear canisters or bear bags for food storage
Rapid weather deterioration; thunderstorms possible July-September
Avalanche terrain, though typically stable outside spring season
Exposure on final scramble; fall could be serious or fatal on Class 3 sections

pitchDetail.notes: Established trail through old-growth forest with switchbacks. ~5 miles roundtrip to maintained trail terminus (5,400 feet). Forest section may be muddy in late spring.
Off-trail scrambling through heather, talus, and alpine meadow. Route-finding required. Climbing directly toward snowfield below West Peak. Loose talus requires careful footing.
Steep permanent snowfield traverse (~400-500 vertical feet) requiring ice axe and crampons/microspikes. This is the crux pitch; fall here is potentially fatal. Angle ~40-45 degrees.
Summit scramble on exposed Class 2-3 terrain with some exposure. Final 100-150 vertical feet to West Peak summit. No technical rock climbing but scrambling is exposed with 500+ foot drops nearby.

road.name: Mount Baker Highway (SR 542) to Hannegan Pass Road (FS 32)
road.status: Open year-round; FS 32 may close seasonally after heavy snow
road.seasonalGate: Hannegan Pass Road (FS 32) may close due to snow November-April depending on conditions
road.driveNote: Excellent paved highway to town of Glacier; continue on SR 542 to Mount Baker area. Turn onto FS 32 (Hannegan Pass Road) for final 2.1 miles to trailhead. The road is narrow and winding but well-maintained. High-clearance vehicles recommended after snowmelt due to potential washboard/ruts.

climate.typical: Marine west coast climate with high winter precipitation and cool summers; weather fronts originating in the Pacific Ocean encounter orographic lift from the Cascades
climate.spring: Highly variable; melting snowpack creates water hazards and wet trail conditions; temperatures 30-45F at trailhead, 10-20F at elevation; frequent precipitation
climate.summer: Warm, dry days (60-70F) with cool nights (40-50F); early mornings often cold but afternoon sun is strong; UV exposure high at elevation; occasional thunderstorms
climate.fall: Cooling trend with increasing precipitation; snow possible above 6,000 feet by late September; temperatures 40-60F at trailhead, 20-30F at elevation
climate.winter: Heavy snow, cold temperatures (0-30F), and potential avalanche terrain; route becomes mountaineering objective requiring additional skills
climate.forecastZone: Northwest Avalanche Center (NWAC) - Mount Baker forecast zone

access.landManager: Mount Baker-Snoqualmie National Forest (Mount Baker Ranger District)
access.passRequired: Northwest Forest Pass (National Forest Recreation Pass): $5 for day use or $30 annual pass. Washington State Discover Pass is NOT valid at Forest Service sites.

emergency.rangerStation: Glacier Public Service Center: 360-599-2714 (ranger station closest to trailhead); Mount Baker Ranger District: 360-856-5700
emergency.nearestHospital: PeaceHealth St. Joseph Medical Center (Level II Trauma Center), Bellingham, WA; approximately 50 miles from trailhead (1+ hour drive)
emergency.notes: Whatcom County Search & Rescue operates 24/7. Bellingham Mountain Rescue Council responds to alpine emergencies in the Mount Baker area (40-60 calls/year). Cell service is unreliable in wilderness; satellite messengers strongly recommended.

routes.corrections: Wikipedia and some sources list main peak elevation as 6,844 feet, but research indicates the East Peak is 6,891 feet and West Peak is 6,721 feet. The given peak elevation (6,892 ft) essentially matches the East Peak (6,891 ft) within survey margins, suggesting reference to the East Peak while this route targets the West Peak. First ascent date (1904) applies to the peak as a whole; specific route first ascent details not available in accessible sources.

blurb: Goat Mountain is a 6,844-foot double-summited massif in the Skagit Range of the North Cascades, located 3 miles south of the Canadian border in the Mount Baker Wilderness. The peak features commanding views of Mount Baker, Shuksan, and surrounding icy summits; its North Cascades character includes remnants of Swamp Creek Glacier on the northern slope between the East (6,891 ft) and West (6,721 ft) peaks. First ascended in 1904 by Frank Calkins and George Otis Smith, it remains a popular alpine scramble destination.", "hierarchyNote": null, "sources": ["https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Goat_Mountain_(Whatcom_County)", "https://www.summitpost.org/goat-mountain/152742", "https://www.wta.org/go-hiking/hikes/goat-mountain-mount-baker", "https://www.fs.usda.gov/r06/mbs/recreation/trails/goat-mountain-trail-673", "https://www.peakbagger.com/peak.aspx?pid=24754", "https://onehikeaweek.com/2025/05/25/goat-mountain-east-peak/", "https://www.wcsar.org/", "https://nwac.us/avalanche-forecast/", "https://www.usgs.gov/staff-profiles/george-otis-smith"]

routes.overview: The standard approach to Mount Spickard summit via Southwest Glacier and south ridge, combining moderate glacier travel with Class 2-3 rock scrambling.
routes.beta: From Ouzel Lake basecamp at 5,640 ft, ascend northeast up broad talus gully toward 7,360 ft pass between Ouzel and Silver Lakes. Veer right onto talus to reach Southwest Glacier; cross glacier carefully avoiding crevasses. Reach 8,000 ft col on south ridge, descend 40 feet, and traverse rightward across open slopes to obvious snow and rock gully. Final approach follows south ridge on right-side until final 100 feet, then cross left for summit. Snow slopes steepen to 35-40 degrees near top.
routes.approach: Primary from Depot Creek (Canada): Drive Chilliwack River Road 31.7 miles from Chilliwack to Depot Creek Road. Park vehicle at 2.5-3 miles (high-clearance recommended) and hike/scramble 8 miles through flagged forest paths with stream crossing on wet rock (fixed hand-lines assist). Total approach time 5-6 hours, elevation gain 3,500-4,000 ft to Ouzel Lake base camp (5,700 ft) with sandy campsites. Alternative: Water taxi from Ross Lake Resort via Silver Creek (approximately 2 hours, 25 miles, contact (206) 486-3751 for reservations, $265 flat rate for up to 6 passengers).
routes.descent: Retrace ascent route back to Ouzel Lake base camp.
routes.descentText: Head north from summit down south ridge on right-side, traverse leftward across rock and snow gullies to open southeast slopes. Drop to 8,000 ft col and carefully cross Southwest Glacier avoiding crevasses. Ascend back to 7,360 ft pass and descend broad talus gully to Ouzel Lake. Watch for loose talus and wet rock hazards. Total descent 4-5 hours to basecamp. Early start recommended to avoid afternoon melt.
routes.itinerary: Day 1: Drive to Depot Creek trailhead, hike to Ouzel Lake base camp (5-6 hours). Day 2: From Ouzel Lake, ascend to summit via Southwest Glacier (4-5 hours up), summit rest, descend to base camp (4-5 hours down). Total 11 hours car-to-car possible but two-day minimum recommended; three days preferred.
routes.bestSeason: Mid-to-late summer (July-August) offers best conditions. Early season (June) presents more snow coverage which can complicate route-finding but may be less icy. Late season (August-September) features more obvious crevasses and harder glaciers but less snow hazard. Peak climbing window is July through August.
routes.fa: 1904 by Walter B. Reaburn (peak first ascent, not route-specific)
routes.proNeeds: Standard alpine glacier protection including ice axe, crampons, crevasse rescue kit (pickets, webbing, carabiners), helmet for rockfall. Rope recommended for crevasse rescue despite relatively easy terrain.
routes.detailedRack: Ice axe, crampons, helmet, 30-meter glacier rope, 2-3 ice screws or pickets, carabiners and webbing for anchor/rescue, personal anchor, 10 essentials. Small rock rack not typically needed on standard route (Class 3 maximum).
watchOut: Crevasses on Southwest Glacier, especially late season when snow bridges weaken
Loose talus and rockfall on south ridge and gullies
Wet rock on stream crossings during approach
Avalanche terrain on snow slopes
Exposure on final summit ridge approach
knownHazards: Crevasses and hidden snow bridges on glacier
Loose, sharp talus throughout route
Steep snow slopes to 40 degrees with avalanche potential
Exposed rock scrambling with minimal protection
Weather changes rapidly in North Cascades
proTips: Start approach early (2-3am) from Ouzel Lake to avoid afternoon rockfall and poor snow conditions
Rope up for glacier crossing despite Class 2 rating for safety
Mark route on descent as gullies can be confusing in poor visibility
Cache water at high point on descent to Ouzel Lake; stream flows intermittently
Late spring/early June typically has easier glacier travel before extensive crevassing

timing.recommendedStart: 2-3 AM from Ouzel Lake basecamp for mid-morning summit

routes.bail: From upper reaches, descent via same route is only practical option. From Ouzel Lake, could bail via incoming route. From glacier, must descend; no safe traverse options.
routes.turnaround: Set firm turnaround time by 2 PM to ensure daylight for glissading and rockfall avoidance on descent. Afternoon conditions (warming, rockfall, poor visibility) increase objective hazards significantly.
routes.comms: No cellular coverage in remote Chilliwack area. Satellite messenger device (inReach, Spot) recommended. Backcountry permit office in Marblemount (360) 854-7245.
objHaz: Rockfall from loose talus and gullies
Wet rock and stream hazards on approach

access.landManager: North Cascades National Park (most area) and Ross Lake National Recreation Area
access.permit: Backcountry camping permit required year-round
access.fees: 10 USD per person plus 6 USD nonrefundable permit fee (May-Oct); free Oct-May

road.name: Chilliwack River Road / Depot Creek Road
road.status: Seasonal access via Canada; Depot Creek Road rough but passable to 2-3 miles
road.seasonalGate: Typically open Jun-Sep; verify before trip
road.driveNote: 31.7 miles on Chilliwack River Road from town of Chilliwack BC. High-clearance vehicle recommended for Depot Creek Road. Park at 2.5-3 miles and hike remainder. From US: cross border near Sumas WA, follow directions to Chilliwack Lake.

climate.typical: Alpine tundra climate with frequent wind and weather changes. Average high 45-50F at summit, lows 30-35F.
climate.spring: Heavy snow, avalanche danger high, approach often blocked by snowpack
climate.summer: Most stable season. Frequent afternoon thunderstorms (40% chance). Temperatures 40-55F during day.
climate.fall: Rapid deterioration; first snow possible late August. Weather window closes quickly.
climate.winter: Not recommended. Extreme avalanche danger, crevasses hard to identify, temperatures -10F to 10F.
climate.forecastZone: Cascade Range North forecast zone (NOAA), Chilliwack Mountains area

emergency.rangerStation: North Cascades Wilderness Information Center, 7280 Ranger Station Rd, Marblemount WA 98267, (360) 854-7245
emergency.nearestHospital: Cascade Valley Hospital or PeaceHealth St. Joseph Medical Center (60-90 minutes drive from trailhead)
emergency.notes: Whatcom County Search and Rescue operates 24/7. Remote location means rescue response may take 12-24 hours. Helicopter rescue possible but weather-dependent.

blurb: Mount Spickard is an 8,980-foot alpine peak in the North Cascades Chilliwack Group, located 2 miles south of the Canada-US border in Whatcom County. Composed of gneiss and featuring three perennial glaciers, it ranks as the 5th highest peak in North Cascades National Park and 9th most prominent in Washington State. The peak was first ascended in 1904 by Walter B. Reaburn and was renamed in 1963 to honor mountaineer Warren Spickard.

knownHazards: The mountain contains very loose, unstable rock - helmets are essential
Rockfall is the primary hazard, especially with parties larger than 6
Early season snow and ice require crampons and ice axes
Ridge sections have significant exposure with fall potential
watchOut: Keep party small (6 or fewer) to minimize rockfall hazard
Wear helmets at all times - this is non-negotiable
Be extra cautious on descent - loose rock is easily kicked down
Check avalanche forecast with NWAC before climbing, especially June-July
This route is known for being chossy (loose and crumbly rock)
routes.overview: The standard alpine scramble on Mount Larrabee features a scenic alpine trail approach to High Pass, then loose rock gully scrambling and ridge travel to the summit. Class 3-4 terrain with significant rockfall hazard and excellent views of Mount Baker, Mount Shuksan, and the North Cascades.
routes.beta: From High Pass, descend into the basin below the peak. Traverse west and ascend boulder fields and scree to the southwest spur at approximately 6,900 feet. Locate the gully system in the rocky face with two narrow channels 30 feet apart; take the right channel and ascend the narrow then open gully to broken rock to attain the ridge top. Follow the ridge, favoring the west side, to the summit. Most of ridge top has a way trail and the route is partially marked with cairns after leaving the basin, but cairns may be far apart and the way trail may disappear for long stretches. If you lose cairns or way trail most gullies probably work though some may be more difficult than others.
routes.approach: From the Twin Lakes trailhead parking area at the end of Forest Road 3065, hike north on the alpine trail to Low Pass (approximately 1 mile, 600 feet elevation gain). Continue to High Pass (approximately 0.5 miles further, 400 feet elevation gain). From High Pass at about 6,400 feet, the trail becomes less distinct. Traverse left and down into the basin below Mount Larrabee (lose 100-200 feet elevation), traversing through light forest and alpine meadow. This section is marked by trampled vegetation showing established foot paths but no formal trail.
routes.descent: Descend the same route via the south ridge and gully system. Going down takes almost the same time as going up as climbers should be extra careful with foot placement and avoid kicking rocks down to other parties. From the southwest spur, descend the gully system (preferably the same channel taken on ascent) to broken rock, then scree and boulder fields back to the basin.
routes.descentText: From the summit, retrace your route down the south ridge, favoring the west side. Descend carefully back to the ridge top and down the gully that was ascended (the right of the two channels). The descent requires careful foot placement due to loose rock; many climbers report spending nearly as much time descending as ascending due to the hazardous loose terrain. Avoid knocking rocks onto parties below. From the bottom of the gully system, descend the boulder fields and scree back to the southwest spur, then traverse east and upslope back into the basin. Follow your ascent route or the trampled vegetation paths back to High Pass, then return via the alpine trail to Low Pass and Twin Lakes trailhead.
routes.itinerary: Day 1: Depart Twin Lakes trailhead early morning. Hike to High Pass (approximately 1.5-2 hours). Begin scrambling from High Pass. Reach summit in 2-2.5 hours. Spend 30-60 minutes at summit enjoying views and eating. Descend via the same route (2-2.5 hours) and return to trailhead by mid-afternoon for a 6-8 hour total day.
routes.bail: From the gully system, downclimbing is the safest option. If weather deteriorates significantly on the ridge, carefully descend the same route. If stuck in the basin during weather, camp in a protected location and wait for conditions to improve rather than attempting the scramble in poor visibility. The route has good escape options at Low Pass or High Pass if needed.
routes.turnaround: Turn around well before dark to ensure safe descent. If summit is not reached by early afternoon, consider turning around to allow sufficient daylight for safe descent. The approach trail can be difficult to locate in poor visibility or darkness.
routes.bestSeason: The climbing season extends from June through September, with July, August, and September offering the most favorable conditions. June is viable but may have lingering snow in the gully system and on the ridge, requiring more winter mountaineering skills. Early season (June-early July) features wet, heavy snow and high avalanche danger. Mid to late summer (July-September) provides the most stable conditions with minimal snow and generally good weather. October can offer excellent fall climbing with clear skies but increasing cold and potential early season storms.
routes.comms: Cell phone reception is unreliable in the area and should not be counted on for emergencies. Satellite communicators (like Garmin InReach or SPOT) are strongly recommended for parties venturing into this remote alpine terrain.
routes.detailedRack: Helmets (mandatory - one per person), ice axe (if climbing early season June-July), trekking poles (highly recommended for approach and descent), microspikes or crampons (early season), day pack, water bottle or hydration system, food and energy snacks, first aid kit.
routes.proNeeds: Helmets are the primary protective equipment for this scramble route due to rockfall hazard. The terrain does not require technical rock protection, but early season ascents may require understanding of basic snow travel and self-arrest technique with an ice axe. The exposure on the ridge requires comfort with scrambling terrain and ability to navigate exposed terrain.
whatToBring: Water bottles or hydration pack (2-3 liters minimum)
Map and compass or GPS device
proTips: Start very early from the trailhead to maximize daylight on the summit and descent
The Northwest Forest Pass is required and can be purchased at recreation.gov or local ranger stations
Cairns marking the route on the upper mountain can be few and far between - bring map and compass
If snow is present on the gully in early season, consider using traction on descent even if not needed on ascent
The rock quality is notoriously poor - move deliberately and avoid unnecessary exposure
Groups of 4-6 climbers are optimal; larger groups significantly increase rockfall risk to each other
Climbers who have forgotten helmets have reported feeling lucky for that reason after rockfall incidents
This peak offers excellent views and is worth the effort despite the loose rock

road.name: Forest Road 3065 (Twin Lakes Road)
road.status: Passable, variable conditions
road.seasonalGate: Road not plowed in winter; typically passable May through October depending on snow conditions
road.driveNote: High clearance vehicle recommended; 4WD helpful. Road is muddy after rainfall. First section can be smooth but deteriorates. The final 2 miles have tight switchbacks, rocky sections, and deep dips. Last section very rough. Park at Twin Lakes trailhead or Yellow Aster Butte trailhead if road is impassable.

climate.forecastZone: Northwest Avalanche Center (NWAC) North Cascades forecast zone
climate.typical: Marine west coast climate zone. Weather fronts from Pacific Ocean bring precipitation when forced upward by Cascade peaks. High precipitation year-round, especially in winter as snowfall.
climate.spring: Late spring (May-early June) features wet, heavy maritime snow. Avalanche danger is significant. Days lengthen but weather is unstable and storms are common.
climate.summer: July-September: High pressure systems from Pacific create generally clear skies. Little to no cloud cover common. Temperatures mild (typically 40-70°F at summit). Wind can be significant.
climate.fall: September-October: Clear, stable weather common. Temperatures dropping (30-50°F at summit). Early storms possible. Excellent visibility for views.
climate.winter: November-April: Heavy snowfall, wet and heavy maritime snow creating high avalanche danger. Temperatures rarely below 0°F due to maritime influence but wind chill significant. Road not maintained.

access.landManager: Mount Baker-Snoqualmie National Forest (Mount Baker Wilderness area)

access.permit: Northwest Forest Pass required for trailhead parking
