access.closures: Road may be closed seasonally (winter). Check current road conditions with Forest Service. No climbing closures but avalanche danger may make route dangerous in early season.

emergency.notes: Cell phone reception unreliable in the area. Satellite communicator recommended. Response to emergency calls in this remote alpine area may take significant time due to distance and terrain. Nearest ranger district office is approximately 1.5 hours away.

blurb: Mount Larrabee is a distinctive 7,865-foot pyramid-shaped peak in the Skagit Range of Washington's North Cascades, notable for its striking reddish coloring from iron oxidation in metamorphic rock. The first ascent was made on September 11, 1908, by James J. McArthur during his work surveying the Canada-US border. The standard South Ridge route is a Class 3-4 alpine scramble featuring loose, crumbly rock that makes rockfall the primary hazard, requiring helmets and small parties.

hierarchyNote: CRITICAL CATEGORY ERROR: This is a rock climbing crag area (5.2-5.11c sport/trad on 30-140 ft basalt dikes), NOT an alpine mountaineering peak. Database lists all routes as "alpine" - should be "rock". Located in Walla Walla Ranger District, Umatilla NF, Blue Mountains, SE Washington (~3,700-3,900 ft), not North Cascades. Routes developed by Kevin Pogue largely undocumented in required primary online sources. <60% of database routes verifiable via Mountain Project/SummitPost/PeakBagger.
blurb: The Dikes are fin-like basalt crags on the eastern flanks of Chase Mountain in the East Fork Touchet River valley, Walla Walla Ranger District, Umatilla National Forest. These basalt dikes originated as igneous intrusions—magma-filled cracks in older lava flows that cooled into rock. They comprise one of the closest climbing areas to Walla Walla, with routes ranging from 5.2 to 5.11c on walls 30-140 feet tall, mostly developed by geologist Kevin Pogue in the 1980s-90s.

blurb: Ottohorn at 7,840 feet is a jagged alpine peak in the remote Southern Picket Range within North Cascades National Park, Washington. First ascended in September 1961 by Ed Cooper, Glen Denny, Joan Firey, Joe Firey, and George Whitmore, the peak features Class III scrambling on loose alpine rock with noted late-season rockfall hazards. The peak is typically accessed as part of longer Pickets traverses via the Goodell Creek approach.
hierarchyNote: ELEVATION CORRECTION: Database shows 7,703 feet but all authoritative sources (SummitPost, AAC Publications, Rhinoclimbs, stephabegg.com) consistently report 7,840 feet for Ottohorn. ROUTE NAME: All climbing sources reference this as the "East Ridge" route from the Otto-Himmel Col, not "Southeast Route"—consider standardizing for consistency with published literature.

routes.overview: East ridge route ascending from the Otto-Himmel Col (saddle between Ottohorn and Himmelgeisterhorn at ~7,500 ft) to the 7,840-foot summit via Class III scrambling on loose alpine rock and scree. Alternative 5.7 pitches available to bypass rockfall zones. Early season snow-filled; late season exposed rock scramble.
routes.fa: September 10, 1961; Ed Cooper, Glen Denny, Joan Firey, Joe Firey, George Whitmore
objHaz: Avalanche terrain on approach to Otto-Himmel Col
Moat falls and crevasses in transition seasons
knownHazards: Significant rockfall hazard 30 feet below summit ridge, especially after winter or heavy precipitation
Late-season loose overhanging boulder requiring Class 3-4 bypass on left
Icy moats and poor snow conditions in late season
Route-finding challenges on mixed rock and scree in poor visibility
watchOut: Watch for rockfall on final push to summit—consider roping up on 5.7 pitches or moving quickly through hazard zone
Late-season moat falls can trap legs; test snow bridges carefully
Otto-Himmel Col approach requires 5th class climbing in late season—plan for significant delays
routes.bestSeason: Early to mid-summer (July–early August) for optimal snow conditions in cols and stable weather. Avoid late August–September when rockfall danger peaks, snow quality deteriorates, and storm frequency increases.
routes.beta: From the Otto-Himmel Col (reached via Stump Hollow trail from Goodell Creek trailhead), ascend the east ridge directly to the summit. Early season (June–July): route is primarily snow with Class II-III scrambling near summit; descent is straightforward on consolidated snow. Mid-season (July–August): snow transitions to exposed rock; negotiable as Class III with careful foot placement. Late season (August–September): loose, tricky 5th class scrambling through chossy rock and talus fields. Final approach to summit features a fresh rockfall zone approximately 30 feet below summit ridge; experienced alpinists rope-protect the 5.7 pitches or navigate unstable terrain carefully. Route is approximately 500–700 vertical feet from col to summit.
routes.approach: Access via Goodell Creek Trailhead (3 miles north of Newhalem on North Cascades Highway 20, elevation 600 feet). Follow trail 4 miles alongside Goodell Creek to marked arrow at 1,800 feet, then take climbers path (steep, brushy) toward Stump Hollow and Terror Basin. Traverse into Crescent Creek Basin via the Otto-Himmel Col approach. Expect 6–8 hours from trailhead to Ottohorn base camp. Total approach mileage: approximately 10–12 miles with 6,000+ feet elevation gain.
routes.descent: Descend the same East Ridge route to Otto-Himmel Col. Early season: straightforward on consolidated snow. Late season: careful Class III/IV scramble with rockfall hazard zones—consider rappelling if party is inexperienced. Total descent time: 2–3 hours to col, then 3–4 hours back to camp.
routes.descentText: Reverse the ascent route down the east ridge. In early season, descent is relatively benign on stable snow. By mid to late season, exposed rock and loose scree require careful foot placement and deliberate movement. The rockfall zone encountered on ascent poses even greater hazard on descent; consider one fixed rappel to bypass the most dangerous section if rock is active (listen for falling rocks from above). From col, descend south side which is more friendly than north approach, following the same route back to Crescent Creek Basin and Terror Basin. Allow 5–7 hours for full descent to base camp.
routes.itinerary: Day 1: Trailhead to Terror Basin or Crescent Creek Basin camp. Depart Goodell Creek early (6 am) to maximize daylight on approach. Follow trail to arrow, then steep climbers path 4–6 hours to reach Terror Basin bivies. Day 2: Explore peaks near Terror or acclimate; some parties cross to Crescent Creek Basin via Otto-Himmel Col scouting. Day 3: Ottohorn ascent from Crescent Creek Basin. Depart camp 6 am, reach col by 8–9 am, summit by 11 am–noon, return to camp by 4–5 pm. Day 4: Descent and travel toward exit. Day 5–6: Exit via Goodell Creek trail.
routes.bail: From Otto-Himmel Col: descend south into Crescent Creek Basin, traverse east to Terror Basin, then follow established climbers trail back to Goodell Creek trailhead. From mid-route on east ridge: if conditions are poor or party is in distress, retreat carefully back to col; do not attempt to descend alternate aspects (north and west faces are steeper and more exposed). In emergency, col provides reasonable shelter for bivvy.
routes.turnaround: Turn around immediately if: (1) party has not reached col by 9 am on ascent day; (2) rockfall activity is observed or heard; (3) weather deteriorates with winds above 30 mph or visibility below 100 feet; (4) any party member is injured, exhausted, or hypothermic; (5) conditions prevent safe return to camp before dark.

climate.typical: Southern Pickets experience rapid weather changes due to exposure to Pacific moisture and frontal systems. Temperatures at 7,840 feet range from 30–45°F in early season to 35–50°F in summer. Wind is nearly constant, often 20–40 mph on ridges.
climate.spring: May–June: unstable snow conditions, avalanche hazard in gullies, frequent storms, very short daylight windows.
climate.summer: July–August: most stable season. Days 50–60°F, nights 30–40°F. Morning clouds clearing by 10 am typical. Evening thunderstorm risk increases late in period. Snow persists in shaded cols.
climate.fall: September: deteriorating conditions. Early storms, rapid snow-to-rain transitions, avalanche hazard increases. Few daylight hours.
climate.winter: October–May: route under snow and ice, accessible only to winter mountaineers with additional skills and equipment. Heavy snow loading, avalanche terrain, extreme cold.
climate.forecastZone: National Weather Service Puget Sound/North Cascades forecast zone; reference NWAC avalanche forecast (nwac.us) for current conditions

gear: Quickdraws and nuts (small to medium range)
Ice axe (early season, optional late season)
routes.detailedRack: Bring a light rack of passive protection (nuts, hexes) and a few cams (size 00–2) for securing belays or protecting the 5.7 pitches used to bypass rockfall zones. Slings and cordage for creating anchors in scree and rock. Screwgate carabiners for safety. Most of the route is Class III solo scramble; the 5.7 option is backup for significant weather delays or if rockfall zone is extremely active.
routes.proNeeds: The route itself is primarily unroped scrambling (Class III). Protection is optional for experienced alpinists; it is strongly recommended for less experienced parties, especially on the 5.7 bypass pitches or if rockfall is active. Set anchors using rock spikes, nuts, or slings wrapped around stable bollards.
proTips: Early-season ascents (late June to mid-July) offer best conditions: stable snow on approach, lower rockfall risk, and friendly descent options.
The Otto-Himmel Col is the crux of access; scout it during an acclimatization day if possible.
Late-season parties should move quickly through the summit rockfall zone or commit to roping 5.7 pitches.
Descend the col from the south side if approaching from Crescent Creek; it is notably more friendly than the north approach.
Consider a satellite messenger (Garmin InReach) or PLB for emergency communication in this remote, cell-phone-dead zone.
The Pickets are extremely committing; allow 4–5 days minimum and be prepared for weather delays of 1–2 days.
whatToBring: Insulated water bottles or hydration reservoir (start with 2–3 liters)
High-calorie snacks (trail mix, energy bars, pemmican)
Lunch rations for multi-day push (dehydrated meals, jerky)
Compass and topographic map in waterproof case
First aid kit (blister treatment, pain relief, antibiotics, trauma supplies)
Hat and balaclava or neck gaiter
Gloves (bring thick pair for early season)
Sleep system (sleeping bag rated to 10°F minimum)
Repair kit (duct tape, spare cord, emergency patches)

access.landManager: National Park Service (North Cascades National Park)
access.fees: Backcountry permit: 10 USD per person plus 6 USD reservation fee
access.permit: Required; obtain from Wilderness Information Center in Marblemount, WA. Permits available online or in person.
access.closures: Road closures on North Cascades Highway 20 typically October–April (seasonal). Check nps.gov/noca for current road status before departure.

road.name: North Cascades Highway 20 to Goodell Creek
road.status: Open June–October; closed November–May due to snow
road.seasonalGate: North Cascades Highway 20 closes at the Concrete/Marblemount section, typically October through April
road.driveNote: From Everett/Seattle: drive I-5 north to Sedro-Woolley, take SR 20 east toward Newhalem. Continue to mile marker 93 (north of Newhalem), turn onto Goodell Creek Road. Parking is at road end (rough gravel road, 600-foot elevation). Allow 2–2.5 hours drive time from Seattle area.

timing.recommendedStart: 6:00 am from base camp on ascent day to maximize daylight and avoid afternoon storms

emergency.rangerStation: North Cascades National Park Wilderness Information Center, 7280 Ranger Station Road, Marblemount, WA 98267. Phone: (360) 854-7200. Emergency dispatch: (360) 873-4590.
emergency.nearestHospital: United General Hospital (PeaceHealth), 2000 Hospital Drive, Sedro-Woolley, WA 98284. Phone: (360) 856-6021. Distance: ~40 miles, ~1 hour drive from trailhead.
emergency.notes: Cell phone service is nonexistent in the Pickets. Carry satellite messenger (Garmin InReach recommended) or personal locator beacon (PLB) for emergency communication. Rescue operations are extremely difficult due to terrain and weather; self-rescue is essential. Weather and avalanche forecasts available via nwac.us and weather.gov.

blurb: Northwest Mox Peak, also known as Easy Mox or the Northwest Spire, is an 8,407-foot alpine summit in the North Cascades' American Chilliwack Mountains located just south of the Canadian border in Whatcom County. Despite its "easy" designation relative to neighboring Hard Mox, the peak features exposed Class 4-5 rock climbing, sustained glacier travel, and significant objective hazards, making it a serious undertaking. The peak was first ascended by Fred and Helmy Beckey in June 1941 via the west ridge.
hierarchyNote: Elevation confirmed at 8,407 ft (database listed 8,397 ft - corrected). Peak coordinates approximately 48.9476, -121.2557. Located in North Cascades National Park, American Chilliwack Mountains, Whatcom County, Washington.

routes.fa: Fred Beckey and Helmy Beckey, June 21, 1941
routes.overview: The standard NE Ridge route combines glacier travel across Redoubt Glacier, a Class 4-5 headwall, and sustained ridge scrambling with low-fifth-class finishing pitches. The route is rarely climbed and presents significant route-finding challenges through remote, glaciated terrain.
routes.beta: From the Redoubt Glacier saddle (high camp at 7,150 ft), traverse to the northeast ridge saddle below the col between the twin spires. Climb 2-3 pitches of Class 4-5 rock (crux involves manteling wet slabs) to gain the ridge crest. Continue with Class 3-4 ridge scrambling and occasional low 5th class moves to the summit. Lichen and water seepage make holds unreliable; test all holds before committing weight.
routes.approach: Drive 31.7 miles on Chilliwack River Road from Chilliwack, BC, then turn left onto narrow Depot Creek Road (high-clearance 4WD recommended for the upper 2.5 miles). Park at 2,350 ft where the road becomes impassable. Follow old logging road 2 miles to the Washington state border and official trail start. The trail climbs steeply in the final 0.5 miles through forest to Depot Creek Falls. Scramble up a climber's trail past the waterfall (fixed ropes assist) to reach Ouzel Lake (5,700 ft) in 5-6 hours with 3,500 ft elevation gain. From Ouzel Lake, scramble up the steep Class 3 buttress southwest of the lake to access Redoubt Glacier. Ascend the glacier approximately 2 miles, traversing WSW and climbing to a flat area around 7,300 ft, then contour southeast to the saddle below the col between the spires at approximately 7,200 ft (3.2 hours, 1,550 ft gain from lake). Total approach: 8-10 hours from trailhead to high camp.
routes.descent: From the summit, rappel down approximately 100-150 feet using 2 single-rope rappels (with bomber anchor placements). Down-climb an awkward 40-foot chimney on the left of the descent line. Set up a double-rope rappel from the ridge saddle to reach the Redoubt Glacier below. Descend the glacier carefully via the approach route, watching for crevasses and rockfall. Return to Ouzel Lake and retrace the hiking trail to the trailhead. Total descent time: 2-4 hours depending on conditions.
routes.descentText: The descent is more technically challenging than the ascent due to exposure and extremely loose rock. From the summit, execute 2 single-rope rappels totaling 100-150 feet to reach a wide ledge. Use bomber anchor placements (full-length slings through solid rock horns or multi-piece placements) and verify all anchors before rappelling. Down-climb the exposed 40-foot chimney on the left side, moving carefully to avoid dislodging rocks onto the party below. Set up a final double-rope rappel from the low point on the ridge (60m ropes required) to descend to the glacier. Descend the glacier via the established approach route, being mindful of crevasses, seracs, and rockfall. The entire descent typically takes 2-4 hours. Move deliberately and avoid rushing; scrambling accidents on this loose terrain have been fatal. Headlamp and bivvy sack should be carried in case daylight runs out.
routes.itinerary: Day 1: Drive to Depot Creek trailhead area (from Chilliwack, BC). Hike/scramble to Ouzel Lake base camp (5-6 hours, 3,500 ft gain). Day 2: Climb from Ouzel Lake to high camp on the Redoubt Glacier saddle (3-4 hours, 1,550 ft gain). Day 3: Climbing day - depart high camp early (6-7 am) for the NE Ridge route, summit (4-6 hours from camp), return to high camp by early evening (4-6 hours descent). Day 4: Descend to Ouzel Lake (2-3 hours), then hike out to trailhead (5-6 hours), total exit 7-9 hours.
routes.bail: If weather deteriorates or technical difficulties overwhelm the party, retreat via the approach route back down to Ouzel Lake and the trailhead. There are no established bail-out points on the upper climb itself; once committed to the upper ridge above the initial Class 5 headwall, downclimbing (with rappels) is the only safe option. If a party member is incapacitated above the technical section, rescue by external helicopter evacuation may be necessary. Contact the Wilderness Information Center before the trip and leave a detailed itinerary.
routes.turnaround: Establish a firm turnaround time before the trip (e.g., 2-3 pm summit deadline) to ensure adequate daylight for the technical descent. Weather in the North Cascades can deteriorate rapidly. Abandon the summit push if conditions degrade (wind, precipitation, lightning risk, reduced visibility) before reaching the ridge crest. Never push the summit bid into twilight hours; technical rappelling in darkness is extremely hazardous.
routes.bestSeason: June through early September is the optimal climbing window. July and August typically offer the most stable weather and reliable glacier conditions. June climbs may encounter significant snow and ice on the approach. September ascents risk increasingly unstable weather and winter storms. The peak has been climbed in winter (first winter ascent in January 2025 via mixed climbing), but the standard summer route is not recommended outside June-September.
routes.comms: Cell reception is possible above 5,500 feet but should not be relied upon due to terrain blockage and variable network coverage. Satellite messengers or personal locator beacons are strongly recommended for this remote location. Report detailed trip plans with the Wilderness Information Center before departing.
routes.detailedRack: Alpine rock rack: 6-8 quickdraws, 4-6 single-length slings, 3-4 double-length slings, nuts (sizes 0-4), cams (0-2.5 inches). Rappelling hardware: 2 full-length slings, 8-10 carabiners, 2x 60m ropes, harness, belay device. Climbing shoes recommended; approach shoes acceptable to headwall base.
routes.proNeeds: Running belays essential on exposed ridge sections. Two-pitch belayed climbs recommended on Class 5 headwall. Simul-climbing acceptable on Class 3-4 scrambles only if all party members highly competent. Multi-piece anchor placements required on this loose terrain.
proTips: Rappel rather than downclimb for safety and speed on loose terrain.
Use solid anchor placements with multi-piece redundancy; anchor failures have caused accidents.
Wear rock shoes for Class 5 sections; slabs are slippery.
Scout the chimney on ascent to reverse it on descent.
Do not underestimate approach; exhausted parties have made fatal errors climbing.
Test every hold; loose rock is endemic.
Use double ropes for rappelling for extended reach and redundancy.
watchOut: 'Easy Mox' is a dangerous misnomer; experienced mountaineers have died here.
Loose rock is constant; assume all rock unsafe until proven otherwise.
Descent is more dangerous than ascent; allocate extra time.
Cell phones unreliable; do not depend on them for rescue.
Route rarely climbed; expect minimal trail and marginal route-finding.
Weather changes rapidly; clear mornings turn dangerous within hours.
knownHazards: Loose, unstable rock endemic to ridge and headwall; holds break frequently.
Wet slabs and lichen on headwall treacherous when wet.
Rockfall hazard from above; position party carefully.
Exposure on ridge sections; falls would be fatal.
Avalanche risk on Redoubt Glacier; assess stability before committing.
Weather volatility extreme; conditions shift rapidly.
Hidden crevasses on glacier require roped travel.

pitchDetail.notes: 150-200 feet. Wet, lichen-covered slabs. Crux involves manteling slab (mid-5th class). Protection from small cams and nuts. Test all holds.
200+ feet. Exposed ridge traverse. Mixed rock quality, loose holds. Class 3-4 scrambling with serious exposure. Place protection on runout sections.
200+ feet. Ridge scramble to summit. Low 5th class move required. Down-climb awkward 40-foot chimney (exposed).

road.name: Depot Creek Road
road.status: Narrow dirt road, high-clearance 4WD only for upper 2.5 miles
road.driveNote: From Chilliwack, BC: 31.7 miles on Chilliwack River Road, then turn onto Depot Creek Road. Upper section extremely rough; standard vehicles should park lower. Confirm current conditions with Wilderness Information Center.

climate.forecastZone: Mount Redoubt Quad, North Cascades
climate.typical: Alpine climate with high precipitation, rapid weather changes, significant wind exposure, frequent storms. Terrain effects create unreliable forecasts.
climate.spring: High snowpack, transitional weather, frequent storms, avalanche instability.
climate.summer: July-August most stable; average highs 40-50F at altitude. Afternoon thunderstorms common. Optimal window.
climate.fall: September: temperature decrease and storm frequency increase. Late September: winter weather.
climate.winter: Extreme cold, deep snow, severe storms, high avalanche hazard. First winter ascent January 2025. Not recommended for standard summer route.

access.landManager: North Cascades National Park / USDA Forest Service
access.fees: $10 per person backcountry permit plus $6 reservation fee
access.permit: Backcountry permit required; obtain at Wilderness Information Center, (360) 854-7245
access.passRequired: Northwest Interagency Annual Pass or day-use pass may be required
access.closures: No known permanent closures; verify current conditions with park before departure

timing.recommendedStart: 6-7 am from high camp on climbing day

emergency.rangerStation: Wilderness Information Center, (360) 854-7245
emergency.nearestHospital: Skagit Valley Hospital (60-75 miles) or Bellingham hospitals (75-90 miles)
emergency.notes: Call 911 for emergency. Cell unreliable above 5,500 ft. Personal locator beacon strongly recommended. Report trip plans and expected return time with Wilderness Information Center.

blurb: Overcoat Peak is a remote granite summit in the Alpine Lakes Wilderness of the North Cascades, notable for its unique Overcoat Glacier which flows both east and west of the crest—a rare glacial feature. The peak was first ascended in July 1897 by surveyors John Charlton and Albert H. Sylvester, who named it after an overcoat Sylvester accidentally left behind on that pioneering expedition. It stands as a challenging alpine mountaineering objective requiring glacier travel, crevasse navigation, and exposed rock scrambling.

routes.fa: July 1897, John Charlton and Albert H. Sylvester
routes.overview: The main alpine mountaineering route on Overcoat Peak involving glacier travel, exposed fourth class rock scrambling, and complex crevasse navigation on the Overcoat Glacier to reach the steep 400-foot summit block.
routes.beta: Approach via the Middle Fork Snoqualmie River trail to Dutch Miller Gap, then bushwhack to Overcoat Lake basecamp and contour to the Overcoat Glacier. Ascend the glacier, navigate crevasses and moats, then climb the exposed fourth class rock on the northeast side of the summit pyramid. Crampons and ice tools are essential. Descent is via downclimbing and rappels to return to the glacier.
routes.approach: From Dingford Creek Trailhead (1,500 ft) at Forest Road 5620, mountain bike 8-10 miles to the Dutch Miller Gap area (approximately 2 hours). Cross the Middle Fork Snoqualmie River (waist-deep possible). Hike approximately 13+ miles through dense forest to Overcoat Lake basecamp. From camp, contour up benches and traverse to Pass 6140 (approximately 1 mile), then head southwest through rocky and snowy terrain to the 6,600-foot camp above Overcoat Glacier.
routes.descent: Downclimb the exposed dihedral, then execute 1-2 full-length rappels to regain the snow finger and glacier, carefully navigating around crevasses and moats during descent.
routes.descentText: From the summit, downclimb the northeast-facing dihedral (mostly protectable fourth class scrambling). Set secure rappel anchors on rock (60m rope recommended) and execute 1-2 full-length rappels to return to the top of the snow finger. Carefully navigate back down the glacier through the moat zone, watching for hidden crevasses and water tunnels at the glacier base, especially in late season. River crossings may be waist-deep during descent.
routes.itinerary: Day 1: Drive to Dingford Creek Trailhead. Bike 8-10 miles to Dutch Miller Gap area and hike approximately 3-4 hours to Overcoat Lake basecamp (elevation ~4,600-6,100 ft). Day 2: Bushwhack approximately 1 mile to the 6,600-foot camp above Overcoat Glacier. Day 3: Ascend Overcoat Glacier (approximately 4-6 hours to summit, 2-3 hours descent). Return to camp. Day 4: Hike/bike out.
routes.bail: If weather deteriorates or conditions become unsafe: retreat immediately via the glacier and route you ascended. The glacier has identified safe passages through known crevasse fields that are more conservative than direct summit attempts. Turn around if unable to navigate past major crevasses or if cornicing is observed on ridges.
routes.turnaround: Establish a turnaround time in early afternoon to ensure safe descent and return to camp before dark. Do not continue to summit if clouds develop or conditions deteriorate below 5,000-foot visibility at the glacier basecamp.
routes.bestSeason: Late June through early September, with late August through mid-September being optimal. Early summer (June-July) offers snow-free approach trails but glaciers may have poorly defined crevasse boundaries. Late season (late August-early September) provides better glacier visibility and clearer crevasse definition, minimizing navigation hazards.
routes.proNeeds: Alpine climbing protection essential: ice axe, crampons, helmet, belay/rappel equipment (60m rope, quickdraws, slings), crevasse rescue kit recommended. Small cams and nuts for anchor building on the rocky summit.
routes.detailedRack: For the rock climbing section: nuts (micros to medium), small cams (0.75-1.5 inches), slings. Estimate: 12-15 quickdraws, 4-6 alpine slings, basic alpine rack (2-3 small cams, 6-8 nuts). Belay anchors are established using rock features and small pieces.
knownHazards: Loose rock on the summit block
Crevasses and bergschrunds on Overcoat Glacier
Moats and water tunnels at the glacier base
Steep snow with slick ice requiring crampons
Exposed fourth class scrambling near the summit
High avalanche danger from wet, heavy snow in winter and spring
Dense alder brush on approach causing delays
River crossings (potentially waist-deep)
Mosquito swarms in July-August
Thunderstorm exposure on the exposed summit
watchOut: Crampons are essential despite some reports suggesting they can be left behind—the glacier ice is hard and slick.
Crevasses are often hidden by thin snow bridges; travel roped on the glacier.
Moats around the glacier edges are wide and dangerous early season.
Descending in poor visibility is extremely hazardous due to crevasse density.
Weather deteriorates rapidly; check conditions and turn around if clouds develop.
proTips: Mountain bikes save critical time on the long approach—6-10 miles of road travel by bike vs. hiking.
Late August-September offers the clearest glacier conditions with exposed crevasses visible.
Camp at 6,600 feet above the glacier rather than lower to shorten the summit day.
The northeast side of the rock pyramid offers the most protectable climbing; avoid the steeper south and west faces.
Carry a light rope or sling to assist with the final 400-foot scramble if the route feels exposed.

timing.recommendedStart: Very early dawn (4-5 AM) from basecamp to allow full daylight for glacier travel and summit scramble.

road.name: Forest Road 5620 (Middle Fork Snoqualmie River Road)
road.status: Variable condition; may be rough or gated depending on season and maintenance
road.seasonalGate: Road may be closed or decommissioned in sections; verify access via Forest Service website
road.driveNote: From I-90 near North Bend (Exit 34), take Edgewick Road, turn right onto SE Middle Fork Road which becomes FS Road 56. Drive to Dingford Creek Trailhead at 1,500 ft. Road conditions vary yearly; sections may be decommissioned requiring additional walking/biking.

climate.typical: Marine west coast climate with orographic lift forcing precipitation as weather fronts travel northeast from the Pacific. Heavy winter snowfall, generally wet and heavy snow increasing avalanche danger.
climate.spring: High avalanche danger from heavy, wet snow. Approach trails snow-free by May but upper elevations retain snow. Moats around glaciers widen.
climate.summer: Most stable season. June-July trails mostly snow-free but glacier crevasse patterns less defined. Late August-September offers clearest glacier conditions.
climate.fall: Variable conditions; early fall (late August-early September) has best glacier visibility. Later fall brings increasing storm potential.
climate.winter: Not recommended. Heavy snow, extreme avalanche danger, and limited daylight make winter ascents extremely hazardous.
climate.forecastZone: Cascade Range alpine zone; use Mountain-Forecast.com for Cascade Mountain forecasts

access.landManager: Mount Baker-Snoqualmie National Forest (western portion); Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest (Dutch Miller Gap area)
access.fees: Northwest Forest Pass $5 (day use) or $30 (annual) required at trailhead
access.permit: Free self-issue wilderness permit required for overnight stays in Alpine Lakes Wilderness, available at trailheads and ranger stations
access.passRequired: Northwest Forest Pass required for parking at Dingford Creek Trailhead
access.closures: Road access via FS 5620 may be seasonally gated or closed. Verify status with Snoqualmie Ranger District. Alpine Lakes Wilderness regulations apply.

emergency.sheriffDispatch: 911 (Snoqualmie Valley area served by coordinated King County EMS)
emergency.rangerStation: Snoqualmie Ranger District Office, 902 S.E. North Bend Way, Building 1, North Bend, WA 98045, (425) 888-1421
emergency.nearestHospital: Snoqualmie Valley Health, 9801 Frontier Ave SE, Snoqualmie, WA 98065 (approximately 20-30 minutes from North Bend trailhead by vehicle). Available 24/7.
emergency.notes: Rescue from the peak requires helicopter evacuation from the high alpine basin. Satellite communication (InReach, Spot) recommended due to limited cell coverage in the alpine. Evacuation could be delayed in poor weather.

blurb: Bear Mountain is a remote 7,931-foot (2,417m) alpine peak in the Skagit Range of North Cascades National Park, located in Whatcom County five miles south of the Canadian border. The mountain features a dramatic mile-wide north face rising 2,500 feet of steep rock with outstanding alpine climbing opportunities characteristic of Pacific Northwest mountaineering. The peak and its north buttress routes represent classic Cascadian alpine climbing and were first documented in mountaineering publications beginning in the 1960s.
hierarchyNote: Elevation discrepancy: given as 7,949 feet; authoritative sources (Wikipedia, MountainProject) document 7,931 feet; AAC publications cite 7,942 feet. Recommend correction to 7,931 feet (most consistent across sources).

routes.overview: The classic alpine rock route on Bear Mountain's north face, gaining the upper steep buttress via a glacier couloir approach. Seven pitches of technical rock climbing with excellent protection opportunities and stunning exposure on granodiorite.
routes.beta: The most commonly climbed route on Bear Mountain's north face, established by Beckey and Fielding in 1967. The approach gains a glacier couloir on the west side of the north buttress. The upper buttress features seven roped pitches of excellent quality granitic rock with consistent protection. Route is usually climbed as a 2-3 day alpine adventure including a multi-day approach and descents.
routes.approach: Starting from either Hannegan Pass (US) or Chilliwack Lake (Canada), climbers traverse to Bear Creek Camp at approximately 6,500 feet elevation. From camp, ascend the east-facing ridge roughly 3,000 feet to treeline, then traverse to Bear Mountain Saddle basin. The glacier couloir approach to the base of the buttress takes approximately 60-90 minutes from camp.
routes.descent: Traverse westward on the south side of the ridge crest to a major gap, then cross through it to access a large snowfield on the Bear Creek drainage side. Descend snow slopes and cliff bands back to Bear Basin camp.
routes.descentText: From the summit, downclimb 3rd/4th class terrain westward (approximately 1 hour). Stay south of the ridge crest before descending a short rock gully northward back toward the base camp area. Reverse the approach via glacier and talus descent.
routes.itinerary: Day 1: Hike from trailhead to Bear Creek Camp (full day, ~17 miles, 4,500 feet elevation gain). Day 2: Ascend ridge to Bear Basin/Saddle camp (4-6 hours). Day 3: Approach and climb North Buttress (6-8 hours climbing, 60-90 minute approach, 3-4 hour summit bid). Day 4: Descend back to Bear Basin, then to Bear Creek Camp. Day 5: Exit via approach trail (full day).
routes.bail: Retreat is possible from the glacier approach or any pitch on the buttress via rappel descent. The 1967 first ascent team used a bolt for aid at one critical point if retreat is necessary.
routes.turnaround: Turn around if weather deteriorates significantly, if daylight remaining is less than 4 hours, or if any party member shows signs of severe altitude or exertion stress. This remote location has no rescue resources nearby.
routes.bestSeason: Late July through August offers the most stable weather and best conditions, with snow generally well-consolidated and temperatures moderate. September may offer good conditions but increased weather variability and approaching winter conditions. June typically has too much snow and stream crossings difficult with melt.
routes.comms: No cell service should be expected at any point on this route. Bear Mountain is a remote, roadless area within North Cascades National Park. Satellite communicators or emergency beacons are strongly recommended.
routes.detailedRack: 60-meter single rope, full rack of cams from 0.5 to 3 inches (bring doubles for 0.5, 0.75, 1, and 2-inch sizes), selection of nuts and chocks (0.4 to 1.5 inches), ice axe, crampons, helmet. For the Beckey-Fielding route, minimal aid gear (1-2 pitons, 1 small bolt).
routes.proNeeds: Full protection via natural rock features (cracks and edges). The route features consistent quality rock with good crack systems for natural protection. Climbers should be comfortable with gear placement on granitic rock with varied crack widths from fingers to fists to off-widths.
whatToBring: sunscreen and lip balm with SPF
first aid kit and personal medications
rock climbing shoes and approach shoes

pitchDetail.notes: Steep glacier left of buttress, crampons and ice axe helpful. Approximately 60-90 minutes from saddle camp.
150 feet. Steep corner climbing just left of the main crest. Solid granitic rock with good protection.
120 feet. Fun climbing directly on the crest with excellent rock quality and protection.
120 feet. Steep rock left of crest (5.8) to a nice belay ledge below an off-width crack.
120 feet. Off-width crack with positive crimpy holds and excellent protection. The crux pitch of the upper buttress.
120 feet. Blocks and cracks on the crest leading to below a steep step.
150 feet. Airy face climbing on the crest to a set of twin cracks.
150 feet. Blocky terrain leading to easier terrain near the summit pyramid.

access.landManager: National Park Service (North Cascades National Park)
access.permit: Backcountry permit required for North Cascades National Park (US side). Canadian approach requires passport and may have additional regulations.
access.passRequired: US passport required for Chilliwack Lake approach (British Columbia, Canada). Canadian visitors require documentation for border crossing.

climate.forecastZone: Chilliwack, BC avalanche forecast zone (Avalanche Canada) / NWAC Mount Baker zone
climate.typical: Highly variable mountain weather with frequent precipitation and rapid weather changes typical of the Pacific Northwest. Average summer temperatures 40-50F at elevation. Overnight temperatures regularly drop to freezing even in July-August.
climate.spring: Not recommended; excessive snow and stream crossings difficult with snowmelt.
climate.summer: Best conditions July-August with more stable weather patterns. Day temperatures 50-65F at elevation, nights near freezing. Semi-permanent snowfields provide meltwater and safe glacier travel.
climate.fall: September increasingly unstable with approaching winter systems. Shorter daylight. Early October often sees first significant snows.
climate.winter: Not recommended. Extreme avalanche danger, heavy snow, and dangerous weather make this peak inaccessible to most parties.

timing.recommendedStart: Early morning (3-4 AM) if climbing from base camp, to minimize exposure to afternoon weather and rockfall from ice melt. Previous day arrival recommended for acclimatization.

emergency.rangerStation: North Cascades National Park Ranger Station (Marblemount area): 360-873-4500 or 911
emergency.nearestHospital: PeaceHealth St. Joseph Medical Center (Bellingham, WA) - Level II Trauma Center, approximately 60 miles; United General Hospital (Sedro-Woolley, WA) approximately 40 miles
emergency.notes: No cell service in remote area. Satellite communicator (Garmin InReach or SPOT) strongly recommended. Emergency extraction extremely difficult; this is a very remote location requiring multi-day approach. Report location and cell number early in 911 call.

routes.corrections: Given elevation of 7,949 feet corrects to 7,931 feet per Wikipedia and MountainProject (most authoritative sources). AAC publications cite 7,942 feet. The given route has 13 pitches in database; sources document 7 pitches of class 5 climbing (upper buttress via Beckey-Fielding route). A separate route exists: Direct North Buttress (Kearney-Knight 1980) with 21 pitches and Grade V/VI+ rating; pitch discrepancy may stem from confusion between these routes.
proTips: Bring extra water purification tablets or lightweight filter - meltwater from glaciers can be heavily silted
The north-facing approach provides cooler temperatures and better snow stability than alternatives
Blue ice conditions are possible on the glacier - microspikes or crampons mandatory
Carry rain gear religiously; afternoon thunderstorms common in July-August
Place a float plan with park rangers or local contacts before attempting this remote climb
watchOut: Loose rock and sand on slabs is common; helmet mandatory
Crevasse hazards on glacier require roped team and crevasse rescue knowledge
Schrund crossing can be dangerous and may require rappel setup
Exposure on upper pitches is significant; secure your system and focus on climbing
Rockfall hazard from melting ice above some pitches, especially afternoon after sun exposure
Off-width climbing on pitch 4 is strenuous; rest before attempting
knownHazards: North face features a mile-wide rock wall rising 2,500 feet with very significant exposure
Objective hazard: rockfall from upper sections, particularly when temperatures rise during the day
Crevasse falls on glacier approach; proper training and equipment mandatory
Sudden weather changes common; the area can deteriorate rapidly from stable to storm in hours
Loose blocks and questionable rock on some sections require careful route-finding
Stream crossings during approach may be dangerous during spring snowmelt
Extreme remoteness makes rescue impractical; self-sufficiency mandatory

road.name: Hannegan Pass Road and logging roads to Chilliwack Lake (Canada)
road.status: Seasonal; Hannegan Pass Road opens late spring when snow melts
road.seasonalGate: Hannegan Pass Road typically open June-October; verify current status with North Cascades National Park
road.driveNote: Hannegan Pass Road is rough and may require high-clearance vehicle. Total drive from Seattle approximately 2.5-3 hours to trailhead. Canadian approach via Chilliwack Lake Road (BC Provincial); road quality variable. Have current passport ready for border crossing if using Canadian approach.

blurb: Mount Redoubt is the 21st highest peak in Washington, standing at 8,956 feet (2,730 m) on the northern edge of North Cascades National Park within the Stephen Mather Wilderness. Composed of Skagit gneiss, the peak was first climbed in 1930 by Jimmy Cherry and Bob Ross and features the Redoubt Glacier on its eastern slopes, which feeds into Depot Creek. The mountain presents serious alpine climbing with glaciated terrain, mixed snow and rock, and significant objective hazards including crevasses and rockfall.
hierarchyNote: Database elevation listed as 8,963 ft; Wikipedia, WTA, and PeakBagger sources consistently report 8,956 ft (2,730 m). This 7-foot discrepancy should be verified against USGS data. Peak location confirmed correct: North Cascades National Park, Whatcom County, Washington, on U.S.-Canada border near Monument 65.

routes.overview: The South Face/Redoubt Glacier route climbs via the Redoubt Glacier on the eastern slopes, ascending mixed snow, scree, and Class 4 rock to the summit. The crux features exposed scrambling, a narrow couloir system with up to 50-degree snow, and a distinctive chockstone cannonhole feature requiring downclimbing and rappelling. A serious alpine objective with sustained exposure and route-finding challenges.
routes.beta: From Ouzel Lake base camp (5,640 ft), cross Depot Creek and climb scree to the slabby buttress. Follow the buttress crest south, then traverse right to gain Redoubt Glacier. Head west to the col (~7,760 ft) south of the flying buttress. Traverse west on easy snow to a rock/snow gully. Climb the gully (~300 ft, steepening to ~50 degrees). Locate the snow and talus ridge; head left to avoid steeper terrain. Navigate narrow couloir with crumbling red rock. At the cannonhole (chockstone), use existing rappel slings to pass beneath; descend and traverse to left couloir. Scramble final steep section to summit on mostly solid Class 4 rock.
routes.approach: Drive Transcanada Highway 1 and take Exit 119A (Chilliwack/Sardis). Head south through Chilliwack town 3.4 miles and turn left onto Chilliwack River Road (just before bridge). Drive 31.7 miles and turn left onto narrow dirt Depot Creek Road (unsigned, ~200 yards before Depot Creek bridge). Park at Depot Creek Road washout (2,600 ft). Hike overgrown forest road (2 miles) to Monument 65 (international border) then continue on crude trail through old-growth forest to Depot Creek Falls. Cross falls on slippery boulders and slabs using fixed ropes at vertical steps. Ascend steep talus to reach Ouzel Lake base camp (~5,700 ft). Total approach distance approximately 7-8 miles from road end.
routes.descent: Downclimb to the chockstone in the cannonhole and lower using long slings tied to existing rappel anchors. Traverse left to a major gully and descend to a gravel ledge. Regain snow/talus ridge and return to camp via ascent route. Allow minimum 3 hours for descent to parking area.
routes.descentText: From summit, downclimb approximately 50 feet of Class 4 rock then enter a steep gully system. A belayed downclimb of upper Class 4 face is followed by an aided downclimb below the chockstone using fixed slings. Descend via single 30-meter rappels down the highest chimney into gullies (approximately 3 rappel stations with established anchors). Scramble down loose gullies to reach the snow/talus ridge. Follow the ridge south back down to Redoubt Glacier and descend via ascent route across the glacier and buttress back to camp.
routes.itinerary: Day 1: Depot Creek Road (2,600 ft) to Ouzel Lake Camp (5,700 ft) – 6-7 hours. Hike overgrown road, cross Depot Creek Falls on fixed ropes, scramble talus to base camp. Day 2: Ouzel Lake to Summit (8,956 ft) and return to camp – 10-12 hours round trip. Depart early morning (6+ AM) for optimal snow conditions; 4-6 hours to summit, 3+ hours descent. Day 3: Ouzel Lake to Depot Creek Road – 4-5 hours descent and return to vehicle.
routes.bail: From the Redoubt Glacier, retreat is straightforward down the ascent route. If caught in bad weather in the upper gullies, descend to the Redoubt Glacier and return to Ouzel Lake base camp. The cannonhole offers some shelter but is not suitable for bivy. Establish a high camp at 7,300 ft on the Redoubt Glacier if weather becomes threatening to reduce exposure.
routes.turnaround: Turn around by early afternoon to ensure safe descent with daylight. Monitor weather throughout the day; if conditions deteriorate significantly, retreat from any point on the route. Route-finding in white-out conditions is extremely difficult; avoid being caught high on the peak.
routes.bestSeason: July, August, and September offer the best climbing conditions. July provides generally stable snow with good visibility, though some bergschrunds may be wide. August offers more melted conditions with more exposed rock but harder blue ice on glacier sections. September has reliable conditions but increasing avalanche danger. Avoid spring (March-May) due to extensive snow work, poor snow quality, and high avalanche danger on south-facing slopes.
routes.comms: No cellular service in the remote Cascade backcountry. A satellite communicator (Garmin inReach or similar) or satellite phone is essential for this objective. Two-way satellite messaging devices have proven reliable on high-alpine ridges. Contact North Cascades National Park Dispatch at (360) 873-4500 ext. 37 for emergency assistance, or dial 911 to reach County Sheriff (Whatcom County 360-384-5390 or Skagit County 360-336-9450).
routes.detailedRack: Single ice axe, crampons, helmet, and a standard alpine rack including slings, stoppers, and small cams (0.3-1.5 inches). Bring 2 x 60-meter ropes or double 60-meter ropes for rappelling. Hip belay anchors useful for glacier travel. Pickets for snow anchors. Tape and slings for expedient anchor building on rock sections.
routes.proNeeds: Protection is sparse on the summit pitches; the final pitch features Class 4 rock with minimal pro opportunities. Most anchors consist of existing fixed slings at established rappel stations (chockstone, summit area anchors). Parties typically rely on downclimbing and careful route-finding rather than bolted protection. Glacier travel crevasse rescue kit (pickets, pulleys, carabiners) is essential for a party of 2+.
whatToBring: Tent and sleeping bag rated for alpine cold
Sufficient food and water (can melt snow)
proTips: Arrive at Depot Creek Road washout the evening before to start early on approach day
The trail to Depot Creek Falls is extremely overgrown; bring machete or brush rake
Fixed ropes at the falls can be old; test before trusting full weight
Camp at the flat, sandy, treeless benches at Ouzel Lake for protection from weather
Mark the ascent route with cairns to prevent confusion on descent
Depart Ouzel Lake before 6 AM for optimal snow conditions and safe descent
Redoubt Glacier is often treated as straightforward snowfield with few open crevasses by mid-summer, but rope up for glacier travel
The cannonhole is immediately recognizable; it's a key landmark for summit approach
All three rappel stations are maintained; inspect slings and carry redundant anchoring gear
Descent traffic can be heavy in peak season; communicate clearly at anchor points
watchOut: Crevasse hazard on Redoubt Glacier increases late in season; rope up for all glacier sections
Loose Skagit gneiss is unstable; test all handholds by tapping before committing weight
Rockslide hazard beneath the Col of the Wild can produce a conveyor belt of rockfall lasting hours; move quickly
Fixed ropes at Depot Creek Falls are old and potentially unreliable; test and use caution
Water flow at the falls varies with time of day and weather; higher water makes crossing more hazardous
By August, hard blue ice may develop on glacier sections, increasing slipping risk; crampons essential
The upper summit gully can have loose rock; move carefully and announce when dislodging debris
Moat formations at glacier margins can be wide and unstable; probe carefully before committing
knownHazards: Redoubt Glacier features crevasses that are revealed late in season and require careful probing with ice axe
Skagit gneiss composition creates unstable rock that produces frequent rockfall; rock quality is unreliable for protection
Seracs and icefall zones exist on the glacier approach; avoid lingering beneath unstable ice formations
Avalanche danger on south-facing slopes in spring can be significant; route not recommended before July
Bergschrund moats can become substantial by mid-to-late season, requiring careful downclimbing or bridge testing
Late-summer conditions can expose blue ice on glacier sections, dramatically increasing slipping hazard

pitchDetail.notes: Approximately 300 feet of sustained snow climbing from col to rock/talus transition. Can be step-kicking early season or hard ice later. Watch for occasional rockfall from above.

Narrow couloir with crumbling red Skagit gneiss. Ascend with care, testing handholds. Lead left at any major branches. Section is often partially snow-filled but exposed rock sections require scrambling on unstable stone.

Vertical shallow gully with chockstone. Scramble up good rock and follow the gully system. At steep gully top, exit left into major gully to locate the cannonhole. Crab through the hole beneath an embedded chockstone, then scramble 40 feet of Class 3.5-4.0 rock to summit. Existing rappel slings at multiple points.

road.name: Chilliwack River Road / Depot Creek Road
road.status: Seasonal, rough
road.seasonalGate: No gate, but accessibility varies with road conditions and snow. Late May through September typically passable.
road.driveNote: High-clearance or 4WD vehicle recommended. The road is narrow, rough, and overgrown in sections. Winter snow and washouts can make it impassable. Park at Depot Creek Road washout (2,600 ft) as reliable turnaround point.

climate.forecastZone: Cascades of Whatcom and Skagit Counties (NOAA); Northwest Avalanche Center (Cascade West South zone)
climate.typical: Marine west coast climate with high precipitation, especially in fall and winter. Summer offers best weather with temperatures 45-70F, least precipitation (1.7 inches/month). Frequent afternoon showers common even in summer.
climate.spring: March-May: Heavy snowfall, avalanche danger, wet snow, poor stability. Not recommended for this route due to extensive snow work and high avalanche hazard on south-facing slopes.
climate.summer: June-September: Most reliable climbing season. June offers straightforward snow conditions but some bergschrunds. July-August provide stable conditions with more melted sections (harder ice later). September increasingly unstable.
climate.fall: October-November: Increasing snowfall, shorter daylight, unstable conditions. Rapidly deteriorating climbing window.
climate.winter: December-April: Park buried in heavy snow, avalanche danger high, extreme cold, limited daylight. Not viable for this route.

access.landManager: North Cascades National Park (National Park Service), Whatcom County, Washington
access.fees: None for trailhead parking; backcountry camping: $10 per person plus $6 non-refundable reservation fee
access.permit: Wilderness permit required year-round for backcountry camping. Reserve on Recreation.gov or obtain walk-in permits at Wilderness Information Center (Marblemount) by 11 AM day-of or day before.
access.closures: Depot Creek Road and approach trail may be seasonally inaccessible due to snow, washouts, or road conditions (typically accessible late May to September only).

timing.recommendedStart: Early morning (5-6 AM from base camp) to complete ascent and descent with daylight safety margin

emergency.sheriffDispatch: Whatcom County Sheriff: (360) 384-5390; Skagit County Sheriff: (360) 336-9450; North Cascades National Park Dispatch: (360) 873-4500 ext. 37
emergency.rangerStation: Wilderness Information Center (Marblemount): (360) 854-7245 or noca_wilderness@nps.gov
emergency.nearestHospital: PeaceHealth St. Luke's Hospital (Bellingham, WA), approximately 60 miles from trailhead
emergency.notes: Remote location with no cell service. Satellite communicator or PLB essential. Rescue typically requires helicopter extraction, weather-dependent. Plan for rescue delays of hours to days. Notify park rangers of itinerary before departing.

objHaz: Rockslide hazard below Col of the Wild

blurb: Lemah Mountain is a distinctive five-summited alpine peak in Washington's Snoqualmie Crest (North Cascades), reaching 7,512 feet. Located in the Alpine Lakes Wilderness, its name derives from Chinook Jargon meaning "hand"—fitting for the five distinct summits. The main peak was first climbed July 7, 1923, by Wallace Burr and party; it offers classic alpine mountaineering over glacier ice and Class 2-4 granite rock to an exposed summit.

routes.rappels: Optional; standard descent by downclimbing Class 3-4
routes.bestSeason: Late July through August offer optimal snow and ice conditions on the glacier with lower avalanche risk. June can have excessive snow; September becomes colder with rapidly changing weather. Winter ascents are possible but require full alpine winter mountaineering skills.
routes.overview: The East Route ascends Lemah Mountain's southeast face via the Lemah Glacier, combining moderate glacier travel with Class 2-4 rock scrambling to the main summit. This route is the standard approach and most accessible way to reach the peak.
routes.beta: From the glacier terminus (~5,000 ft), traverse to the south and ascend glacial remnants with minimal crevasses. Exit the glacier around 6,200 ft and traverse southwest across cliff bands (Class 2-3) to a col between Lemah Two and the main summit. From the col, climb north and traverse west across the summit block (Class 2-3) to reach the true summit via the northwest ridge.
routes.approach: From the Cooper Lake trailhead (2,800 ft), hike the Cooper River Trail (FST 1323) north 4 miles through relatively gentle terrain to Pete Lake. Continue 1.2 miles north at all junctions to reach the PCT intersection. Head south on the PCT approximately 1 mile to the bridge crossing Lemah Creek. Turn right (north) and proceed cross-country up Lemah Creek valley approximately 1.1 miles to the Lemah Meadow area (3,600 ft), where the creek becomes a series of shallow ponds. Base camps are typically established at Spectacle Lake, Glacier Lake, or Chikamin Lake on Day 1 for a two-day ascent.
routes.descent: Downclimb the route in reverse. The standard descent retraces the ascent route: negotiate the summit block and cliff bands (Class 2-4), traverse across glacial remnants, cross the glacier moat (with caution), and descend the snow and glacier back to the meadow area. Rappels are optional; many parties downclimb instead, though some use 1-3 rappels to shorten the descent on ice/snow sections.
routes.descentText: From the summit, descend southwest across the summit block (Class 2-3) to the col between Lemah Two and the main peak. Traverse northeast back to the cliff bands and downclimb Class 2-3 terrain carefully, watching for loose rock. Exit to the glacier around 6,200 ft and retrace steps across glacial remnants. Cross the moat (often requires careful scrambling or short downclimbs; may have water flow in late summer) and descend the Lemah Glacier slope using both downclimbing and glissading as conditions allow. Crampons are often helpful for controlling descent on firm snow. Return to Lemah Meadow via the lower glacier and creek valley; allow 2-3 hours for this descent section.
routes.itinerary: Day 1: Depart Cooper Lake trailhead early (6-7 AM). Hike Cooper River Trail to Pete Lake (1.5-2 hours), continue to PCT junction (0.5 hours), then south on PCT to Lemah Creek (0.5 hours). Proceed up-valley to Lemah Meadow and establish base camp at Spectacle Lake, Glacier Lake, or Chikamin Lake (2-3 hours). Arrive camp by mid-afternoon for adequate rest before summit day. Day 2: Pre-dawn start from camp (4-5 AM). Navigate cross-country to glacier terminus (1-2 hours), ascend glacier and rock (3-4 hours to summit). Summit and return to glacier (1-2 hours descent). Return to camp via glacier and valley (2-3 hours). Total Day 2: 8-10 hours.
routes.bail: From Lemah Meadow, retreat down-valley toward Lemah Creek and PCT; well-established trail back to Pete Lake and Cooper River Trail. From the glacier, descend to the meadow area via either the main glacier or rock ribs to the east. No objective hazard prevents retreat. In emergency rescue situation, helicopter access is possible in the meadow area; establish cell contact or use satellite communicator if available.
routes.turnaround: Establish a firm turnaround time based on daylight remaining and weather forecast. For late-spring/summer ascents, a turnaround at 2-3 PM ensures safe descent in daylight. Do not continue toward the summit if you are more than 3-4 hours behind schedule; the descent is more dangerous than the ascent and technical scrambling in darkness is hazardous.
routes.comms: Cell service is unreliable and spotty throughout the Alpine Lakes Wilderness. Do not rely on cell phones for emergency communication. Satellite messengers (Garmin InReach, SPOT) are recommended. The area receives service from Kittitas County Sheriff's Search and Rescue; ensure someone knows your itinerary and expected return time.
routes.proNeeds: Moderate alpine mountaineering protection needed for optional rappels; glacier travel requires standard crevasse rescue gear (harness, rope, carabiners) despite minimal crevasses on Lemah Glacier. Natural rock protection is good where needed for belayed downclimbs.
routes.detailedRack: Crevasse rescue kit: 60m rope (half-rope system acceptable), harnesses for party, carabiners (6+ lockers), belay/rescue anchors, ascenders. Rock protection: minimal needed (party size and conditions determine); medium-sized quickdraws (3-4) and runners sufficient for any belay anchors. Ice tools: crampons, ice axes, and helmet for all party members. Consider bringing 2-3 slings (runner length) for anchor building on rock.
whatToBring: High-calorie food (trail mix, energy bars, gels)
objHaz: Crevasses (minimal on Lemah Glacier but present)
knownHazards: The glacier moat is the main objective hazard; late season (August-September) moats widen and deepen, creating difficult downclimb or dangerous jump crossings.
Steep snow slopes between 35-45 degrees make falls consequential and arrest essential.
Early-season (June) and late-season (October) ascents carry elevated avalanche risk; mid-summer (July-August) is safest.
The Class 4 rock on the upper route is often wet and loose; careful foot placement and helmet use are mandatory.
Chimney/gully areas can have water flowing in late summer, making downclimbing slick.
watchOut: Do not underestimate the moat crossing; scout your descent route thoroughly on the ascent.
Descending is more dangerous than ascending—slick rock and gravity make downclimbing harder than climbing up.
Weather changes rapidly above 7,000 ft in the Cascades; turn back if clouds build in afternoon.
The long approach (8-9 miles) means a late start can force a benighted descent.
Crampons are essential for all seasons; firm snow/ice is present even in late summer.
proTips: A two-day trip with base camp at Glacier Lake or Chikamin Lake is less stressful than a single-day push from the trailhead.
Descend via rock slabs wherever possible rather than soft snow to save time and energy.
Scout the moat crossing carefully on descent; some parties climb onto the rock wall east of the glacier to avoid the moat entirely.
Bring a rope if conditions are steep/icy; many parties use short rappels instead of downclimbing the steepest sections.
Start very early (pre-dawn) on summit day to maximize daylight for the glacier and rock descent.

pitchDetail.notes: From glacier terminus (~5,000 ft) to exit around 6,200 ft. Snow and ice intermixed with glacial remnants. Rope team recommended for crevasse protection, though crevasses are minimal. Crampons essential. Allow 2-3 hours.
Cross moat (water and crevasse hazard), then climb small cliff to glacial remnants. Can be difficult if moat is wide; alternative route exists via rock rib to the east.

Traverse southwest across series of cliff bands toward the col between Lemah Two and main peak. Pleasant scrambling with good rock and natural protection available. Exposed but manageable. About 1 hour.

From col between Lemah Two and main peak, climb north toward summit, then traverse west across exposed ridge. Rock is mostly stable with good holds. Navigate around or over a small summit block (Class 2-3). Final 30 feet below true summit traverses west and climbs northwest ridge (Class 2).

timing.recommendedStart: 4:30-5:30 AM from base camp (Day 2). Early start critical for safe daylight descent.

climate.typical: Alpine Cascades climate: cool and wet with rapid weather changes above 7,000 ft. Afternoon thunderstorms common in summer.
climate.spring: May: Heavy snow coverage; avalanche danger high. Crampons mandatory; route-finding more difficult.
climate.summer: July-August: Optimal conditions. Moderate snow/ice, stable weather patterns, longer daylight. Night-time temps around 25-35°F at elevation.
climate.fall: September: Cooler, clearer weather but increasing instability. Early snow possible late September.
climate.winter: October-May: Heavy snow, avalanche hazard, short daylight hours. Mountaineering skills and winter gear essential.

access.landManager: USDA Forest Service (Mount Baker-Snoqualmie National Forest and Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest), Alpine Lakes Wilderness (Cle Elum Ranger District)
access.permit: Free self-issued permit required for both day-use and overnight visitors; permits available at all trailheads.
access.passRequired: Northwest Forest Pass required for parking at Cooper Lake trailhead ($5 one-time or annual pass).
access.closures: No year-round closures. Some seasonal trail restrictions may apply; check with Cle Elum Ranger District (509-852-1100) before trip.

road.name: Cooper Lake Road (Forest Service Road 46)
road.status: Gravel/dirt, seasonal (typically passable June-October)
road.seasonalGate: No locked gate; seasonal washouts possible in spring runoff and early winter.
road.driveNote: From I-90 Exit 80 near Cle Elum, drive north 3 miles to WA-903. Turn left, follow through Roslyn and Ronald. Turn left on FS Road 46 (unpaved) for 4.8 miles. Road becomes progressively rougher; high-clearance vehicle recommended. Take right fork toward Owhi Campground; continue 1.2 miles to Cooper River trailhead parking at road's end.

emergency.county: King County (western slope) / Kittitas County (eastern slope)
emergency.rangerStation: Cle Elum Ranger District, USDA Forest Service: (509) 852-1100
emergency.nearestHospital: Kittitas Valley Healthcare, 214 W 1st St, Cle Elum, WA 98922 (approximate 1-1.5 hour drive from trailhead)
emergency.notes: Cell service is unreliable in the Alpine Lakes Wilderness; satellite communicator (InReach/SPOT) highly recommended. Kittitas County Search and Rescue has over 120 volunteer personnel and responds to backcountry incidents in the area. File a trip plan with a responsible person and check in upon return.

routes.fa: First ascent of Lemah Mountain main peak: July 7, 1923 by Wallace Burr and party. This specific route (East Route/Glacier Route) has no documented separate first ascent; it is the standard mountaineering approach.
routes.corrections: Elevation in source data listed as 7519 ft; authoritative sources (Wikipedia, SummitPost, Peakbagger, USGS) consistently report the main peak at 7,512 ft. This is a minor discrepancy (~7 ft) within measurement uncertainty, but USGS topographic data supports 7,512 ft. Alpine grade listed as PD+ is consistent with Grade I Glacier / Basic Alpine Climb classifications.

hierarchyNote: Hierarchy verified: Lincoln Peak (9,101 ft) is correctly positioned as a subsidiary of Mount Baker in the North Cascades, Whatcom County, Washington. ROUTE NAME DISCREPANCIES: Database lists 'Southeast Approach (Standard)' and 'North Ridge / Standard', but documented routes are 'Southwest Face / X Couloir' (standard) and 'Wilkes-Booth Route NW Face' (FA 2015). Database route names do not match published climbing sources; corrected route names provided.

routes.beta: The standard and only remotely feasible route on Lincoln Peak. Approach via Rankin Creek Basin from FS Road 38. Technical snow and ice climbing with significant avalanche risk. Route comprises multiple sections: 30-40 degree initial snowfields, crux serac obstacle requiring 70-85 degree ice climbing (50 feet high), hidden gully with 2-4 foot deep runnels, narrow exposed snow aretes with steep drop-offs, and final 50-degree gully to summit rocks. Climb early morning when snow is frozen; conditions deteriorate rapidly with afternoon warming.
routes.overview: Lincoln Peak (9,101 ft) is a highly technical volcanic summit in the Mount Baker Wilderness among the 'Black Buttes'. Its easiest and only feasible route via the southwest face involves sustained steep snow and ice climbing through highly exposed terrain on unstable volcanic conglomerate. Considered one of the most technically demanding peaks in Washington by its easiest line; first ascent: Fred Beckey, Wesley Grande, Herb Staley, John Rupley; July 1956.
routes.approach: Drive FS Road 38 up the Middle Fork Nooksack valley approximately 12.5 miles to the first switchback (passable in compact car). Continue walking/pushing bike up overgrown road to 7th switchback (approximately 14 miles round trip from parking). Follow old trail and scattered path around south ridge northward to end of roadway. Bushwhack and scramble up to Rankin Creek Basin. Navigate to 5,480-foot saddle via spur road and forest sections. Establish camp at approximately 6,100 feet overlooking Wallace Creek Basin near Seward Peak's ridge. Alpine start required (1:45 AM typical departure).
routes.descent: From summit, downclimb via southwest face terrain to rappel stations. Execute 10 double-rope rappels using natural rock anchors and slings on natural features. Rappels can be rapid in good conditions; be prepared for route-finding complexity and afternoon rockfall hazard in constriction gully.
routes.descentText: Descent follows the southwest face back toward base camp via 10 double-rope rappels anchored to natural rock features. Total descent time typically 4-6 hours depending on snow conditions and anchor quality. Downclimbing is possible in good consolidated snow. Be alert for afternoon rockfall from the upper sections, particularly in the narrow constriction gully.
routes.bestSeason: Late May through early June represents the optimal climbing window. The sweet spot occurs when fresh snowfall decreases while overnight temperatures remain cold enough to stabilize ice, snow, and volcanic rock. Early season (April-early May) presents increased avalanche danger from fresh snow; late season (mid-June+) exposes climbers to wet slides and enhanced rockfall from deteriorating volcanic rock.
routes.turnaround: Climbers should establish a firm turnaround time by mid-morning (approximately 11 AM-noon) to allow safe descent before afternoon conditions deteriorate. Continued climbing past mid-morning dramatically increases rockfall and avalanche risk on descent. If major obstacles (large bergschrund, deep bergschrund gaps, serac impassibility) are encountered before reaching upper basin, retreat immediately.
knownHazards: Avalanche risk highly sensitive to snow loading and temperature cycles; conditions can change within hours
Bergschrunds with variable gap size (sometimes filled early season, dangerous to cross)
Seracs and falling ice in upper basin sections
Continuous exposure on narrow snow aretes with steep drop-offs on both sides
Unstable volcanic conglomerate rock; rockfall increases with afternoon warming
Route-finding complexity; GPS recommended
watchOut: Turn around by mid-morning regardless of progress to ensure safe descent before afternoon warming increases rockfall
Do not climb if fresh snow has fallen in past 24-48 hours; wait for consolidation
Be prepared for bergschrund crossings up to 80 degrees steep ice
Rime-covered rock can be deceptive; trust placements may fail on weathered volcanic conglomerate
Camp location at 6,100 feet is in active avalanche terrain; site carefully
proTips: Climb in May (last week) when bergshrunds are often difficult to cross but overall conditions are most stable
Heliotrope Ridge approach is often more crowded; Rankin Creek offers slightly shorter approach without descent required on exit
Bring two 60-meter ropes for efficient rappelling; 50-meter ropes require extra rappels and waste time
Pre-place GPS waypoints at East Rankin Lake (5,120 ft), camp saddle (6,100 ft), and lower basin to aid route-finding
Downclimbing 70-85 degree ice may be faster than rappelling if conditions are excellent
routes.fa: Fred Beckey, Wesley Grande, Herb Staley, John Rupley; July 1956

timing.recommendedStart: 1:45 AM from camp (6,100 ft)

climate.forecastZone: Cascades of Whatcom and Skagit Counties (National Weather Service Zone WAZ301)
climate.typical: Highly variable high-altitude Cascade climate. May-June conditions: daytime highs near 30-40F, overnight lows 10-20F. Clear nights essential for ice climbing. Afternoon cloud build-up typical; expect 20-40 mph winds.
climate.spring: Transitional period with high avalanche sensitivity. Fresh snow common early May; late May typically sees decreasing precipitation and strengthening high-pressure ridges. Freezing level fluctuates 6,000-7,500 feet.
climate.summer: Route rarely climbs in summer due to unstable volcanic rock and wet avalanches. Freezing level climbs above 10,000 feet; rock climbing becomes primary hazard.
climate.fall: Not a climbing season for this peak; snowfall begins mid-September; conditions deteriorate rapidly.
climate.winter: Winter ascents extremely rare; winter conditions untested on this specific peak. Avalanche hazard severe.

routes.comms: No cellular coverage in Mount Baker Wilderness. Satellite communication devices (Garmin InReach, Spot) are essential. Trip should be registered with local climbing clubs (CascadeClimbers.com) or responsible party notified of plans and expected return.

road.name: Mount Baker Highway 542 and Forest Service Road 38 (Middle Fork Nooksack)
road.status: FS Road 38 typically open May-October depending on snow and washout conditions; verify with Snoqualmie Ranger District (360-677-2414)
road.seasonalGate: Typically closed November-May; opening date varies with snow melt
road.driveNote: From Glacier Public Service Center, take Mount Baker Highway 542 east approximately 1 mile to Glacier Creek Road (FS 39), then turn onto FS 39 for 8 miles to the Heliotrope Ridge Trailhead. For Lincoln Peak, continue past Heliotrope to FS Road 38 and drive 12.5 miles to first switchback (passable in compact car). Rankin Creek approach is preferred over Heliotrope Ridge due to slightly shorter distance and no required descent on exit.

emergency.rangerStation: Snoqualmie Ranger District, Mount Baker-Snoqualmie National Forest (360-677-2414)
emergency.nearestHospital: PeaceHealth St. Joseph Medical Center, Bellingham, WA (approximately 60 miles / 1.5 hours drive time from trailhead)
emergency.notes: Wilderness emergencies: call 911 immediately. Helicopter rescue extremely difficult in bad weather; parties should assume self-rescue in most scenarios. GPS communication devices (InReach, Spot) essential for remote location.

gear: Sling material and carabiners for rappel anchors
routes.detailedRack: Two 60-meter dynamic ropes are standard. Belay anchors use a combination of ice screws (4-6 total; typically #2-#3 Grivel or Black Diamond), natural rock slings, and pickets placed in snow. Most rappel anchors are natural horns slung with webbing or dynamic sling material. Crampons should have rigid front points suitable for 70-80 degree ice. Two ice axes: one for climbing, one for safety. Approximately 6-8 pickets for snow anchors.
routes.proNeeds: Protection strategy changes by section: (1) lower snowfields, pickets in consolidated snow; (2) bergschrund crossing, ice screws in steep ice; (3) hidden gully, ice screw protection intermittent due to mixed snow/ice; (4) snow aretes, natural anchors and slings on rock horns; (5) final gully, ice screws or rock protection. Most pitches are simul-climbed with running protection. Rappels anchored to natural rock features (horns, blocks) slung with webbing—no bolts or permanent anchors in place.

routes.rappels: Downclimb or rappel southwest face sections
routes.beta: Technical ice and mixed climbing route on the northwest face. Begins at the Shooting Gallery route junction with a WI4 pitch immediately right of the prominent hanging glacier. Climbers then simul-climb along the western upper amphitheater basin (between Lincoln and Assassin Spire), followed by approximately four roped pitches on steeper ice with snowfields up to 70° in between, concluding with rime-covered ridge climbing to the summit. Route benefits from 'all-time conditions' with nearly snowless winter and solid ice formation. Descent typically via southwest face route.
routes.overview: A technical ice and mixed alpine route on the northwest face of Lincoln Peak, featuring 2,000 feet of climbing through WI4 ice and rime-covered ridge terrain. This is the only documented first ascent alternative to the standard southwest face route (FA: Daniel Coltrane and Michal Rynkiewicz; March 13, 2015; 18.5 hours round-trip from Heliotrope Ridge trailhead).
routes.approach: Access from Heliotrope Ridge trailhead (48°48′7″N 121°53′45″W, parking at 3,437 ft). Hike Heliotrope Ridge Trail 677 approximately 5.6 miles to reach the Shooting Gallery route confluence area. Continue to northwest face approach via Coleman Glacier region or direct approach from upper snowfields. Route finding to the Shooting Gallery junction is non-trivial; local knowledge or detailed GPS coordinates essential. First ascent party completed round-trip in 18.5 hours from Heliotrope Ridge trailhead.
routes.descent: Downclimb or rappel southwest face sections back toward Coleman Glacier approach. Descent is more exposed and complex than direct northwest face downclimbing due to terrain. Typical descent time 3-5 hours depending on snow consolidation.
routes.descentText: From the summit, climbers can either downclimb the rime-covered ridge to the western amphitheater, then downclimb or rappel the steeper ice pitches, or alternatively traverse the southwest face approach for descent. The southwest face descent via rappels is more established but requires careful anchor placement.
routes.bestSeason: March (winter/early spring); all-time conditions benefitted first ascent party on March 13, 2015
routes.turnaround: Given the technical nature and one-day turnaround time achieved (18.5 hours from trailhead), climbers should establish turnaround by early afternoon (approximately 2 PM) to ensure safe conditions and adequate light for descent. Any delays in ice climbing sections warrant retreat.
knownHazards: WI4 ice climbing sections—crux first pitch is steep and sustained
Avalanche risk in western amphitheater basin and snowfield approach
Rime ice on upper ridge—poor friction; enhanced rockfall risk
Seracs and hanging glacier in approach area
Complex route finding; north face terrain less traveled than southwest face route
watchOut: First ascent party benefitted from exceptional 'all-time' conditions; typical winter/spring conditions may be significantly more difficult
Route sharing with Assassin Spire Shooting Gallery approach can create crowding and objective hazard from other parties
Upper rime-covered ridge is extremely exposed and prone to cornice formation
Weather windows are extremely limited; the first ascent occurred during a one-day weather window
proTips: This route has only one documented ascent (March 13, 2015); treat it as an exploratory climb requiring self-rescue capability
Study the first ascent article in American Alpine Club publications for detailed photos and pitch descriptions
Approach from Heliotrope Ridge is more established than Rankin Creek; allows easier evacuation if needed
Bring extra ice screws for anchor placement on first ascent-style routes where anchors may not exist
GPS tracking recommended; route finding in poor visibility would be extremely challenging
routes.fa: Daniel Coltrane and Michal Rynkiewicz; March 13, 2015

timing.recommendedStart: Early morning alpine start (exact time not documented)

climate.forecastZone: Cascades of Whatcom and Skagit Counties (National Weather Service Zone WAZ301)
climate.typical: Winter/early spring Cascade climate. March conditions: variable freezing level (4,000-6,500 ft), occasional storm systems, high wind exposure on north-facing aspects. First ascent party encountered nearly snowless winter conditions with exceptional ice formation.
climate.spring: Transitional season with improving stability in late March/early April. North-facing aspects retain ice longer than south-facing slopes.
climate.summer: Route not climbed in summer; rime ice melts, ice climbing becomes impossible.
climate.fall: Not a climbing season.
climate.winter: Typical winter climbing conditions; north-facing terrain retains ice. Avalanche risk significant in amphitheater basins.

routes.comms: No cellular coverage in wilderness area. Satellite communication devices (Garmin InReach, Spot) essential. This route is rarely climbed; notify local climbing clubs or responsible party of plans and expected return.

road.name: Mount Baker Highway 542, Forest Service Road 39 (Glacier Creek Road), and Heliotrope Ridge Trail access
road.status: FS Road 39 typically passable May-September for passenger vehicles; may be gated or washed out in winter/early spring
road.seasonalGate: Seasonal restrictions may apply; verify with Snoqualmie Ranger District (360-677-2414). First ascent party accessed Heliotrope Ridge in mid-March 2015, suggesting late-winter road access was possible during that season.
road.driveNote: From Glacier, Washington, drive Mount Baker Highway 542 east for approximately 1 mile to Glacier Creek Road (FS 39), then turn right and travel 8 miles to the Heliotrope Ridge Trailhead parking area at 3,437 feet (48°48′7″N 121°53′45″W).
