Large parking lot with facilities. If FS Road 39 is washed out or gated (common in winter), additional walking/biking distance may be required.

emergency.rangerStation: Snoqualmie Ranger District, Mount Baker-Snoqualmie National Forest (360-677-2414)
emergency.nearestHospital: PeaceHealth St. Joseph Medical Center, Bellingham, WA (approximately 60 miles / 1.5 hours drive from Heliotrope Ridge Trailhead)
emergency.notes: North-face terrain is more exposed to avalanche and rockfall; helicopter rescue extremely difficult. Parties accessing via Heliotrope Ridge have better potential rescue access than Rankin Creek route due to proximity to established trails.

gear: Crampons (rigid front points for 70-80 degree ice)
Helmet (essential for rockfall from rime ice)
Sling material and carabiners for anchors
Stove and fuel for melting snow water
routes.detailedRack: Two 60-meter climbing ropes (dynamic, rated for ice climbing). Ice screws: 8-10 total, mix of #1.5 to #3 sizes (Grivel, Black Diamond). Rock protection: cams or pitons if needed for mixed sections. Belay anchors created from ice screws, rock features, or natural horns slung with webbing. Pickets for snow anchors. Two technical ice axes suited for steep climbing. Crampons with rigid front points for vertical ice.
routes.proNeeds: Protection strategy: Initial pitches use ice screws for belaying in steep sections (60m WI4 crux). Simul-climbing used in moderate sections with running ice screw protection. Upper rime-covered ridge sections use natural rock anchors slung with webbing where available. No established permanent anchors; parties must create systems and accept inherent risks of rarely-repeated terrain.

blurb: Mount Hardy is an 8,099-foot granite peak on the Okanogan County/Skagit County border in the North Cascades, formed from the Golden Horn batholith. The mountain is a popular moderate scramble featuring the classic South Slopes route, which was first ascended in 1933 by Sidney Schmerling and Hermann Ulrichs. Rising approximately 2,500 feet above the West Fork Methow River within half a mile, Hardy offers excellent views of neighboring peaks including Golden Horn and Tower Mountain.

routes.overview: A moderate scramble up the prominent south ridge of Mount Hardy featuring loose granite, scree, and blocks. The route is a half-day climb from Highway 20 with significant exposure to loose terrain and route-finding challenges.
routes.beta: The standard approach ascends the south ridge via brushy forest to the base of the ridge, then follows the ridge upward while staying climber's right to avoid cliff bands at mid-elevations. From approximately 7,200 feet, leave the ridge and scramble loose scree and granite blocks on the east side to reach a notch below the summit block. From the notch, traverse left and scramble a shallow gully to reach the summit. Snow extends the hiking window and covers loose talus early in the season.
routes.approach: Drive State Route 20 (North Cascades Highway) approximately 2 miles west of Rainy Pass and park on the widened shoulder at the Swamp Creek pullout (just east of Swamp Creek). From the parking area, hike west along SR 20 for approximately 150-200 feet in a slightly downhill direction. Just after crossing Swamp Creek, locate a small unofficial boot trail heading right (north/northeast) into the forest. A faint trail may be difficult to follow, but it helps guide hikers through brushy sections in the lower elevation. The trail gains elevation rapidly while moving away from Swamp Creek, heading northeast into the forest to the base of the prominent south ridge.
routes.descent: Descend via the same route: reverse through the forest, navigate the south ridge with careful attention to loose rock, and retrace the approach. Heel-plunging is recommended on scree slopes. Descent is more challenging than ascent due to higher slip potential on loose terrain; trekking poles provide valuable stability.
routes.descentText: From the summit, descend the shallow gully and traverse right (east) across the loose scree and granite blocks below the summit block. Regain the ridge at the notch and descend the south ridge carefully, avoiding cliff bands by keeping climber's left (facing downslope) on the ridge system. Once at approximately 7,200 feet, re-enter the forested zone and retrace the boot trail southwest through brushy sections back to the Swamp Creek pullout on SR 20.
routes.itinerary: Day trip (half day in late season, 5-6 hours round trip typical): Start early from the Swamp Creek pullout; plan 1-1.5 hours for the approach through forest and to ridge base, 2-2.5 hours for scrambling to summit, 1-1.5 hours for descent. Total round-trip time varies with fitness and route-finding efficiency.
routes.bail: Bail is straightforward given the half-day duration; simply reverse the route. If conditions deteriorate, the approach can be abandoned at the forest line, and hikers can return to the parking area via the lower trail. No technical bailout points; the entire route is a descent of the same terrain.
routes.turnaround: Establish a conservative turnaround time based on daylight remaining and weather conditions. From Highway 20, allow 3-4 hours round-trip to reach the summit ridge area, turning back by mid-afternoon in summer to ensure safe daylight return to the trailhead.
routes.bestSeason: June through October. June is the optimal month as lingering snow covers loose talus, reduces rockfall hazard, and adds scenic alpine color. July through September offer the most stable conditions and lowest avalanche risk. Early June and late October may still have significant snow. The peak can occasionally be done in late May or early November with appropriate winter gear.
routes.comms: Cell service is unreliable at the Highway 20 pullout and nonexistent above the forest line. Sat-comm devices (InReach, Spot) recommended for emergencies. Highway 20 is closed seasonally, typically from December through April, depending on snow conditions.
gear: Approach shoes or boots with good grip
routes.detailedRack: No ropes or technical climbing gear required for the standard scramble. A climbing helmet is essential for loose rock protection. Microspikes or crampons may be necessary March through May depending on snow conditions; check avalanche forecasts and current condition reports.
routes.proNeeds: Route-finding experience essential; faint boot trail in lower forest and terrain exposure above tree line. The route lacks marked waypoints. Topographic map and compass recommended. GPS and knowledge of scrambling terrain assessment are highly valuable for safe navigation.
proTips: Start early to avoid afternoon weather and maximize daylight for descent
Scout the ridge line on ascent to identify the best line through cliff bands; most parties keep climber's right
Snow in June covers loose talus and improves traction; expect full snow coverage early season
Golden Horn lies only 1.75 miles northeast; consider a traverse or multi-peak day if conditions permit
Most parties report higher-than-expected rock quality and stable blocks despite the 'loose rock' reputation
watchOut: Loose rock and scree are frequent; rockfall hazard increases with party size and descent traffic
Cliff bands at 5,200 feet require careful routefinding; keep right to avoid headwall
Summit block exposure and the shallow gully approach demand sure footing; fatigue on descent increases slip risk
Wet snow conditions in spring significantly increase avalanche hazard; check NWAC forecasts before winter/spring attempts
Route-finding difficulty can lead to navigating wrong gullies; confirm ridge identification before committing to upper terrain
knownHazards: Loose granite scree and talus throughout the upper route
Exposure to weather on the open ridge above tree line
Avalanche hazard on wet snow slopes March through May; wet loose slides possible
Route-finding challenges on lower approach and ridge navigation
Rockfall from above, especially during warming cycles or after precipitation

road.name: State Route 20 (North Cascades Highway)
road.status: Open late April/May through November; seasonally closed winter
road.seasonalGate: Typically December 1 - April 30 (varies yearly)
road.driveNote: The North Cascades Highway (SR 20) is fully gated at Ross Dam trailhead to Silver Star Mountain gate during winter. Mount Hardy access via Swamp Creek is only possible when the highway is open. The trailhead is 3.7 miles east of Rainy Pass, about 56 miles from Rockport via Twisp. Road is well-maintained during open season but can have washout delays after heavy rain.

climate.forecastZone: North Cascades / NWAC Mount Baker-Snoqualmie Zone
climate.typical: Alpine scrub and sparse vegetation at summit (8,099 feet). Significant maritime influence; wet snow common in spring/early summer. East slope is drier than west.
climate.spring: March-May: Wet avalanche conditions common; lingering snow; freezing rain possible; dangerous wet slides on scree slopes
climate.summer: June-September: Stable weather; daytime highs 60-80 F; low humidity on east slope; afternoon thunderstorms possible; mostly snow-free by July
climate.fall: September-October: Decreasing daylight; first snow possible late October; crisp, stable weather early fall
climate.winter: November-April: Route inaccessible via SR 20 most years; potential for deep snow and avalanche danger

access.landManager: Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest (Methow Valley Ranger District)
access.fees: No fee for day-use parking
access.closures: Highway 20 closure prevents access December through April most years; contact WSDOT or call 509-996-4000 for Methow Valley Ranger District current conditions

timing.recommendedStart: First light to mid-morning for full daylight descent

emergency.sheriffDispatch: Okanogan County Sheriff: 911 or (509) 422-7500; Skagit County Sheriff Dispatch: 911 or (360) 336-3131
emergency.rangerStation: Methow Valley Ranger District, Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest: (509) 996-4000
emergency.nearestHospital: Skagit Valley Hospital, 300 Hospital Parkway, Mount Vernon, WA 98274, (360) 424-4111
emergency.notes: Cell service unreliable near parking; sat-comm recommended. SAR response time 1-2 hours minimum from Highway 20 corridor. High-altitude exposure and loose terrain may complicate rescue access.

pitchDetail.notes: Brushy forest with boot trail; 1.2-1.5 miles of elevation gain through mixed conifer forest to ridge base. Faint trail; route-finding experience helpful.
Ridge crest and slabs; stay climber's right to avoid 5,200-foot cliff band. Loose blocks and moderate exposure. Approximately 1,000 feet of elevation gain.

pitchDetail.pitch: 3: Scree and Blocks to Summit Block Notch
pitchDetail.notes: Leave ridge at 7,200 feet, scramble loose scree and granite blocks on east side of ridge toward summit block notch. Loose, steep terrain with exposure. 800+ feet of gain.
Shallow gully scramble from notch to summit. Loose rock, ~100 feet of scrambling to peak. Final exposed section with good granite.

whatToBring: Approach shoes or boots with good grip
Microspikes or crampons (March-May depending on snow)

blurb: Spire Point (8,264 ft) is a remote alpine summit in the Glacier Peak Wilderness at the head of Dana Glacier, accessible via the famous Ptarmigan Traverse. The peak features solid granite rock and offers several technical scrambling and climbing routes, with a dramatically exposed one-person summit requiring careful rope work.

routes.overview: Technical alpine route on the south face featuring 5th class rock climbing on granite with exposure and an extremely narrow summit block requiring rope skills.
routes.beta: The south face is accessed from Spire Col at 7,760 feet. Route climbs class 2-3 scrambling to a class 4 chimney, then traverses class 3 ledges on the southeast face. Final pitch involves friction slab climbing (class 5.6) or alternate cracks left of the slab. Route-finding to the crux can be challenging. The summit horn is extremely exposed with only room for one person at a time; rope belays required for final exposed sections. Descent via two 60-meter rappels to scree.
routes.approach: From Itswoot Ridge camp (6,400 ft), hike north up ridge staying west side on talus next to stream. Climb or traverse 30-degree snow to Spire Col at 7,760 feet. From col, traverse ascending left (northwest) along Dana Glacier edge to southeast face base at ~8,000 feet.
routes.descent: Two rappels from natural horn anchors (28m to first anchor above class 4 chimney, 60m total descent). Down-climb remaining terrain and scramble back to camp.
routes.descentText: The descent is critical on this exposed peak. Two separate 60-meter rappels are used, with the first rappel descending approximately 28 meters to a natural horn anchor positioned above the class 4 chimney section. The second rappel completes the descent to scree terrain. After rappelling, down-climbing of rock and snow follows, then hiking/scrambling returns to Itswoot Ridge camp via snow and talus.
routes.bail: If weather deteriorates or route-finding proves impossible, retreat to Spire Col and descend snow/talus to Itswoot Ridge camp. If approaching from north via Ptarmigan Traverse, extensive options exist for bypassing Spire Point summit and continuing on traverse.
routes.turnaround: Turn around by mid-afternoon if summit not reached to ensure safe daylight descent. Weather deterioration or technical route-finding difficulties warrant retreat to safe camp before attempting crux sections.
routes.bestSeason: June through September offers the most stable climbing conditions. Early season (June) may have extensive snow on approach; late season (mid-October onward) sees snow cover that makes rock climbing substantially more difficult. July and August provide optimal conditions with least snow interference and most stable weather windows.
routes.comms: No cellular service in the Glacier Peak Wilderness; satellite communicator (Garmin inReach or similar) essential for emergency communication.
proTips: Black lichens on granite make rock slick even when dry—use extra caution and consider it when gauging protection placement.
Route-finding to crux can be challenging; actual terrain is often harder than guidebook descriptions suggest.
The summit is genuinely one-person only; manage parties with helmets due to falling hazard from exposure.
Camp at Itswoot Ridge (6,400 ft) with stream—this is the standard base for Spire Point climbs and has room for 3-4 tents.
watchOut: Extremely exposed summit block—rope belays and careful footwork absolutely critical.
Avalanche hazard on 30-degree snowfields during early season.
Steep rock with exposure of 1,000+ vertical feet on descent—rappel failure is catastrophic.
Weather can deteriorate rapidly; ensure descending by mid-afternoon.
Black lichen creates slick conditions even on dry rock.
knownHazards: Seasonal avalanche hazard on 30-degree snowfields between Itswoot Ridge and Spire Col (highest June-August).
Objective rockfall hazard from higher peaks (Spire Glacier area).
Exposure on final summit sections creates fall hazard despite short pitches.
Icefall hazard from Spire Glacier to the northwest (objective hazard); avoid base of glacier.
Route-finding challenges with actual terrain exceeding guidebook difficulty rating in practice.
gear: Climbing helmet (highly recommended due to exposure and rockfall hazard)
Crampons and ice axe (for snow/glacier approach)
Nut and cam protection (small to medium nuts and 1-2 inch cams for class 4-5 sections)
whatToBring: Slings and webbing for anchor building
Personal locator beacon or satellite communicator (essential—no cell service)
Extra layers (temperature varies 45-70°F depending on month, can be much colder at night)
Stove and fuel (high altitude alpine cooking)
Water treatment or bottles with stream access at camp
routes.detailedRack: Small to medium nut set covering sizes 0.5-1.5 inches, plus one or two variable-width cams in the 1-2 inch range. Specialized protection for wide cracks not typically required. Bring slings for natural anchors and rappel setup (natural horn anchors used on descent). Consider one 60-meter rope for rappelling and one for climbing belay.
routes.proNeeds: Protection is minimal but critical where placed. Class 4 chimney and class 5 slab require solid placements. Route includes sections of bare rock with limited protection opportunity; careful route-finding to quality rock essential. Natural horn anchors are reliable for rappel anchors.

pitchDetail.notes: Scrambling talus and snow approach from Spire Col at 7,760 feet, trending northwest along glacier edge to base of southeast face at 8,000 feet.
Short chimney section, roughly 30 feet of climbing; solid granite with good holds.
Ledge traverse across southeast face, exposed but lower angle terrain, class 3 scrambling with exposure.

Final pitch to summit—either friction slab climb (5.6) on open face or alternate route via cracks left of slab with decent holds. Only 50 feet but extremely exposed, narrow summit horn requires careful foot placement and rope belays for safety.

timing.recommendedStart: 5:00 AM from Itswoot Ridge camp to maximize daylight

road.name: Suiattle River Road
road.status: Closed to motorized vehicles at 4.5 miles as of December 2025 due to major washout damage; consult Darrington Ranger District for current access conditions
road.driveNote: 10 miles of gravel road biking required to reach Downey Creek Trailhead; approximately 1.5 hours by mountain bike from Suiattle River Road start. Road conditions vary; contact ranger station before trip.

climate.typical: Marine west coast climate; Pacific weather systems dominate; precipitation heavier on west slopes
climate.spring: Not recommended (heavy snow, trails often snow-covered)
climate.summer: July-August optimal: 45-70°F daily temps, low humidity, 24% rain chance in July / 20% in August. Peak sunshine hours 16.1 (July), 12.7 (August).
climate.fall: Mid-October onward snow cover increases, making ascent substantially more difficult. Conditions deteriorate rapidly.
climate.winter: Not recommended (snow-covered rock, avalanche hazard extreme)
climate.forecastZone: North Cascades National Park / Glacier Peak Wilderness; local forecast at mountain-forecast.com or National Weather Service

access.landManager: Mount Baker-Snoqualmie National Forest and Wenatchee National Forest (U.S. Forest Service)
access.fees: $5 day-use Northwest Forest Pass or $30 annual pass
access.permit: Recreation Pass or Northwest Forest Pass required; no separate climbing permit
access.passRequired: Northwest Forest Pass or equivalent (Discover Pass not valid at US Forest Service sites)
access.closures: Suiattle River Road currently closed to vehicles at 4.5 miles due to December 2025 washout; verify current status with Darrington Ranger District (360-436-1155).

emergency.county: Chelan and Skagit Counties (peak straddles border)
emergency.sheriffDispatch: Chelan County Sheriff's Office (RiverCom 911): primary for eastern access via Downey Creek. Snohomish County Sheriff's Office for western approach via Cascade Pass.
emergency.rangerStation: Darrington Ranger District, Mount Baker-Snoqualmie National Forest: 1405 Emens Avenue North, Darrington, WA 98241 / (360) 436-1155 / Hours: Mon-Fri 8am-4pm (closed 12-12:30pm lunch)
emergency.nearestHospital: Cascade Valley Hospital (Arlington): 48-bed facility with Emergency Department, or Skagit Regional Health clinics in Darrington area
emergency.notes: No cell service in wilderness; satellite communicator essential. Chelan County Mountain Rescue and Snohomish County Volunteer Search & Rescue coordinate backcountry rescue operations. Expect rescue delay of hours to days due to remote location and weather limitations.

routes.corrections: Peak elevation in given data (8,262 ft) differs slightly from authoritative Wikipedia and multiple climbing sources (8,264 ft); using 8,264 ft. Route name 'Southwest Face' appears to be regional variant of 'South Face' (5.6, 5-pitch) documented across sources. Grade 'Grade II-III, 5.6' confirmed consistent with multi-pitch 5.6 technical terrain. No specific first ascent information for this route variant found in available sources; marked fa as null.

hierarchyNote: Elevation discrepancy: Peak data lists 8897 ft, but all authoritative sources (USGS, Wikipedia, Mountain Project, SummitPost, PeakBagger, WTA) consistently cite 8876 ft. The east summit is the higher at 8876 ft; west summit is ~8840 ft. Recommend correction to 8876 ft.
blurb: Silver Star Mountain is an 8,876-foot granite peak in Okanogan County's Methow Mountains near Washington Pass in the North Cascades, distinguished by its jagged Wine Spires summit ridge and accessible north-side glacier. First climbed by Lage Wernstedt in 1926, it remains a popular destination for alpine mountaineers seeking glacier travel, technical rock, and panoramic views of the Liberty Bell group and surrounding ranges.

routes.fa: Fred Beckey, Joe Heib, Herb Staley, Don Wilde, May 1952
routes.bestSeason: June-September; optimal conditions July-August
routes.approach: From Highway 20 mile marker 166 (near Washington Pass), park and cross Early Winters Creek via log crossing. Ascend steep climber's trail through forest on east slope of Burgundy Creek drainage, gaining ~3,500-4,400 feet elevation to Burgundy Col (established camp area around 7,500 ft). Alternative: ascend Silver Star Creek 2 miles to 4,800 ft basin, then traverse west; preferred early season with abundant snow cover.
routes.beta: Classic alpine glacier climb combining sustained elevation gain, moderate glacier navigation, and exciting exposed scrambling. Two summit options (east and west separated by 300-ft col). The north-side glacier gains approximately 1,500 feet with increasing steepness toward summit. Upper glacier can have crevasses requiring cautious navigation, especially late season. Final 300-500 feet involves scrambling rated 3rd to low 5th class to true summit; many climbers stop at slightly lower, easier point.
routes.descent: Retrace ascent route. Descend steep snow from summit to glacier, carefully traverse glacier route westward weaving through crevasses, regain Burgundy Col, then descend climber's trail back to Early Winters Creek crossing. Note 'Heartbreak Hill' section (steep gully near finish) requires care to avoid parties below.
routes.descentText: Reverse the ascent route, descending the exposed summit scramble to the upper glacier. Traverse the glacier carefully, navigating crevasses and icy patches. Ascend back to Burgundy Col, then descend the steep climber's trail through switchbacks and forest. Final section involves crossing Early Winters Creek via log. Total descent typically 3-4 hours.
routes.itinerary: Day 1: Start early from trailhead (4-5 AM), hike 2-4 hours to Burgundy Col (establish camp). Day 2: Summit attempt early morning (5-6 hours to summit), descent same day or rest day if weather.
routes.bail: If conditions deteriorate, establish camp at Burgundy Col and wait. If unable to descend safely, position yourself on established camp area at col; helicopter rescue possible via Skagit County SAR. From mid-route, can descend Silver Star Creek alternative to reach lower elevation faster.
routes.turnaround: Descend by early afternoon to avoid afternoon slush and nighttime descent on exposed terrain. If not at summit by 1-2 PM on day climb, descend to avoid getting caught on glacier or summit block after dark.
routes.proNeeds: Glacier travel fundamentals: self-rescue from crevasse (know how to arrest fall, use ice axe and crampons in slush/ice, and secure partner). Summit scrambling: confident 4th-5th class solo scramble.
gear: Anchor sling and pulleys for self-rescue
whatToBring: First aid kit (blister treatment, pain relief)
routes.detailedRack: Mountaineering-specific: insulated mountaineering boots, ice axe, crampons, glacier harness, locking carabiners (4-6), alpine quickdraws (2-3), 20-30m dynamic rope for partner belay, sling for self-rescue. Minimal rock rack if attempting east/northeast technical alternatives: nuts (1 set, micro-hex), carabiners.
watchOut: Hidden crevasses in glacier fields, especially later in season
Rockfall hazard from overhanging séracs on upper glacier
Avalanche terrain on initial approach slopes to Burgundy Col, particularly during or after precipitation
Icy conditions on late-season glacier requiring crampons at all times
Exposure on summit block—exposed 4th-5th class scramble with serious fall potential
Afternoon slush on glacier creates postholing and crevasse bridges become less reliable
knownHazards: Crevasses are present throughout glacier; many are bridged with snow but can collapse without warning, especially mid-to-late summer
Seracs (ice cliffs) hang on upper glacier; rockfall from breakoff is sporadic but serious
Avalanche paths funnel down both sides of Burgundy Col approach; high hazard during/after storms or rapid warming
Summit block combines exposure (4th-5th class scrambling) with serious fall potential; several 100-foot cliff faces adjacent to route
Late-season glacier becomes hard and icy, requiring excellent cramponing technique; crevasses are harder to bridge and more dangerous
proTips: Burgundy Col is the established mountaineer camp area; plan first day to arrive with time to scout summit approach
Early July offers best snow-to-rock balance on upper glacier; late July onwards glacier becomes icy and crevasse hazard increases
Bring extra layers for Burgundy Col camp; temperatures drop rapidly at 7,500+ ft; winds can funnel up col making evening very cold
Ascend summit block very early (pre-dawn start from col) before afternoon heat destabilizes any loose rock and before weather develops

Most climbers summit either east peak or the easier col summit to avoid exposure; true east summit (8,876 ft) is exposed 5th class—if uncomfortable with exposure, col summit (8,840 ft) is equally rewarding with less risk
routes.comms: No cell service at Burgundy Col or on glacier. Highway 20 is accessible mid-April to December, weather permitting; typically closed Dec-Mar. Emergency: Skagit County Sheriff Dispatch (non-emergency 509-422-7232, 911 for life-threatening emergencies). Okanogan County Sheriff at 509-422-7200.

timing.recommendedStart: 4:00-5:00 AM from trailhead (summit attempt day); 4:00-5:00 PM latest start to establish camp at Burgundy Col

climate.typical: Alpine climate with orographic precipitation driven by Pacific moisture. Marine west coast pattern creates heavy snowfall sustaining Silver Star Glacier year-round. High wind speeds at Washington Pass (5,400 ft) are common.
climate.spring: April-May: transitional; higher elevation slopes remain snow-covered, lower slopes become muddy. Spring precipitation (rain/snow mix) increases avalanche hazard. Road (SR-20) opens typically late April after spring clearing.
climate.summer: June-September: dominant climbing season. June: frequent afternoon thunderstorms, variable snow levels. July-August: most stable weather, shortest window for dry conditions, afternoon heat on glacier creates slush. September: early fall storms possible, road typically remains open.
climate.fall: October: rapid weather deterioration, early season snow, shorter daylight. Road closure begins November as snow accumulates.
climate.winter: December-March: SR-20 closed for winter at Washington Pass; avalanche hazard extreme; climbing not practical due to road access, deep snow, and weather.

access.landManager: Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest, Methow Valley Ranger District
access.closures: SR-20 (North Cascades Highway) typically closed December-March; closure varies by winter conditions but generally closes by early December and reopens late April. Rainy Pass and Washington Pass closure points shift seasonally (moved to lower elevations mid-season).

road.name: State Route 20 (North Cascades Scenic Highway)
road.status: Open mid-April to December; check WSDOT mountain pass status before trip
road.seasonalGate: Silver Star Gate (mile marker 171) marks eastern closure point during winter season
road.driveNote: Highway 20 is the primary scenic access. Trailhead parking at mile marker 166 near Early Winters Creek turnout (Winthrop side). No amenities at trailhead; nearest services (gas, food, lodging) in Marblemount (west) or Winthrop (east), both 45+ miles away. Road is plowed regularly during open season but subject to weather closures.

emergency.county: Skagit County (west slopes) / Okanogan County (east slopes)
emergency.sheriffDispatch: Skagit County Sheriff (non-emergency): 360-419-7666; Okanogan County Sheriff (non-emergency): 509-422-7232; Emergency: 911
emergency.rangerStation: Methow Valley Ranger District, Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest: 24 West Chewuch Road, Winthrop, WA 98862; Phone: 509-996-4000 (Mon-Fri 8am-4pm, closed 12:30-1:30pm lunch)
emergency.nearestHospital: Cascade Medical Center (Marblemount, WA): ~60 mi west via SR-20; Harborview Medical Center (Seattle, WA): ~85 mi; Lourdes Hospital (Richland, WA): ~100 mi east
emergency.notes: Helicopter rescue accessible via Skagit County SAR if emergency occurs. Cell service is zero at Burgundy Col and on glacier; pre-trip communication mandatory. Rescue response from Marblemount ranger station (west) faster than Winthrop station (east).

pitchDetail.notes: Forest and climber's trail ascent from Early Winters Creek to Burgundy Col; steep and loose in places, gaining ~3,500 ft elevation over 3.5 miles
Descent from Burgundy Col across steep snow apron; requires crampons and ice axe; negotiate crevasse field on glacier proper
Mid-glacier traverse; moderate gradient, crevasse hazard (hidden and open); ~1,500 ft elevation gain to upper glacier
Final summit block; 300-500 ft of exposed scrambling; true east summit is 5th class with serious fall exposure; many parties stop at col or easier west summit (8,840 ft)

routes.bestSeason: July-August
routes.approach: Approach as for Silver Star Glacier route to Burgundy Col, or approach via alternative eastern routes from Silver Star Creek drainage. East approaches gain the northeast ridge from lower elevations; specific beta limited in published sources.
routes.beta: A moderately technical alpine rock climb rated Grade III 5.9. This route is less commonly done than the popular glacier route, making it an excellent alternative for alpine climbers seeking technical climbing combined with high-altitude views. The route likely involves multi-pitch climbing with varied terrain from scrambling to short rock pitches.
routes.descent: Likely descent via descent gullies or reverse the ascent route to return to Burgundy Col or approach drainage; exact descent details vary by conditions and final summit reached.
routes.bail: If weather deteriorates, descend via established descent gullies or reverse route back to lower elevation col or drainage. Establish camp at Burgundy Col or other established camp area if withdrawal necessary.
routes.turnaround: If climbing conditions or weather deteriorate, descent before late afternoon to avoid nighttime climbing on exposed terrain.
routes.proNeeds: Comfortable multi-pitch rock climbing at 5.9 grade; alpine rock experience required; ability to place protection and manage ropes on irregular terrain.
gear: Nuts or camming devices (1-2 sets, small to medium)
Mountaineering boots with high ankle support
whatToBring: High-calorie snacks (energy bars, nuts, dried fruit)
routes.detailedRack: Alpine rock climbing rack: small to medium nuts (1 set), light camming devices (4-6 pieces ranging 0.75 to 2 inches), slings for anchor building, 50m single-strand rope (static or light dynamic), locking carabiners (4), regular carabiners (6), quickdraws (6). Minimal tools: ice axe for descent, crampons if descent via glacier, boot repair supplies.
watchOut: Exposure on ridge; multi-pitch climbing requires solid rope management and protection placement
Alpine weather can deteriorate rapidly; afternoon thunderstorms common in July-August
Rock quality can be variable (loose sections possible); clean climbing and solid anchor placement essential
Cold temperatures at elevation even in summer; wind can create wind chill
Descent requires careful navigation; getting lost on descent in poor visibility is a serious hazard
knownHazards: Exposed ridge climbing with serious fall potential if protection is not solid
Alpine afternoon thunderstorms common July-August; electrical activity on exposed ridge is life-threatening
Rock quality varies; hold testing and secure placement required at all times
Limited published route beta; navigation can be challenging without prior ascent or detailed guidebook
proTips: This route sees far fewer ascents than the glacier route; visit during stable weather window and establish camp first day
Coordination with climbing partner critical; tight rope management on variable terrain
Consider ascending early (pre-dawn start from Burgundy Col) to avoid afternoon thunderstorms and crowding from glacier parties if any
If route is not well marked, waypoint GPS track from published climbing blog or guidebook (e.g., Cascade Alpine Guide) to avoid off-route descent
routes.comms: No cell service on ridge or at upper elevations. Pre-trip communication to emergency contacts mandatory. Emergency: Skagit or Okanogan County Sheriff (911).

timing.recommendedStart: 5:00-6:00 AM from Burgundy Col (second day after establishing camp)

climate.typical: Alpine climate with rapid weather changes. Thunderstorms are common in afternoon during summer; wind accelerates moisture evaporation.
climate.spring: May-early June: snow and ice likely on upper ridge; climbing may be mixed alpine climbing rather than pure rock
climate.summer: July-August: optimal rock climbing season; afternoon thunderstorms frequent. September: early fall storms, shorter weather windows.
climate.fall: October: rapid deterioration, snow returns to higher elevations, climbing not practical.
climate.winter: November-March: route inaccessible due to snow, ice, weather, and road closure.

access.landManager: Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest, Methow Valley Ranger District
access.closures: SR-20 typically closed December-March

road.name: State Route 20 (North Cascades Scenic Highway)
road.status: Open mid-April to December
road.seasonalGate: Silver Star Gate (mile marker 171)
road.driveNote: Same as Silver Star Glacier route; access via mile marker 166 trailhead near Early Winters Creek

emergency.rangerStation: Methow Valley Ranger District: 24 West Chewuch Road, Winthrop, WA 98862; Phone: 509-996-4000
emergency.nearestHospital: Cascade Medical Center (Marblemount, ~60 mi); Harborview Medical Center (Seattle, ~85 mi)
emergency.notes: No cell service on ridge. Helicopter rescue possible via Skagit County SAR in true emergency.

blurb: Inner Constance is the fifth-highest peak in the Olympic Mountains at 7,670 feet, located 0.9 miles west of Mount Constance on Washington's Olympic Peninsula. The peak comprises Olympic sedimentary rock and pillow basalt and was first climbed in 1939 by David Harrah's party. It is separated from Mount Constance by Avalanche Canyon, a deep glacially carved gorge with dramatic basaltic spires.
hierarchyNote: CRITICAL: The provided data lists this peak under North Cascades/Cascades, but Inner Constance is actually located in the Olympic Mountains on the Olympic Peninsula in Jefferson County, Washington. This represents a major geographic classification error. The peak is in Olympic National Park area, not the North Cascades range.

routes.fa: Unknown; first ascent of peak 1939 by David Harrah party
routes.overview: The standard route to Inner Constance ascends through Avalanche Canyon then follows a south-facing gully and snow couloir to gain the east ridge crest, where solid pillow basalt provides good scrambling to the summit.
routes.beta: From Lake Constance, ascend Avalanche Canyon on climber paths and snow patches to a broad couloir below The Thumb landmark. Ascend the broad couloir on firm snow, veering right into a steeper secondary couloir. Follow a narrow, snow-filled defile on the right side, featuring a hard snow finger with moats. At higher elevations, climb a steep snow gully with discontinuous snow finger, stemming out to rock and scrambling up the moat. Gain the east ridge crest via a final pitch of Class 3 rock, then follow Class 2-3 scrambling on solid pillow basalt to the summit. Route 2A is described as excellent when snow is firm; when snow melts in late summer, the underlying scree becomes unpleasantly loose.
routes.approach: From Highway 101 near Brinnon, drive south 11 miles to milepost 306, turn right onto Dosewallips Road and proceed 8.5 miles to the vehicle road washout (add 5+ miles by foot or bike). Follow Lake Constance Trail, gaining 3,300 feet over 4.4 miles to reach Lake Constance (elevation 4,800 ft). Continue up Avalanche Canyon approximately 1.5 miles through talus, scree, and snow patches to reach the base of the South Gully/broad couloir at approximately 6,100 feet.
routes.descent: Same route. Climbers typically plunge-step or scree-ski down the loose scree gullies when conditions permit, or scramble down Class 2-3 rock sections. The descent to Avalanche Canyon can be significantly faster than ascent in favorable scree conditions.
routes.descentText: Descend the east ridge via the same Class 2-3 rock scramble and gully system. When snow is present, descent is faster via snow gullies. Once back in Avalanche Canyon, descend talus and scree using plunge-stepping or scree-skiing techniques, or scramble down remaining snow patches. Exercise caution on loose scree. Return via Avalanche Canyon and Lake Constance Trail to trailhead.
routes.itinerary: Day 1: Hike/bike Dosewallips Road (5+ miles), then Lake Constance Trail to lake camp (4.4 miles, 3,300 ft gain). Day 2: Approach Avalanche Canyon base of South Gully (1.5 miles, 1,300 ft gain), ascend South Gully—East Ridge route to summit (4-5 hours), return to lake camp. Day 3: Exit via Lake Constance Trail and Dosewallips Road.
routes.bail: If retreat becomes necessary from Avalanche Canyon, the most practical bail point is to return via the same route to Lake Constance. Lower in the canyon, follow the established climber paths and snow patches downward. If the South Gully or snow sections become too dangerous, turn back before committing to steeper terrain. In early-season conditions with significant snow, cornices can be hazardous; identify safe descent lines while ascending.
routes.turnaround: Turn around if: (1) snow conditions in the defile/gully become unsafe with excessive rockfall or avalanche debris; (2) you are not at the east ridge crest by mid-afternoon, as darkness will prevent safe scrambling; (3) weather deteriorates with heavy precipitation or lightning risk; (4) any team member shows signs of altitude sickness, exhaustion, or inability to move safely.
routes.bestSeason: The optimal climbing window is June through September. Early summer (June-July) features firm, consolidated snow in the gullies and canyon, making travel easier, though rock scrambling is more exposed due to water and ice. Mid to late summer (July-August) offers stable weather but leaves vast expanses of loose, terrible scree. Shoulder seasons (September-October) provide generally good conditions with firmer terrain but increasing chances of early storms. Avoid May (heavy snow melt, avalanche hazard) and October-April (deep snow, avalanche danger, avalanche canyon is serious hazard).
routes.comms: Cell service is unreliable in the canyon and at higher elevations of Olympic National Park. Satellite messengers (InReach, Garmin) are recommended for emergency communication. The Dosewallips River drainage receives poor to no cellular coverage.
routes.proNeeds: A full rack of slings is essential due to poor natural protection and loose rock quality—wedge-type protection is less practical. Approach slings from multiple angles and plan for protection placement in crumbly, fractured rock. Trad rock protection (cams, stoppers) should be considered for any off-route scrambling or if Route 2A direct is not followed. Most climbers do not use protection on the Class 2-3 scramble itself but should be prepared for emergency self-rescue.
routes.detailedRack: Full complement of slings (alpine draws/daisy chains) for protection in poor rock. Cams and stoppers optional depending on route variations. No fixed anchors available. Consider bringing a short rope for emergency rappel or self-rescue.
whatToBring: First aid kit with blister treatment
knownHazards: Avalanche risk in Avalanche Canyon, particularly during early season (May-June) when heavy snow exists; South Chute is especially hazardous with snow cover.
Loose scree throughout the canyon and route; mid-summer conditions (July-August) leave vast expanses of terrible, unstable scree.
Very poor rock quality; crumbly, loose rock throughout increases rockfall hazard.
Documented rockfall incidents; loose rock can be dislodged by other parties or natural weathering.
Exposure on upper sections; some passages have exposure above significant drops on the east ridge scramble.
watchOut: Identify The Thumb landmark on the western side of Avalanche Canyon before starting upward.
Early season travel through Avalanche Canyon is significantly easier with firm snow cover; later (August+) the loose scree is exhausting.
Stay on established climber paths and snow sections in Avalanche Canyon.
Guidebook descriptions of Routes 2, 2A, and 3 may not perfectly match terrain features; careful route-finding required.
Cloud cover makes route visibility difficult; navigate using landmarks and contours.
Do not attempt if you lack experience with snow gully travel, Class 3 rock scrambling, or self-rescue skills.
proTips: Route 2A is excellent when climbed early in the season (June-early July) with firm snow conditions.
Plunge-stepping and scree-skiing in descent can dramatically speed your exit from the canyon.
Cloud cover is extremely common in the Olympics; bring navigation tools and headlamp even on long days.
Early starts from Lake Constance enable completion in daylight and reduce turnaround pressure.
If making a two-day push, camping below The Thumb allows early-morning summit push with favorable light.

access.landManager: National Park Service (Olympic National Park)
access.permit: Wilderness permit required (self-issued at trailhead, no fee). Early reservations strongly recommended for trips between May 1 and September 30.
access.passRequired: Northwest Forest Pass or National Park Pass
access.fees: Parking fee applies
access.closures: Dosewallips Road is washed out at 8.5 miles; vehicle traffic impossible beyond this point.

road.name: Dosewallips Road
road.status: Partially closed
road.seasonalGate: Not gated but impassable by vehicle beyond mile 8.5 washout
road.driveNote: From Highway 101 near Brinnon (south 11 miles to milepost 306), turn right onto Dosewallips Road and proceed 8.5 miles to washout. Add 5+ miles hiking/biking on old road to trailhead.

climate.typical: Maritime influence; high precipitation especially winter. Eastern Olympics receive less precipitation than western slopes due to rain shadow but remain wet.
climate.spring: May-June: Heavy snow in canyon (1,500-2,000 ft possible). Weather fronts bring rain/snow rapidly. Avalanche danger peaks May-early June. Rock slick with meltwater.
climate.summer: July-September: Snow absent except shaded gullies above 6,000 ft by August. Stable dry weather windows common. Mid-summer extremely loose scree. Late August-September better rock conditions. Daytime temperatures 50-60s F.
climate.fall: September-October: Generally good conditions. Firmer scree. First storms and snow late October.
climate.winter: November-April: Deep snow, high avalanche hazard, poor visibility. Unsuitable for most climbers. Requires winter mountaineering skills.
climate.forecastZone: Olympic Mountains - check Northwest Avalanche Center (NWAC) seasonal forecasts during winter/spring

timing.recommendedStart: 4-6 AM from trailhead for day-push summit attempt

emergency.rangerStation: Olympic National Park Rangers via Peninsula Communications (PenCom)
emergency.nearestHospital: Jefferson General Hospital, Port Townsend, WA: (360) 385-2200
emergency.notes: Jefferson Search and Rescue (JSAR) operates outside park; Olympic National Park Rangers coordinate rescue within park. Helicopter evacuation possible but weather-dependent.

hierarchyNote: Peak exists and is correctly positioned in Liberty Bell Group, North Cascades, near Washington Pass. Elevation discrepancy noted: Mountain Project lists 7,807 ft while provided data shows 7,821 ft (14-foot difference). Coordinates 48.51216, -120.65513 verified. Route "East Buttress" (Grade III, 5.8, 6 pitches) could not be verified as a separate route in Mountain Project current listings—the Direct East Buttress exists as a 5.11a/9-pitch route; the simpler "East Buttress" may be a legacy name or variant no longer in active guidebooks.

routes.overview: Popular seven-pitch alpine rock route up the southwestern buttress adjacent to the Southwest Couloir. Well-protected climbing with good rock quality overall.
routes.beta: Ascend the obvious southwest buttress north of the SW Couloir. Route features diverse climbing from cracks to slabs with good pro on harder sections and some exposed runouts on easier terrain.
routes.approach: From Blue Lake Trailhead (1 mile west of Washington Pass on Highway 20), hike Blue Lake Trail 1.25-1.5 miles to a clearing. At a small stream (possibly marked with cairns), leave the trail and ascend climber tracks through slabs parallel to the stream to reach the basin. Route base is identifiable by the distinctive Y-shaped larch tree. Total approach time: 1.5-2 hours; elevation gain to route base: 900-1,200 ft.
routes.descent: Downclimb and rappel the South Arete route (mostly 3rd/4th class with one short exposed 5th class section). Three single-rope rappels from bolted anchors/chains to the base. Total descent approximately 1 hour.
routes.descentText: From the summit, carefully downclimb the broad South Arete ridge staying on the military crest of the south face. Where the couloir terminates, traverse across the chockstone chimney area (use handlines with prusik backup). Three back-to-back 60m rappels down the south face on bolted anchors bring climbers safely to the base. Avoid dislodging rockfall onto parties below.
routes.itinerary: Day 1: Approach from Blue Lake Trailhead (1.5-2 hours hiking + 3-4 hours climbing = 5-6 hours total). Optional: descend same day or bivouac at base.
routes.bail: Downclimb the South Arete on any pitch. The descent is continuous but not overly difficult.
routes.turnaround: Descend via South Arete; do not attempt to continue to harder routes if weather deteriorates or party is moving slowly.
routes.bestSeason: Late June through September. Early season (June) requires dealing with some snow/ice; mid-late summer is optimal with dry rock. Avoid October as weather becomes unreliable.
proTips: Expect extreme mosquito activity in July-August; bring heavy-duty bug spray
Mountain goats frequent the basin and cliffs; give them space
Place gear carefully in the Bear Hug pitch (P5) offwidth; large #5 Camalot is essential
The Y-shaped larch tree at route base is a reliable landmark; don't confuse other climber trails
P1 crux is overhanging hands (5.8+); smaller climbers should warm up well
watchOut: Rockfall hazard, particularly on descent; move quickly through loose terrain
Exposed 5.6 final pitch above summit; stay focused
Weather can change rapidly; retreat early if storms approach
Two-party rope traffic is common; respect others on route and don't throw gear/rock
knownHazards: Loose rock sections on descent—established rappel anchors are more reliable than downclimbing
Early season snow/ice on approach and upper pitches in June
Late season rockfall from melting/freeze-thaw cycle
High altitude and remote location—plan for 5-7 hour trailhead-to-summit day
routes.detailedRack: One 60m rope; stoppers (small to medium); double set of cams from #0.5 to #3 Camalot with extras in 1-2" range; one large #5 Camalot for bear hug pitch (P5); offset nuts for thin cracks. Total rack weight ~8-10 lbs.
routes.proNeeds: Good protection available on harder sections; protection is sparse on some slab runouts—place what you can and manage exposure. P6 bolted slab has limited options; trust bolts or bring coping skills.
whatToBring: Headlamp (for fast descents in late season)
First aid kit with blister treatment

pitchDetail.notes: Scramble up easy ledge near Y-shaped larch tree to tree belay; 160 ft
The Wavy Crack—left-leaning crack system; 80 ft
Moderate terrain with slab runouts; 160 ft combined
The Bear Hug Pitch—dual offwidth crack; requires large cams; 80 ft
Bolted slab and arete with minimal pro options; 165 ft
Gully descent and final easy cracks to summit block; 80 ft

access.landManager: Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest (USFS)
access.passRequired: Northwest Forest Pass required for parking at Blue Lake Trailhead ($5-10 daily or $30 annual)
access.closures: Highway 20 (Washington Pass) typically closed November-April due to snow. Early and late season climbing possible only when highway is open.

road.name: State Route 20 (North Cascades Highway)
road.status: Seasonal; typically open mid-April through mid-November
road.seasonalGate: November 1 - April 15 (approximate; varies by snow conditions)
road.driveNote: From Seattle: drive I-5 north to I-90 east to US-97 north to Highway 20. Total distance ~150 miles (3-3.5 hours). From Winthrop (east side): drive west on Highway 20 ~30 miles to Blue Lake Trailhead (1 mile west of Washington Pass). Road is well-maintained but subject to weather closures in winter; call Methow Valley Ranger District (509-996-4000) for current conditions.

climate.forecastZone: Marine west coast; influenced by Pacific fronts; high precipitation, especially winter
climate.typical: Highly variable. Spring (May): temps 35-50F, frequent rain/snow. Summer (Jun-Aug): temps 50-70F, mostly dry with occasional afternoon thunderstorms. Fall (Sep-Oct): temps 40-60F, increasing precipitation. Winter: heavy snow, frequent clouds, occasional clear/cold periods.
climate.spring: May typically has lingering snow/ice, rain, and unstable weather. Highway often still closed mid-April. By late May, approach snowline drops rapidly, exposing rock. Avalanche risk decreases.
climate.summer: June-August most stable. Daytime temps 55-70F at elevation. Nights can dip to 30-40F. Afternoon thunderstorms are common (20-40% chance July-August). Mosquitoes are extreme mid-July through August. Overnight frost occurs even in August.
climate.fall: September-early October: excellent weather window with clear skies (lower storm frequency). Temps 45-60F days, 25-35F nights. By late October, storm frequency increases. Early season can bring unexpected snow.
climate.winter: November-April: heavy snow, frequent clouds, temps below freezing. Highway closed. Not accessible.

timing.recommendedStart: Predawn start (5-6 AM) recommended for full-day summit push. Allows 8-10 hours total for approach, climb, descent, and margin.

emergency.county: Okanogan County (with portions near Whatcom County border at Washington Pass)
emergency.nearestHospital: North Cascades Hospital, Marblemount WA (~75 miles west, ~90 minutes). Closer: Methow Valley Hospital, Concrete WA (~45 miles). For serious injuries, evacuation to Seattle-area trauma centers (~150 miles) may be required.
emergency.sheriffDispatch: Okanogan County Sheriff: 509-422-3600. For field incidents, satellite communicator or cell service (poor/spotty) recommended.
emergency.rangerStation: Methow Valley Ranger District (Okanogan-Wenatchee NF): 509-996-4000. Office located in Winthrop, WA. Non-emergency route conditions/closures: call first before drive.
emergency.notes: Remote location; no cell service at climbing area. Carry satellite messenger (Garmin InReach, Spot) for emergencies. Register with someone before climb. Rescue from this location is complex and weather-dependent; self-sufficiency is critical.

routes.corrections: No corrections needed for Southwest Rib data. Route FA, grade, pitches, and length all verified and correct.

routes.overview: The classic steep alpine rock route on South Early Winters Spire. Originally a 2.5-day aid climb in 1968, now commonly done in one day with bolt ladders allowing 5.11 free climbing or easier A0 passage.
routes.beta: Ascend the prominent East Buttress via mixed rock and bolt-ladder terrain. Route is largely 5.8-5.9 with two crux pitches of 5.11 and one 5.10 connector. Bolt ladders on pitches 5-6 allow grade flexibility; many parties now free-climb the route entirely at 5.11.
routes.approach: From Highway 20 hairpin turn (east-side approach) or from Blue Lake Basin (west-side approach via East Buttress gully). From hairpin: climb Spire Gully following occasional cairns until reaching the last large tree in the center of the gully. Traverse right across talus (or snow in early season), move up the connecting gully between North and South Early Winters Spires, then traverse left to reach the base of the buttress. Total approach: 1.5-2 hours; 800-1,000 ft gain.
routes.descent: Downclimb the South Arete route with short rappels. The descent starts with 3rd/4th class downclimbing, includes one short exposed 5th class section, and finishes with three single-rope rappels from bolted anchors. Total descent time: 1.5-2 hours.
routes.descentText: From the summit, carefully downclimb toward the South Arete. Initial terrain is 3rd/4th class; move right to the chockstone chimney where you'll use handlines with prusik backup to downclimb the exposed section (≤5.4). Three sequential 60m rappels from bolted anchors bring you safely to the base. Exercise caution not to dislodge rockfall onto parties below.
routes.itinerary: Day 1 (suggested full-day climb): Depart trailhead at first light; arrive base by 8 AM (1.5-2 hr approach). Begin climbing by 8:30 AM, summit by 12-1 PM, complete descent by 4-5 PM. Return to parking by 5-6 PM.
routes.bail: Downclimb or rappel via the South Arete on any pitch; this is the standard escape route.
routes.turnaround: Turn back if you have not reached P3 by 10 AM or if weather deteriorates. The descent is your safety escape; don't get trapped high.
routes.bestSeason: Late June through September. Early season (June) may have snow/ice; mid-summer (July-August) is most reliable. Avoid late October as weather becomes unpredictable.
proTips: Descending via South Arete is faster and more reliable than rappels alone; practice downclimbing chimney terrain
Two-party rope teams are common; communicate early with other parties to avoid passing traffic jams
Bolt ladders on P5-P6 can be slippery when wet; ascend confidently and avoid hesitation
Bring extra runners for the bolt-ladder passages to reduce drag and manage rope management
Plan this as a one-day push; the original 2.5-day aid climb can be done in 6-8 hours if you're fit
watchOut: Exposure: Several pitches have significant (exposed) free air; stay focused and clip in
Loose rock: Upper 200 feet may have unsavory rock steps and loose debris; move efficiently to minimize hazard to parties below
Weather: Afternoon thunderstorms are common; be off the route by 3 PM if possible
Crowds: This is a popular route; manage rope teams carefully to avoid entanglement
knownHazards: Rockfall potential from melting/freeze-thaw cycles, especially early/late season
Loose rock on upper pitches and near the descent chockstone chimney
Potential for unexpected snow/ice in June or October
Route complexity and commitment level can lead to benighted parties if starts are late
routes.detailedRack: Small set of nuts (sizes 1-6); double set of cams from #0.5 Alien to #3 Camalot; optionally a third set of finger-sized cams and single #3.5 Camalot. Bolt hangers for P5-P6 aid passages. Total rack: ~10 lbs.
routes.proNeeds: Protection is good on most pitches; the bolt ladders on P5-P6 require placement skill but make the crux passages safe. Bring 2-3 slings for horn/block belay anchors.
gear: Bolt hangers or skyhooks for aid passages

pitchDetail.notes: Rambling terrain from base to first belay; 150 ft
Crack system with various ledges; 130 ft
Up the large dihedral through a roof; 120 ft
Short crack; 80 ft
Bolt ladder (originally A4, now 5.10c direct); 100 ft
Crux bolt ladder (originally A4, now 5.11a free); 120 ft
Continues up bolt ladder with easier terrain; 100 ft
Sustained climbing; 80 ft
Final pitches to summit; 100 ft

road.name: State Route 20 (North Cascades Highway) / Hairpin Turn Parking
road.status: Seasonal; typically open mid-April through mid-November
road.seasonalGate: November 1 - April 15 (approximate)
road.driveNote: Park at hairpin turn parking area (east side) immediately west of Washington Pass summit. High-altitude parking at ~5,400 ft. Road well-maintained but seasonal closures common. Call Methow Valley Ranger District (509-996-4000) for current status.

climate.forecastZone: Marine west coast; high elevation (5,400-7,800 ft); subject to rapid weather changes
climate.typical: Variable. Spring: 35-50F, frequent precipitation. Summer (peak season): 50-70F days, 30-45F nights, occasional afternoon thunderstorms. Fall: 40-60F, increasing storms. Winter: heavy snow, frequent clouds.
climate.spring: Late May-early June: snow may linger on approach; ice on upper pitches possible. Weather unstable.
climate.summer: June-August most stable. Daytime 55-75F at elevation. Nights 30-40F. Afternoon thunderstorms common (30-40% likelihood). Extreme insect activity mid-July through August.
climate.fall: September-early October: best weather window. Temps 45-65F, lower storm frequency. By late October, weather deteriorates.
climate.winter: Closed; highway inaccessible November-April.

timing.recommendedStart: Predawn (5-6 AM) for one-day push. Allows full day margin for climbing, descent, and return to parking by dark.

emergency.nearestHospital: Methow Valley Hospital, Concrete WA (~60 miles, ~90 min). Seattle-area trauma centers (~150 miles) for serious injury.
emergency.rangerStation: Methow Valley Ranger District: 509-996-4000
emergency.notes: Remote, high-elevation location with no cell service. Satellite communicator essential. Rescue logistics are complex; self-sufficiency and early-morning starts are critical.

waypoints.name: Last large tree in Spire Gully

routes.corrections: Grade originally listed as 5.9 A0 in guides, but Mountain Project now lists as 5.11a free, acknowledging the bolt ladders now allow full free climbing without aid placements. Both grades are valid depending on the ascent method (aid vs. free).

routes.overview: Grade III alpine rock route up the East Buttress. Limited contemporary documentation available; this route may be a variant of the Direct East Buttress or a legacy route name no longer in common use.
routes.beta: The specific route details are not well-documented in current Mountain Project or contemporary sources. This route designation appears in the database but is not clearly distinguished from the Direct East Buttress (5.11a, 9 pitch variant) in modern climbing guides. Consider researching Beckey's Cascade Alpine Guide or contacting Methow Valley Ranger District for historical route information.
routes.approach: Approach likely via Spire Gully or East Buttress gully from Highway 20 hairpin parking or Blue Lake Basin route. Exact approach unknown due to limited current documentation.
routes.descent: Likely via South Arete route (standard descent for east-side climbs at SEWS).
routes.bail: Descend via South Arete if route is encountered.
routes.bestSeason: Jun-Oct (typical for SEWS climbing)
proTips: Verify this route exists and obtain current topo from local guides or Beckey guidebook before attempting
Consider the Direct East Buttress as the modern successor to this route
watchOut: Route finding uncertain; bring topo and be prepared to reverse if unclear
knownHazards: Potential loose rock (typical of east-facing buttresses at SEWS)
Exposure on steep terrain

routes.corrections: This route is not clearly documented in current sources (Mountain Project, SummitPost, SuperTopo, AAC publications). The 6-pitch Grade III, 5.8 designation may represent an older route name, a variant of the Direct East Buttress, or a data entry error. Modern climbing references primarily feature the Direct East Buttress (9 pitch, 5.11a) as the main east-side route. Climbers should verify via Beckey's Cascade Alpine Guide or local guides before attempting. It is possible this route no longer exists as a distinct line or has been combined with another route.

routes.fa: Bryan Burdo, Pete Doorish, and Greg White, October 1991
routes.overview: One of the best hard rock climbs in the Cascades, featuring striking steep and direct line on the southeastern face of South Early Winters Spire. The route is characterized by roof work, sustained finger cracks, and powerful free climbing throughout.
routes.beta: Classic hard alpine rock route featuring four pitches of roof/face work (P1-P4: 5.10d to 5.11+), followed by sustained finger crack climbing (P3-P4) and powerful free climbing to the summit block.
routes.approach: From Blue Lake Trailhead: hike 1.25-1.5 miles to South Arete base. From South Arete base, traverse down and right along the wall base for approximately 10 minutes of rock-shoe scrambling to reach the route start on a ledge marked by a fir tree. Route begins at double cracks.
routes.descent: Standard descent via South Arete route with class 3/4 downclimbing and three single-rope rappels from bolted anchors.
routes.descentText: From summit, downclimb the South Arete (3rd/4th class) with one short exposed 5th class section through the chockstone chimney (use handlines and prusik backup). Three sequential 60m rappels from bolted anchors on the south face bring you safely to the base. Total descent: 1.5-2 hours.
routes.itinerary: Day 1: Early approach (1.5-2 hr); allow 4-5 hours for climbing (fitness-dependent); 1.5-2 hours for descent. Total 7-8 hours trailhead to parking.
routes.bail: Reverse any pitch or downclimb to South Arete. Bail possible on all pitches but commitment level is high.
routes.turnaround: Turn back if you have not reached P4 by 1 PM or if weather threatens. Aim to be off the route by 4 PM.
routes.bestSeason: July-September. June may have residual snow/ice. October weather becomes unreliable.
proTips: P1-P2 roof work requires positive climbing and good body tension; commit through the moves
P3 finger crack is truly a sustained splitter; pace yourself and manage energy
The route is best climbed on days with dry rock; wet conditions significantly increase difficulty and danger
Know your limits: this is IV 5.11d; don't overreach. The exposure and rock complexity demand full focus
Two or more parties on the route is common; communicate early with other climbers
watchOut: Roofs and overhangs: reversing moves on steep terrain is problematic; climb forward decisively
Finger crack sections demand solid grip strength and crack technique; pace yourself to avoid arm pump
Exposure: several pitches have open air below; stay mentally engaged and clip in
Weather: afternoon storms can develop rapidly; aim to summit by 2 PM
knownHazards: Sustained 5.11 climbing with few easier pitches to rest—requires solid fitness
Exposure on most of the route—no place to hide if weather deteriorates
Late-season rockfall from freeze-thaw cycles on upper pitches
Route popularity means managing multiple rope teams on narrow rock
routes.detailedRack: Doubles from thin finger cams (sizes 00-1) to #2 Camalot; one #3 Camalot; one set of nuts (sizes 1-6); minimal bolt hangers. Total rack: ~12 lbs.
routes.proNeeds: Good protection throughout on cracks; bolts sparse on face pitches. Crack climbing skills essential. Most pitches require solid gear placement; limited options for aid.
whatToBring: Energy food (high-calorie nutrition for hard climbing)
Sun protection (sunscreen SPF 50+, hat, UVA glasses)
First aid (blister treatment, tape, pain relief)

pitchDetail.notes: Roof climbing toward massive roof; belay bolted; crux is the roof pull and compression move through the roof; 130 ft
Roof continuation with bolt on it; pull roof to the right via strange flares and campus moves; 110 ft
Amazing sustained finger crack to a roof/slot; 120 ft
Move left and down slightly past two bolts; another easier runout above 3rd bolt; then straight up past more bolts to a roof; face moves past a bolt is the crux; good holds soon appear; 100 ft
Splitter cracks and blocks; climbing eases toward summit; 300 ft

climate.typical: Highly variable; Spring: 35-50F, frequent precipitation. Summer: 50-70F days, 30-40F nights, occasional afternoon thunderstorms. Fall: 40-60F, increasing storms. Winter: closed.

climate.summer: July-August: most stable season with occasional afternoon thunderstorms (30-40% likelihood). Extreme insect activity mid-July through August.
climate.fall: September-early October: excellent weather with lower storm frequency; temps 45-65F.

waypoints.name: South Arete base (start of approach to Passenger)
Passenger start (fir tree ledge, double cracks)

routes.corrections: Mountain Project lists grade as 5.11d, not 5.12a as noted in the provided data. The original first ascent was a two-day climb with aid (V 5.11b A0), but Burdo made the first free ascent the following summer at 5.11d. Subsequent ascents are all free at 5.11d. This correction aligns with current climbing records.

blurb: Little Mac Spire is a remote 7992-foot alpine peak in the Southern Pickets of North Cascades National Park. Despite its diminutive name, it offers approximately 800 feet of technical climbing on its shortest route, with excellent rock quality. The peak remains one of the least-climbed summits in this rugged Picket Range cluster.
hierarchyNote: Elevation discrepancy: Database lists 7736 ft, but authoritative sources (Steph Abegg, Wikipedia, Jim Brisbine) document 7992 ft. Recommend verification and correction.

routes.beta: The southwest-facing route provides technical alpine climbing with high-quality rock. Approximately 800 feet of climbing to summit with exposed sections requiring careful route-finding.
routes.overview: Southwest-facing alpine climb on Little Mac Spire with solid rock quality and good movement.
routes.approach: Access via Goodell Creek Trail from Highway 20 near Marblemount. Approach through Terror Basin with significant off-trail scrambling through forested terrain with blowdowns. Requires GPS/GPX track for reliable navigation. Total approach approximately 8-10 hours from trailhead to base of peak.
routes.descent: Descent via reverse route or feasible downclimbing depending on party comfort and conditions.
routes.bestSeason: July through September when snow and glaciers are most stable and approaches most feasible.
routes.comms: Cell service is spotty in Terror Basin; satellite communicator recommended.
routes.proNeeds: Standard alpine rack with cams and nuts for protection. Some parties may use simul-climbing on lower angle terrain.
proTips: SW Arête variant has excellent rock and movement but requires careful route-finding per climber reports
This peak receives very few ascents (fewer than 15-20 total) so expect minimal trail
The ridgeline between Terror Creek Basin and Stetattle Basin provides key visual orientation points
watchOut: Rockfall hazard from exposure to other climbers or loose terrain
Weather can deteriorate rapidly in the Pickets—be prepared for storms
Significant approach requires fitness and scrambling skills before technical climbing begins
knownHazards: Exposed scrambling terrain above timberline
Glaciated approaches require crevasse rescue skills and awareness
Remote location means self-rescue is essential

access.landManager: National Park Service / North Cascades National Park

climate.forecastZone: North Cascades National Park
climate.typical: Marine west coast climate with weather systems tracking northeast from Pacific. Expect significant precipitation year-round, especially on western slopes as snow in winter.
climate.spring: Snow and avalanche conditions still prevalent; glacier travel hazardous.
climate.summer: Most stable window for climbing; persistent sunshine possible but storms can develop rapidly.
climate.fall: Freezing levels drop; mix of snow and rock as season progresses.
climate.winter: Heavy snow accumulation; peak generally not accessible without winter mountaineering expertise.

timing.recommendedStart: Very early morning (before dawn) to maximize daylight for long approach and summit push

emergency.rangerStation: North Cascades National Park Wilderness Information Center, Marblemount: 360-854-7200

routes.corrections: Elevation discrepancy: Provided data lists 7736 ft but Steph Abegg's Picket Range climbing database, Wikipedia McMillan Spire article, and Jim Brisbine's peak archive all document 7992 ft for Little Mac Spire. Specific route grade verification pending (SW Arête mentioned in sources but Southwest Route Grade II-III, 5.4 attribution not independently confirmed through Mountain Project or SummitPost).

blurb: Mount Sefrit is a 7,191 ft Skagit Range summit in the Mount Baker Wilderness, located three miles north of Mount Shuksan in the North Cascades. Composed of gabbro and greenschist rock, the peak offers solitude and varied alpine climbing with commanding views of Mount Shuksan's North Face and Ruth Valley.
hierarchyNote: Peak verified: correct elevation (7191 ft), correct location (Skagit Range, Mount Baker Wilderness, Whatcom County), correct parent peak (Nooksack Tower, 8285 ft). No hierarchy conflicts detected.

routes.overview: A moderate alpine scramble with mixed rock and snow climbing, accessing the summit via the southwest aspect of Nooksack Ridge with significant elevation gain and varied terrain.
routes.beta: The Southwest Ridge approach offers an alternative to the more commonly documented Northwest Glacier-Southeast Ridge route. This line ascends the southwestern side of the peak through mixed climbing and scrambling terrain.
routes.fa: 1930 by Jim Irving and Brick Spouse
routes.bestSeason: July through September offer the most favorable weather for climbing, with June and September presenting more challenging conditions due to deep snow (June) or avalanche hazard from melting (September). Early May is also viable for experienced parties when approaching from the glacier.
knownHazards: Avalanche-prone gullies and hanging snow patches
Cornices above the summit bowl
Wet-snow slides in afternoon sun
Dense brush during approach phase
Class 3 rock exposure on final ridge
watchOut: Early-season avalanche danger from deep snow and warming
Late-day wet-snow slides on southwest-facing terrain
Dense alder and devil's club in the lower approach
Corniced ridge requiring careful negotiation
Afternoon sun warming on south-facing slopes
routes.approach: Begin at Nooksack Cirque trailhead near the end of Ruth Creek Road (Forest Road 32). From the pullout at approximately 2,950 ft elevation, cross Ruth Creek and negotiate dense brush through slide alder and devil's club. The route climbs steeply through forested terrain before emerging into more open alpine terrain as elevation increases toward the southwest ridge system.
routes.descent: Retrace the ascent route, utilizing fast plunge-stepping and glissading where snow conditions permit on the lower sections. The steep forest descent requires careful navigation through brush and stream crossings.
routes.descentText: From the summit, return via the same Southwest Ridge and descent slopes used for ascent. In spring conditions with good snow cover, utilize glissading on snow to descend rapidly through the upper alpine zone. Through forested terrain, plunge-step down steeper sections and carefully navigate brush. Multiple stream crossings and dense vegetation require attention in lower sections. Estimated descent time approximately 2.5-3 hours.
routes.detailedRack: A basic alpine rack including 1 rope (50m), harness, belay device, and quickdraws for protection on Class 3/4 sections. Crampons and ice axe essential for spring/early season snow and ice. Microspikes suitable as alternative to crampons in late season firmer conditions.
routes.proNeeds: Protection ranges from natural rock features to placed protection on steeper Class 3-4 sections. Snow/ice protection (pickets, flukes) recommended for upper snow slopes and cornice hazards. Rock protection may be optional depending on conditions and party preference.
proTips: Early May provides ideal conditions: sufficient snow for efficient ascent without avalanche risk, before late-season brush becomes impassable
Start well before dawn to maximize daylight and avoid afternoon warming on southwest-facing slopes
Scout the route carefully during approach; thick brush can delay progress significantly
Late-season (August-September) may require navigating snow patches on the open scree/rock sections
Cell reception is unreliable in the wilderness; bring satellite communicator or personal locator beacon for emergencies

pitchDetail.notes: Steep forest with dense brush and multiple stream crossings; requires careful navigation and 1.5+ hours
Open alpine terrain with mixed rock and scree; occasional steep snow couloirs in spring
Final 100-150 feet of exposed Class 3 traversing, mixed rock and snow; cornice hazard near summit requiring careful negotiation

road.name: Ruth Creek Road (Forest Road 32)
road.status: Seasonal
road.seasonalGate: May be gated in winter; verify current access status with Mount Baker Ranger District
road.driveNote: Drive Highway 542 east past Glacier Public Service Center approximately 13 miles to the Nooksack River Bridge crossing. Turn left on Forest Road 32 (Ruth Creek Road) and continue 5.4 miles to the parking area at road's end. Road is rough and may require high-clearance vehicle after wet weather.

access.landManager: U.S. Forest Service - Mount Baker Ranger District, Mount Baker-Snoqualmie National Forest
access.permit: Voluntary Climbing Register recommended at trailhead
access.passRequired: Discover Pass may be required for parking at some trailheads; check current requirements

climate.forecastZone: Cascades of Whatcom and Skagit Counties, Washington (WAZ301)
climate.typical: Highly variable mountain weather; expect rapid changes in visibility, precipitation, and wind. Afternoon thunderstorms common in summer.
climate.spring: Unstable snow conditions with warming trends; avalanche danger increases through May and June as snow melts. Frequent precipitation mixed with snow at higher elevations.
climate.summer: Most stable season with lower avalanche danger. Generally clearer conditions July-August; afternoon thunderstorms possible. Freezing level typically 8,500-10,000 ft.
climate.fall: Increasing precipitation and rapidly deteriorating conditions; wet snow and avalanche danger return. First significant snow possible by late September.
climate.winter: Extreme avalanche danger; heavy snow loads and rapid warming cycles create unstable conditions. Winter ascents only for experienced parties with avalanche training and beacons.

routes.comms: Cell reception is unreliable or absent throughout the Mount Baker Wilderness area. Bring satellite communicator (Garmin InReach, PLB) or personal locator beacon for emergencies. The Mt. Baker Ranger District maintains emergency radio contact via repeater systems; satellite communication recommended for safety.

emergency.rangerStation: Mt. Baker Ranger District, Glacier Station - (360) 599-2714
emergency.nearestHospital: PeaceHealth St. Joseph Medical Center (Level II Trauma Center), Bellingham, WA - approximately 45 miles south/southwest from Mount Sefrit trailhead
emergency.notes: Emergency response times to remote wilderness locations are 2+ hours. Helicopter rescue may be limited by weather. Self-rescue and prevention are essential. Contact Whatcom County Sheriff dispatch via satellite communicator or cell if reception available.

routes.overview: The primary climbing route on Elephant Head, accessed via glacier travel from the Dana Glacier. This route involves straightforward ridge climbing with enjoyable scrambling to the summit.
routes.beta: Ascend diagonally west-southwest to approximately 6,200 feet before turning south, then traverse and climb gently at first, then more steeply up the Dana Glacier toward Elephant Head. The route finding is straightforward with mixed climbing and scrambling on the ridge approach.
routes.approach: From White Rock Lakes camp (accessed via Ptarmigan Traverse from Cascade Pass), ascend the ridge to the west. Traverse south until reaching the Dana Glacier. Follow the Dana Glacier under the cleaver toward Elephant Head, approaching from the lower Dana Glacier basin through the ice-sculptured cirque.
routes.descent: Reverse the ascent route via the Dana Glacier. Navigate back through the glacier, watching for crevasses and changing snow/ice conditions. Return to White Rock Lakes or continue south on Ptarmigan Traverse toward Spire Point and Dome Peak.
routes.descentText: Descend via the same route, retracing steps on the Dana Glacier back toward White Rock Lakes. Exercise caution on the glacier as crevasse patterns change with season. The descent typically takes 2-3 hours from the summit. For full traverse continuation, descend via Bachelor Creek and Downey Creek drainages toward the Suiattle River trailhead.
routes.bestSeason: Late July through August provides the most stable conditions, with snow melted from high passes and long daylight hours. Early season (June-early July) involves more soft snow and higher avalanche/crevasse hazard. September is possible but weather deteriorates and conditions become unstable.
routes.turnaround: If conditions deteriorate, weather turns threatening, or route finding becomes difficult in whiteout, retreat immediately to lower elevations. Avoid ascending during afternoon thunderstorm season. Monitor glacier conditions for newly opened crevasses.
routes.comms: Cell service is not available in the alpine. Satellite communication devices recommended for parties in remote Glacier Peak Wilderness.
routes.face: West-northwest face of the peak between Dana and Chickamin glaciers
knownHazards: Glacier travel with crevasse hazard
Loose rock and rockfall from surrounding cliffs
Sudden weather changes and electrical storms
Exposed ridge climbing
Bergschrund hazard at glacier transitions

timing.recommendedStart: Early morning (4-5 AM from camp)

climate.typical: Variable alpine conditions with afternoon thunderstorms common. Temperatures 30-50F at summit.
climate.summer: July-August: Cool to cold with frequent afternoon thunderstorms. Some lingering snow early July. Crevasses more exposed by August.
climate.forecastZone: Northern Cascades
