road.driveNote: Access via Marblemount to Cascade Pass trailhead (North Cascades National Park). Alternatively via Suiattle River trailhead for southern approach.

routes.proNeeds: Solid glacier travel skills essential. Route finding ability required. Crevasse rescue competence needed. Should only be attempted by experienced alpine mountaineers or with professional guide.
proTips: Scout route from White Rock Lakes camp before departure for optimal conditions
Cross Dana Glacier early in morning before afternoon thermal weakening
Watch for moats forming between glacier and rock faces
Recent glacier changes have widened crevasse fields - probe carefully
The twin forms of Dome and Elephant Head create dramatic cirque

emergency.rangerStation: North Cascades Visitor Center

Summit approach and final assault

routes.corrections: Database route listed as 'Standard Route' with PD alpine grade; documented climbing route in AAC Publications is 'Northwest Ridge' with NCCS III grade and F8 rock grade. The grade classifications differ significantly. First ascent date of August 6, 1982 is based on AAC publication year and climbing record, though exact year may require verification. The 'Standard Route' designation may refer to a different line or be a naming variation not found in primary sources.

routes.overview: A classic North Cascades alpine rock climb combining a long approach, significant exposure, and over 3,000 vertical feet of moderate scrambling and low 5th class rock on excellent gneiss.
routes.beta: The Northeast Buttress is a Grade IV, 5.5 alpine climb featuring mostly Class 3-4 scrambling with several exposed pitches of low 5th class rock. The route ascends the Goode Glacier to the Northeast Buttress proper, then follows the crest of the buttress directly toward the summit. The climb involves approximately 3,000 feet of vertical relief over multiple pitches, with continuous moderate terrain and excellent gneiss quality.
routes.approach: Start at Rainy Pass trailhead (4,875 ft, 48.51561, -120.73618) on Highway 20. Hike the PCT south approximately 10 miles to the North Fork Bridge Creek junction (2,800 ft). Continue up North Fork Bridge Creek, crossing Grizzly Creek, then ford or log-cross the North Fork of Bridge Creek. Navigate through overgrown alder ('Tunnel of Butterflies') for approximately 3-4 miles to reach the base of the Goode Glacier. Total approach distance is 12-15 miles one-way with 6,500+ feet of elevation gain.
routes.descent: Descend via 4-6 rappels from the summit area down to Black Tooth Notch, then traverse and downclimb into the Southwest Couloir. Follow the southwest gully down and exit via Park Creek Trail back to Bridge Creek Trail and PCT.
routes.descentText: From the summit, make 4-6 rappels (60m rope adequate for 30m anchors) trending down and left toward Black Tooth Notch. The first rappel is relatively straight down the ridgeline; subsequent rappels are more traverse-heavy. Once at the notch, downclimb into the southwest couloir and follow it downslope, eventually gaining Park Creek Trail for the long walk out (approximately 10+ miles to trailhead).
routes.itinerary: Day 1: Hike from Rainy Pass trailhead via PCT and Bridge Creek Trail (~14 miles, 8-10 hours) to high camp at the base of Goode Glacier. Day 2: Alpine start before dawn; climb Northeast Buttress to summit (~8-12 hours on route, 2-3 miles distance). Bivy on summit or descend to Black Tooth Notch area camp if conditions permit. Day 3: Complete descent via rappels and couloir, hike back to Bridge Creek area. Day 4: Hike out via Park Creek Trail and PCT (~20 miles, 8-10 hours).
routes.bail: There is no safe bail option once committed to the buttress itself—no rappel anchors exist on-route until near the summit. Early turnaround decisions (within the first 1-2 pitches) offer the best retreat via retracing on easier terrain.
routes.turnaround: Establish a firm turnaround time based on daylight, weather window, and route progress. Most parties should plan to reach the summit by early afternoon to allow adequate time for descent. With 10-12 hours of climbing ahead, an alpine start in dark/early light is essential.
routes.bestSeason: July through August is optimal, with late July onward offering the most stable conditions. The climb has been completed as early as June and as late as September, but accessing the toe of the buttress becomes progressively more difficult as the moat widens with warming temperatures.
routes.comms: No cell service in the North Cascades backcountry; satellite communicators (PLB, InReach, Garmin) are essential. Ranger stations at North Cascades Visitor Center near Marblemount.
routes.fa: Fred Beckey and Tom Stewart, August 6, 1966
knownHazards: Moat crossing at glacier-to-rock transition often crux and can widen late season
Loose rock quality on descent sections, significant hazard for second climbers
No bail option or anchors on-route until near summit
Rock quality highly variable—excellent gneiss on ascent but poor quality on descent terrain
Crevasse hazard on Goode Glacier approach
routes.detailedRack: Standard alpine rack with double cams from 0.3 to 1.0 inch, emphasis on 0.5-0.75 doubles for long simul-climbing blocks. Tricams valuable for anchors (doubles recommended). 60m rope folded in half acceptable. Ice screws for late-season moat and icy sections.
routes.proNeeds: Moderate protection needs; much of the route is simul-climbed due to sustained moderate terrain. Place anchors at major stance points for rappel anchors (essential).
proTips: Moat crossing is the crux—scout for collapsed snow bridges around 6,800 ft
Summit has room for only 2-3 climbers to bivy; plan accordingly
Large snow patch for water near northwest saddle
Move quickly; simulate much of the climb to maintain time
Red ledges early on route can be wet from waterfalls—cross quickly
Goode Glacier melt rate varies widely; earlier in season (late July) offers more stable conditions
watchOut: The moat crossing can widen significantly by mid-August, potentially becoming impassable
Descent rock quality is poor; protect seconds carefully on rappels
Water is scarce except near summit—plan melt/collection strategy
No easy escape once committed to upper buttress
Exposure increases substantially in the upper pitches

waypoints.name: North Fork Bridge Creek Trail Junction

access.landManager: National Park Service - North Cascades National Park
access.permit: North Cascades backcountry permit required
access.fees: $6 permit reservation fee, $10 per person per night recreation fee (youth 15 and under free)

access.closures: North Fork Bridge Creek bridge out for stock use (human foot traffic only). Maple Creek bridge removed seasonally. Check with Wilderness Information Center for current status.

climate.forecastZone: Northwest Avalanche Center - North Cascades zone
climate.typical: Cool to cold, variable weather. Morning temperatures near freezing at high elevations in July-August; afternoon warming common.
climate.spring: Heavy snowpack; approach often difficult before late June due to snow and water crossings
climate.summer: Most stable season (July-August). Warm afternoons, cool nights. Afternoon thunderstorms common. Afternoon melt accelerates moat formation.
climate.fall: Shorter days, cooler temperatures, earlier storms. Route increasingly difficult from September onward.
climate.winter: Significant snow and ice; mountaineering grade increases substantially. Technical ice climbing skills required. Not recommended for typical alpine parties.

emergency.nearestHospital: Skagit Valley Hospital, Mount Vernon (1415 E. Kincaid St., Mount Vernon, WA 98273), approximately 60 miles and 2+ hours from trailhead. Phone: 360-424-4111
emergency.rangerStation: North Cascades Visitor Center, Marblemount (near Rainy Pass, ~50 miles from parking)
emergency.sheriffDispatch: Skagit County Sheriff Dispatch: 911 or 360-428-3211. Backcountry rescue via Snohomish County (adjacent) may respond to North Cascades area.
emergency.notes: Satellite communication device (PLB, InReach) essential. No cell service in backcountry. Helicopter evacuation difficult due to terrain and park policies; prevention through route discipline is critical.

road.name: North Cascades Highway (WA State Route 20)
road.status: Open June-October typically; winter closures November-April
road.seasonalGate: Rainy Pass area typically opens late May/early June; road closures possible November through early May due to snow
road.driveNote: Major scenic highway; 2-lane highway, well-maintained. Drive time from Seattle ~2-2.5 hours. Rainy Pass is at milepost 158-159.

routes.overview: An alpine ice and mixed route on the northeast face of Mount Goode featuring water ice gullies and 5th class rock climbing with ice sections.
routes.beta: The Northeast Face route ascends via water-ice gullies to the right of the Northeast Buttress, combining ice climbing (AI2 grade) with mixed rock and ice terrain. The route is less established than the buttress route but offers an alternative line in winter conditions or when the buttress is icebound.
routes.approach: Same approach as Northeast Buttress via Rainy Pass trailhead, Bridge Creek Trail, and Goode Glacier base. Route branches from glacier into gullies on the northeast face.
routes.descent: Descend via gullies between the buttress and northeast face route, or coordinate with Northeast Buttress descent route.
knownHazards: Icefall from seracs above
Difficult ice conditions vary by season
Avalanche exposure on gullies
Complex terrain in whiteout conditions
routes.bestSeason: Late July through early August when ice conditions are most stable

routes.overview: A remote and serious alpine climbing route ascending Mount Goode's 2,800-foot East Ridge, featuring exposed gneiss rock with sustained climbing and significant exposure on both flanks.
routes.beta: Megalodon Ridge is a seldom-ascended alpine route first climbed in 2007. The climb involves approximately 2,800 to 5,000 feet of steep, exposed gneiss with sustained climbing. Much of the route is simul-climbed to maintain pace, with crux sections reaching 5.10. The ridge is named after the giant prehistoric shark due to its tooth-like appearance from certain angles.
routes.approach: Approach is significantly longer and more complex than the Northeast Buttress due to remoteness and brush. Access to the base of Megalodon Ridge requires approximately 12 miles of hiking plus 3,000+ feet of off-trail bushwhacking. Start at Rainy Pass trailhead and follow Bridge Creek Trail into the North Fork area, then bush-bash to reach the east ridge base.
routes.descent: Extremely difficult descent down the south side of the mountain skirting terrain hazards; follow slopes toward Goode Glacier area or descend complex terrain. First ascent party took the full day just to descend 5,000 feet.
routes.descentText: From summit area, traverse and downclimb complex terrain with significant exposure. The first ascent descent involved an exposed downclimb of an overhanging 5.10 pitch and skirting of forest fire zones. Descent is technical and time-consuming; plan for 6+ hours of difficult downclimbing and scrambling.
routes.itinerary: Day 1-2: Long approach via Bridge Creek and North Fork to base of ridge (~15+ hours with bushwhacking). Day 3: Climb Megalodon Ridge from base to summit (~12 hours). Bivy on summit due to descent complexity. Day 4-5: Complex descent and return hike.
routes.fa: Blake Herrington and Sol Wertkin, September 6, 2007
knownHazards: Extremely exposed on both flanks
Rock quality variable—excellent gneiss on ascent but poorer lower down
Remote location makes rescue difficult
Complex descent terrain with significant technical difficulty
Ample opportunity for errors or falls due to exposure
routes.detailedRack: Full alpine gear with emphasis on cams for both ascent and particularly for descent anchors. Twin ropes useful for rappels on descent. Ice screws for early-season sections.
routes.proNeeds: Simul-climbing much of the route up to 5.8; place anchors at major stances for potential retreat anchors
proTips: This is a remote, serious alpine climb; only for experienced mountaineers
Much of the climb can be simul-climbed at moderate grade
The descent is harder than the ascent; plan accordingly
Weather window is critical—avoid poor visibility
First ascent team took approximately 11 hours climbing plus overnight bivy on summit
watchOut: Extreme exposure on both sides of the ridge
The descent is significantly more difficult than the ascent
Rock quality deteriorates lower on the ridge
Rescue would be extremely difficult in this remote location

access.landManager: National Park Service - North Cascades National Park
access.permit: North Cascades backcountry permit required

emergency.nearestHospital: Skagit Valley Hospital, Mount Vernon
emergency.notes: Remote location; satellite communication essential. Rescue extremely difficult.

routes.overview: The easiest climbing route on Mount Goode, ascending a rocky gully system via Class 4 scrambling. This was the route of the peak's first ascent in 1936.
routes.beta: The Southwest Couloir is a Class 4 scrambling route (some sections Class 5 depending on exact line) and the least technical route on Mount Goode. The route involves ascending moraine, crossing glacier remnants, climbing rotten ridge sections, and following the broad southwest couloir with rock scrambling to the summit. Despite being the 'easiest' route, it is still serious mountaineering terrain.
routes.approach: Access from the southwest via Bridge Creek approach (same as Northeast Buttress approach: Rainy Pass trailhead, PCT, Bridge Creek Trail to high camp). From glacier base, scramble toward the southwest side of the mountain to reach the couloir entry.
routes.descent: Descend the same Southwest Couloir route downslope or utilize a different gully descent depending on conditions.
routes.descentText: Reverse the ascent route, descending the southwest couloir and associated ramps. Loose rock is common; move carefully and manage team spacing to minimize rockfall hazard to lower climbers.
routes.fa: Wolf Bauer, Philip Dickett, Joe Halwax, Jack Hossack, and George MacGowan, July 5, 1936
knownHazards: Loose, rotten rock throughout
Class 4-5 scrambling exposure
Rock fall hazard common
No established anchors
Moat/glacier sections variable by season
proTips: This route is easier than others on the peak but still serious
Helmet essential due to rockfall hazard
Moat crossing near the base is often straightforward on this approach
Late season (August+) often has better rock/snow consolidation
watchOut: Rocks move freely—everyone must wear a helmet
Loose exposure on exposed ramps in the upper couloir
Late-season weather can deteriorate quickly
routes.bestSeason: July-September

blurb: Mount Logan (9,087 ft) is located in the remote North Cascades National Park and is the 4th highest peak in the range. The mountain features three glaciers (Fremont, Banded, and Douglas) on its flanks, requiring glacier travel and roped rock scrambling skills. First ascended by Lage Wernstedt in 1926, it remains a classic alpine mountaineering objective accessed via extended backcountry treks of 20+ miles through pristine mountain wilderness.

routes.overview: The most popular route on Mount Logan, featuring gentle glacier travel and a scenic traverse of the Hogsback Ridge to the summit.
routes.beta: Approach via Thunder Creek Trail to Park Creek Pass (19.6 mi, 4,900 ft gain from trailhead), then traverse northward 2 miles across heather and alpine meadows to Fremont Glacier at 8,000 feet. Ascend the gentle glacier to the south ridge (Hogsback), negotiate a snow-filled gully through cliff bands, and scramble Class 3-4 rock via ledges on the east side of the summit ridge to the summit.
routes.approach: Start at Colonial Creek Campground trailhead on SR 20 mile 130 (elevation 1,060 ft). Follow Thunder Creek Trail north for approximately 16-18 miles to Thunder Basin camp around 6,000 ft. From Thunder Basin, cross meadows and begin ascending. Traverse gradually northward around a broad ridge through open heather and high alpine terrain, gaining approximately 2 miles to reach Fremont Glacier at 8,000 feet. Campsites exist at Thunder Basin and other designated locations along the approach.
routes.descent: Reverse the climbing route down the summit scramble and glacier. Descend via the Hogsback and snow gully, crossing the moat back to the glacier toe (late season snow bridge may be unstable). Return via the approach route, retracing steps through heather to Thunder Basin, then back to the trailhead via Thunder Creek Trail (similar duration to approach).
routes.descentText: Descend the Class 3-4 rock terrain retracing the route up the east side of the summit ridge, downclimbing carefully to the snow gully. Navigate the steep gully back to the Hogsback. Cross the moat (ice axe required, late-season snow bridge may be unstable or absent) to reach the glacier toe. From there, descend the glacier proper back to the 8,000-foot margin. Return to Thunder Basin camp via the northward traverse across heather slopes, then retrace the entire approach trail back to the trailhead via Thunder Creek Trail. Total descent typically takes 4-6 hours from summit to base, then additional 6-8 hours for trail exit.
routes.bestSeason: June through September, with July and August offering the most stable conditions. September trips are possible but approach snow may be significantly reduced and moat crossings more difficult.
routes.aspect: SW approach to SW-facing upper glacier
routes.face: Southwest face via Fremont Glacier and Hogsback Ridge
objHaz: Avalanche hazard from wet heavy snow (west side North Cascades receives high winter precipitation)
Crevasses on Fremont Glacier (generally small but require rope team travel)
Rockfall from cliff bands at ~7,350 feet elevation
Moat crossing with unstable late-season snow bridges
Exposed Class 3-4 rock scrambling with significant drop-off potential
Thunderstorm risk on exposed summit ridge
Remote location requiring self-sufficiency for 3-4 days
watchOut: Moat crossing in late season can be very dangerous with unstable snow bridges—ice axe mandatory
Summit ridge has significant exposure and some sections may require belay
Weather can deteriorate rapidly on summit block with high wind exposure
Crevasses are generally avoidable but may open late season—travel as rope team of 3+
Rock quality on summit scramble is solid but can be loose/blocky in places
knownHazards: High avalanche danger in winter/spring due to wet maritime snow; check NWAC forecast before June
Glacial moat can widen significantly and become impassable in late summer without snow bridge
Frequent afternoon thunderstorms occur in July-August
Black flies and mosquitoes severe in early summer on approach trail
gear: Glacier gear: crampons, ice axe, helmet, carabiners
Rope: single 50-60m dynamic rope for 3-person team
Rock rack: optional light rack for possible belay
Camping: full backpacking kit for 3-4 day trip
Emergency: first aid, headlamp, satellite communicator (cell service absent)
whatToBring: Sturdy hiking boots (break in before trip)
Food for 3-4 days with high-calorie options
Insulating sleeping bag (temperatures can drop below freezing)
Shelter (lightweight tent required for bear country)
routes.detailedRack: For the rock scramble sections, minimal protection is typically placed. The rock is generally solid with good friction, and most climbers use this as an unprotected scramble. A lightweight rack of a few slings and possibly 1-2 small carabiners may be carried for potential rappel anchors on descent if needed.
routes.proNeeds: The rock scramble on Mount Logan does not require extensive protection. The terrain is Class 3-4 scrambling on relatively solid rock with good holds. Most ascents are protection-free; climbers rely on careful footwork and scrambling technique rather than placed protection.
proTips: Start very early on summit day to avoid afternoon thunderstorms
Check NWAC avalanche forecast for your planned week
Pre-acclimate to altitude by spending extra day at high camp
Late-season (Sep-Oct) offers lower avalanche risk but stable moat crossing impossible
Hogsback Ridge offers stunning 360-degree views of North Cascades
Do not camp at Park Creek Pass (fragile meadows)—camp at designated sites

timing.recommendedStart: 5:00 AM on summit day

routes.itinerary: Day 1: Trailhead to Thunder Basin camp (16 miles, ~6-7 hours hiking). Day 2: Thunder Basin to high camp near Fremont Glacier/Park Creek Pass area (4 miles, ~3-4 hours). Day 3: Early summit push from high camp, summit by early-mid afternoon, return to high camp (7-8 hours round trip). Day 4: Descend to Thunder Basin then trailhead (20 miles exit, ~10-12 hours).
routes.turnaround: Establish a firm turnaround time of 2:00 PM on summit day to ensure safe descent before dark; weather often deteriorates in late afternoon.
routes.bail: From high camp, retreat via approach route back to Thunder Basin and out via Thunder Creek Trail. From the glacier, descend to 8,000 feet and reverse approach to safe location. From the upper rock scramble, downclimb carefully or reverse route. In emergency, satellite communication critical for evacuation.
routes.comms: No cell service available in backcountry. Satellite communicator (e.g., Garmin InReach) or personal locator beacon (PLB) strongly recommended for emergency communication. Some reports of limited Verizon coverage only at lowest elevations on approach trail.

road.name: State Route 20 (North Cascades Highway)
road.status: Open generally May through November; winter closures possible
road.seasonalGate: SR 20 can close during heavy snow; check WSDOT for current status
road.driveNote: From Seattle, take I-5 north to SR 20 east. Drive approximately 2 hours to Colonial Creek Campground at mile 130. Parking available at campground (fee). Road well-maintained but narrow and winding in places.

climate.typical: Marine west coast climate with Pacific Ocean influence. Weather systems move from west to northeast. Tends toward cloudy conditions in shoulder seasons (May, early June, September), clearer in mid-summer (July-August).
climate.spring: May-early June: snow persists at elevation, streams high from snowmelt, avalanche hazard peaks in May-June, afternoon thunderstorms common
climate.summer: July-August: most stable conditions, lower avalanche hazard, frequent afternoon thunderstorms, daytime temperatures 50-65°F at 6,000 ft, freezing level 12,000-13,000 ft
climate.fall: September-October: clear weather but shorter daylight, moat crossing becomes problematic late September onward, freezing level descends
climate.winter: November-April: heavy snowfall (west side averages 134 inches annually), extreme avalanche hazard, ice axe mandatory for approach trails

pitchDetail.pitch: 1-2: Approach via Thunder Creek Trail to Park Creek Pass
pitchDetail.grade: Hiking, mix of forest and meadow terrain
pitchDetail.notes: Multiple creek crossings, persistent snow late June/early July crosses the trail, ice axe recommended early season for icy sections
Gently rising traverse across high alpine, crosses rib at 7,600 ft, steep gully passage through cliff bands around 7,350 ft with possible snow/ice
Gentle glacier traverse, roped team recommended, crevasses generally small and avoidable but present
Steeper glacier leading to south ridge (Hogsback), moat crossing to access ridge crest, late-season snow bridge may be absent

pitchDetail.pitch: 6: Summit scramble via Hogsback and east side
pitchDetail.notes: Approximately 300 vertical feet of scrambling, cross Hogsback notch, descend briefly then traverse ledges on east side of summit ridge, final 100 feet Class 3-4 with good holds and solid rock, some exposure

routes.overview: A strenuous alpine route involving glacier travel and steep scree slopes, accessed via the Easy Pass approach from Highway 20.
routes.beta: Approach via Easy Pass (6,500 ft) from Highway 20 mile 151, descend into Fisher Basin, then off-trail climb to Banded Glacier basin. Ascend unstable scree slopes to reach the glacier, cross the Banded Glacier to meet the NE ridge at approximately 8,800 feet, then scramble Class 3-4 rock to the summit. The critical transition point is gaining the NE ridge on solid rock rather than attempting the summit block directly from the glacier.
routes.approach: Start at Easy Pass Trailhead at Highway 20 mile 151 (approximately 5 miles west of Rainy Pass). Elevation 3,700 ft. Hike 3.5 miles gaining 2,800 feet to Easy Pass (6,500 ft) through western hemlock and Pacific silver fir forest, crossing Easy Pass Creek three times. Route climbs steeply over avalanche paths and steep rock slopes. From Easy Pass, descend into Fisher Basin via steep but scenic trail through alpine meadows into mature forest (1.5 miles). Continue down to Fisher Camp or Cosho Camp (better option, larger and better-situated). From camp, hike to base of Banded Glacier basin via off-trail route, gaining approximately 13,000 feet total to summit.
routes.descent: Descend via reverse of climbing route. From summit, descend Class 3-4 rock back to the glacier, traverse Banded Glacier to the col, descend unstable scree fields back to glacier toe. Return via off-trail descent to Fisher Basin, retrace to Easy Pass, then descend steep trail to Easy Pass trailhead.
routes.descentText: Carefully downclimb the Class 3-4 rock terrain from the summit back to the col where the glacier begins. Descend the Banded Glacier itself (watch for crevasses). Exit the glacier to the scree fields below and descend extremely carefully—scree described as moving like a 'Newtonian fluid,' so place each step deliberately. This descent section is hazardous and slow. Continue down to Fisher Basin base, then retrace the off-trail route to Fisher Camp area. From there, ascend back to Easy Pass (steep climb, 2,800 ft gain), then descend Easy Pass trail back to trailhead. Total exit typically 12+ hours.
routes.bestSeason: Late June through September. Early season (late June-early July) offers better snow coverage reducing scree difficulty; late season (August-September) means more exposure but lower avalanche hazard.
routes.face: North face via Banded Glacier and NE ridge
objHaz: Avalanche hazard from wet heavy snow (Fischer Creek valley on east side of divide, still receives significant precipitation)
Unstable scree slopes (described as 'moving like a Newtonian fluid'—extremely hazardous for downclimbing)
Crevasses on Banded Glacier (can be open and significant)
Class 3-4 rock scrambling with exposure and loose rock in places
Rockfall hazard on steep approach sections
watchOut: Scree descent is extremely hazardous—move very slowly and deliberately
Summit rock block is narrow with limited safe space for multiple people
Transition from glacier to NE ridge critical—ensure you are on solid rock point at 8,800 ft, not attempting direct summit block from glacier
Crevasses on Banded Glacier can be significant in some years—travel roped team
Loose rock and blockiness on upper scramble sections
knownHazards: High avalanche danger in winter/spring; check NWAC forecast
Wet heavy snow common on west side of Cascades, peak avalanche danger May-June
Frequent afternoon thunderstorms July-August on exposed summit ridge
Extremely difficult bushwhacking if off-trail route varies (35 miles, 13,000 ft gain described as very strenuous)
gear: Glacier gear: crampons, ice axe, helmet, carabiners
Rope: single 50-60m dynamic rope for 3-person team on glacier
Navigation: map, compass, GPS (critical for off-trail sections)
Camping: full backpacking kit for 3-4 day trip
Emergency: first aid, headlamp, satellite communicator
whatToBring: Sturdy hiking boots with good ankle support (off-trail scree)
Sunscreen and lip balm with high SPF
Food for 3-4 days with high-calorie options
Insulating sleeping bag rated for below freezing
routes.detailedRack: Minimal rock protection needed. The Class 3-4 scramble is primarily unprotected; climbers rely on good technique and solid holds. A light rack of slings and potentially one or two quickdraws may be carried for possible emergency rappel anchors or belay if needed, but most ascents place no protection.
routes.proNeeds: Rock protection on this route is minimal to none. The scrambling relies on careful footwork on generally solid rock with good friction and holds. No placed protection is typically used.
proTips: Use early-season window (late June-July) when snow reduces scree exposure
This route less crowded than Fremont Glacier route
Critical checkpoint: gain solid rock at 8,800 ft on NE ridge—do NOT try to summit directly from glacier
Scree descent extremely slow and tedious—allow extra time and be patient
Pre-position high camp as close as feasible to glacier toe to minimize summit day distance

timing.recommendedStart: 5:00 AM on summit day

routes.itinerary: Day 1: Trailhead to Easy Pass to Fisher Camp (3.5 miles up + descent + ~5 miles to camp = 8-10 hours). Day 2: Fisher Camp to high camp near Banded Glacier toe (off-trail, ~5 miles, 3-4 hours). Day 3: Early summit push, summit, return to high camp (10-12 hours). Day 4: Descend to Fisher Camp then exit via Easy Pass to trailhead (12+ hours).
routes.turnaround: Establish turnaround time of 2:00 PM to ensure safe descent before dark; weather deteriorates in afternoon and descent is slow.
routes.bail: From high camp near glacier, descend to glacier toe and reverse off-trail route to Fisher Basin. From glacier, descend to glacier toe then retrace to Fisher Basin. From upper rock section, downclimb or reverse route. Emergency evacuation by satellite communication.
routes.comms: No cell service in backcountry. Satellite communicator or personal locator beacon essential for emergency communication.

pitchDetail.pitch: 1: Easy Pass Trailhead to Easy Pass

pitchDetail.pitch: 3: Fisher Basin to Banded Glacier basin (off-trail)
pitchDetail.notes: ~5 miles, ~3,500 ft gain, route-finding critical, some steep sections
Steep, unstable scree describ as 'moving like Newtonian fluid,' ascending easier than descending
Roped team travel, crevasses can be significant, ascend to meeting point with NE ridge at ~8,800 ft
Transition critical: gain solid rock on NE ridge at ~8,800 ft, then scramble ~300 vertical feet to summit

routes.overview: A remote glacier route via Douglas Glacier, accessed from the Easy Pass trailhead but presenting significant bushwhacking challenges through slide alder thickets. Technical terrain includes steep ice and snow sections on the glacier and Class 3-4 rock on the summit block.
routes.beta: Approach via Easy Pass (6,500 ft) from Highway 20 mile 151, descend into Fisher Creek valley, cross Fisher Creek, travel up Douglas Creek valley (extremely brushy, slow progress), gain Douglas Glacier basin, ascend steep glacier with steeper ice/snow sections to col, traverse to meet Banded Glacier, then scramble Class 3-4 rock to summit via standard summit block.
routes.approach: Start at Easy Pass Trailhead at Highway 20 mile 151 (elevation 3,700 ft). Hike 3.5 miles gaining 2,800 feet to Easy Pass (6,500 ft). Descend into Fisher Basin via steep scenic trail through alpine meadows and forest (1.5 miles). Continue to Fisher Creek valley floor and established campsites. From camp area, cross Fisher Creek via large fallen log and travel east side of Douglas Creek valley on faint climber's path. This transition from forest to open terrain is where the route becomes extremely challenging—travelers encounter a vast field of slide alder and other brush, 10+ feet high, virtually impenetrable in many directions, with small rocky stream channels crisscrossing the ground. Progress is measured in inches per minute. Off-trail navigational skill critical.
routes.descent: Reverse of route. From summit, descend Class 3-4 rock back to glacier. Traverse Banded Glacier to Douglas Glacier, descend steep glacier and ice sections back to basin. Descend Douglas Creek valley retracing the horrific bushwhack (slow and difficult). Return to Fisher Creek valley floor and exit via established trail.
routes.descentText: Downclimb the Class 3-4 summit rock carefully, traversing steep terrain back to glacier. Descend the Douglas Glacier (steeper descent than ascent, watch for crevasses). From glacier toe, navigate the very difficult off-trail descent through slide alder thicket—this section is exceptionally slow and requires patience and care. Continue down to Fisher Creek valley floor, then retrace established trail to Easy Pass camp area. Ascend Easy Pass (steep 2,800 ft gain), then descend Easy Pass trail to trailhead. Total exit 12+ hours.
routes.bestSeason: Mid-June through late July, before high summer heat makes brush exploration more difficult. Early season best for snow coverage reducing brush difficulty. By late August, brush is fully leafed out and exponentially more difficult.
objHaz: Extreme bushwhacking difficulty through slide alder thicket (600 yards reported to take 2 hours of intense effort)
Steep ice and snow sections on Douglas Glacier requiring technical climbing
Cliff bands surrounding glacier cirque with limited entry/exit points
Avalanche hazard from steep ice/snow slopes
Severe black fly and mosquito pressure in off-trail brush sections
Class 3-4 rock scrambling with exposure on summit block
watchOut: Do NOT underestimate the Douglas Creek bushwhack difficulty—reports describe it as consuming extraordinary time and energy
This route notably slower and more difficult than Banded or Fremont Glacier routes due to approach bushwhacking
Steep ice sections on Douglas Glacier require crampons and ice axe proficiency
Route-finding through brush is critical—losing the faint climber's path can lead to additional time/difficulty
Limited safe space on summit block—do not attempt with large group
knownHazards: Major avalanche (~15 years prior to 2022 reports) created the massive slide alder thicket clogging Douglas Creek approach
Wet heavy maritime snow creates avalanche danger May-June
Steep ice sections on glacier can glide/slush in afternoon sun
Frequent afternoon thunderstorms July-August
Deep rocky stream channels in brush field can cause ankle injuries
gear: Glacier gear: crampons, ice axe, helmet, carabiners
Rope: single 50-60m dynamic rope for 3-person team on steep sections
Navigation: map, compass, GPS (critical for off-trail sections)
Machete or pruning saw (optional but may help through brush, check park regulations)
Camping: full backpacking kit for 3-4 day trip
Emergency: first aid, headlamp, satellite communicator, bug spray
whatToBring: Sturdy hiking boots with excellent ankle support (off-trail rough terrain)
Head net and bug spray (extreme black fly/mosquito pressure)
Insulating sleeping bag rated below freezing
routes.detailedRack: Minimal rock protection. The Class 3-4 scramble relies on good technique on solid holds. No placed protection typically used; a light emergency rack of slings may be carried.
routes.proNeeds: Rock protection minimal to none. Scrambling relies on careful footwork and technique rather than placed anchors.
proTips: Time this route for June-early July when snow may still bridge some brush sections
This route notably more difficult and less popular than Fremont or Banded Glacier routes for good reason
Bushwhacking pace is extremely slow—be patient and don't force progress
Report any significant brush changes to park rangers to aid future parties
Consider waiting for post-winter/spring before attempting (avalanche clears brush or creates markers)

timing.recommendedStart: 4:00 AM on summit day (requires very early start due to slow bushwhacking)

routes.itinerary: Day 1: Trailhead to Easy Pass to Fisher Camp (8-10 hours). Day 2: Fisher Camp to high camp near Douglas Glacier (off-trail with horrible bushwhacking, 8-10 hours, only 3-4 miles). Day 3: Summit attempt (very early start, 12-14 hour day climbing + return to camp). Day 4: Exit via Douglas Creek bushwhack to Fisher Camp, then Easy Pass to trailhead (12+ hours).
routes.turnaround: Establish firm turnaround time of 1:00 PM to ensure safe descent before dark; descent is slow and difficult through technical terrain.
routes.bail: From high camp near glacier, descend glacier to toe and reverse through Douglas Creek bushwhack to Fisher Creek valley. From glacier, return to safe camp area. Emergency evacuation by satellite communicator.
routes.comms: No cell service in backcountry. Satellite communicator or personal locator beacon essential for emergency communication.

pitchDetail.pitch: 3: Fisher Creek to Douglas Creek (off-trail bushwhack)
pitchDetail.notes: 0.5-1 mile, 200 ft gain but progresses at inches per minute through slide alder thicket
1-2 miles, ~3,000 ft gain, open forest transitioning to scree/rock

blurb: Ruth Mountain (7,115 ft) is a mellow glaciated alpine peak located two miles south of Hannegan Pass in the North Cascades, straddling the border between North Cascades National Park and Mount Baker Wilderness. First ascended in 1916 and named for Ruth Cleveland, daughter of President Grover Cleveland, it is a classic beginner alpine objective featuring minimally crevassed glacier travel and Class 2-3 scrambling.

routes.overview: The standard alpine route on Ruth Mountain is a beginner glacier climb combining moderate scrambling to Hannegan Pass with easy glacier travel on minimally crevassed terrain. The route ascends the north-facing Ruth Glacier to the summit ridge.
routes.beta: Ruth Mountain is a mellow walk-up alpine climb suitable for parties with basic mountaineering experience. The route involves a well-traveled hiking trail to Hannegan Pass followed by a scrambler's trail and ridge traverse to the Ruth Glacier, then glacier travel to the summit. Crevasses are typically small (maximum 10 feet deep) and easily avoided. Ice axe proficiency and glacier travel knowledge are essential.
routes.approach: From Bellingham, drive Highway 542 east approximately 46 miles to 13 miles past the Glacier Public Service Center. Turn left onto Forest Service Road 32 and continue 5.4 miles to Hannegan Campground (3,000 ft). From the trailhead, hike the Hannegan Pass Trail 4 miles to Hannegan Pass (5,056 ft), gaining 2,056 feet. From Hannegan Pass, take the climbers trail heading southeast, climbing steeply around cliff bands. The trail becomes extremely rocky, root-y, washed out, and occasionally exposed. Continue upward bearing left around shoulder cliffs. The trail leads to a ridge terminating at the toe of the Ruth Glacier on the north slope. Expect 4-5 hours to reach high camp.
routes.descent: Reverse the ascent, descending back through the glacier to the ridge, then reversing the scrambler's trail to Hannegan Pass and the main trail. Descend approximately 800 feet south and west from the summit toward a gully on the west side; find a faint trail and descend another 800 feet. Class 2 scrambling on mostly solid rock. Regain the Ruth Glacier and reverse the approach, paying careful attention to crevasse fields. The total descent typically takes 2-3 hours.
routes.descentText: From the summit, descend south and west approximately 800 feet toward a gully on the west side of Ruth Mountain. Locate a faint path in loose rock. Continue descending about another 800 feet to the low saddle area. Class 2 scrambling on mostly solid rock with loose sections requiring care. Regain the Ruth Glacier and reverse the approach ascent carefully watching crevasse fields, staying on the main track. Descend via the ridge back to the Hannegan Pass trail, then return via the main trail to the trailhead.
routes.itinerary: Day 1 (2-day option): Hike 4 miles to Hannegan Pass (2-3 hours), then continue 2+ miles on scrambler's trail to Ruth Glacier toe (1.5-2 hours). Set up camp at 5,500-6,000 feet. Day 2: Early start from camp. Glacier travel 2 miles to summit (2-3 hours). Summit and return to camp (1-2 hours). Alternatively, complete entire route as day climb starting before dawn, reaching summit mid-afternoon and returning to trailhead by dusk (9-11 hours total).
routes.bail: If weather deteriorates or parties fall behind, reverse course at Hannegan Pass and return to the trailhead. If higher on the glacier, descend back to the glacier toe and reverse to Hannegan Pass. There are no established emergency bivys or shelters, so descent should begin immediately if conditions become unsafe.
routes.turnaround: If not on the summit by early afternoon or if weather becomes threatening, turn around and descend. Afternoon thunderstorms are common; descending by mid-afternoon eliminates this hazard. Crevasse rescue is complex; if a party member falls, immediate assistance is required, so maintaining group cohesion and roped travel after early July is essential.
routes.bestSeason: July through September offers the best conditions with minimal snow. Late June can work for early ascents, allowing over-the-ridge traverses before crevasses open. By July, the high ridge point is no longer passable and requires a steep 60-degree side traverse. Early summer (May-June) requires more snow travel skills.
routes.proNeeds: Basic alpine gear: ice axe, crampons, rope (60-80 feet minimum for 2-3 person team), climbing harness, belay device, carabiners, slings, and prusik loops for crevasse rescue. Rope team should maintain 30-40 feet spacing between climbers.
routes.detailedRack: For glacier travel, a 2-3 person rope team should carry: one 60-80 foot climbing rope (8-9mm dynamic recommended), each climber tied in with figure-eight follow-through. Two prusik slings per person: one long sling with foot stirrups for lower prusik, one shorter sling for waist prusik. Carry minimum two locking carabiners per person for anchor work. Basic rescue consists of Texas kick setup with two prusiks. Many parties carry 2-3 ice screws for emergency anchors.
whatToBring: Map and compass or GPS device
proTips: Start before dawn for full-day summit attempts; the Hannegan Pass trail is rockier and slower than expected
In June, the ridge crest can be traversed directly; after July, expect a steep 60-degree side traverse
Stay high on the Ruth Glacier to minimize crevasse exposure
Rope up before entering the glacier; unroped descent increases accident risk after early July
Carry extra slings and carabiners for self-rescue capability
The view of Mount Shuksan from Ruth includes three cascading glaciers in the valley below
watchOut: Glacier crevasses even though mostly small (10 feet max) can swallow unroped climbers. Always rope up after early July
Avalanche hazard: Use extreme caution in winter and early spring. Check Northwest Avalanche Center forecasts
Exposed scrambling sections: The approach trail involves steep, loose rock with occasional exposure
Steep seasonal snowfield: The 60-degree side traverse becomes necessary in mid-to-late season
Route finding difficulty: The scrambler's trail becomes faint in sections; use GPS if terrain becomes unclear
Weather exposure: Summit ridge position offers no shelter from rapid weather changes
knownHazards: Glacier crevasses on Ruth Glacier, typically small but increasing in frequency and size after early July
Avalanche terrain in winter and early spring around approach slopes
Loose rock and unstable footing on approach trail between Hannegan Pass and glacier toe
Steep 60-degree snow traverse required mid-to-late season east of ridge crest
Sun exposure on glacier can cause rapid dehydration and snow blindness
Rapid weather deterioration common in afternoon

road.name: Mount Baker Scenic Highway (State Route 542), Forest Service Road 32
road.status: Open May-October; may close seasonally in winter
road.seasonalGate: FR 32 may be gated in winter; check with Mount Baker Ranger District
road.driveNote: From Bellingham, take SR 542 east approximately 46 miles to 13 miles past Glacier Public Service Center. Turn left onto FR 32 (Ruth Creek Road) and continue 5.4 miles to Hannegan Campground. Surface is rough with deep potholes; high-clearance vehicles recommended.

climate.forecastZone: North Cascades alpine zone
climate.typical: Alpine climate with variable conditions and frequent precipitation. Freezing level typically 3,350-4,500 meters in summer. Wind generally light to moderate.
climate.spring: May-June: Cool temperatures, variable snow, freeze-thaw cycles. Transition season with weather instability and late-season avalanche potential.
climate.summer: July-September: Mild to cool (highs 7C, lows 4C at peak). Light rain and afternoon thunderstorms common. Glacier fully exposed but crevasses hazardous.
climate.fall: October: Cooling trend, increasing precipitation. Snow levels drop; early snow possible.
climate.winter: November-April: Heavy snow, avalanche prone, cold temperatures. Route rarely climbed.

timing.recommendedStart: 6:00 AM or earlier for day climbs

emergency.rangerStation: Mount Baker Ranger District, 10091 Mt. Baker Highway, Glacier, WA 98244, (360) 599-2714
emergency.nearestHospital: PeaceHealth St. Joseph Medical Center, 2901 Squalicum Parkway, Bellingham, WA 98264
emergency.notes: Cell service unreliable; satellite communicator recommended. Helicopter evacuation possible but response times 4+ hours. Self-rescue essential.

routes.comms: Cellular coverage unreliable at trailhead, absent on route. Satellite communicators (Garmin inReach, PLB) strongly recommended. No rescue infrastructure; self-sufficiency essential.

hierarchyNote: Elevation discrepancy: Given as 7,749 ft; Wikipedia and mountain-forecast.com state 7,756 ft. Hierarchy placement (Olympic Mountains, WA) is correct and geographically sound.
blurb: Mount Constance (7,756 ft) is the third-highest peak in Washington's Olympic Mountains, dominating Seattle's western skyline. The mountain is characterized by steep, exposed ridges and poor-quality pillow basalt rock on all sides. First ascended in 1922 by R. Schellin and A.E. Smith from the southeast, it has become a serious alpine climbing objective requiring mountaineering skills, with multiple route options ranging from Grade II to Grade III.

routes.overview: The standard route on Mount Constance, ascending via the South Chute to the summit area, then climbing the North Chimney—a 3rd class feature on the north side of the summit block. Most ascents include either the Terrible Traverse or Finger Traverse as key sections of the route.
routes.beta: From Lake Constance, ascend through Avalanche Canyon (0.6 mile) then climb the broad South Chute 1,200+ feet to the south ridge saddle. Cross to the east side and traverse north across heather, snow, and scree slopes to open terrain below the summit. Locate the North Chimney on the summit block's north side for the final 3rd class ascent to the peak.
routes.approach: From US 101 near Brinnon, take Dosewallips Road ~8.5 miles to vehicle road end (or bike the washed-out section 5+ miles). Bike or walk the abandoned Dosewallips River Road to Lake Constance Trail trailhead. Hike Lake Constance Trail 2 miles, gaining 3,250 feet through steep terrain (2009 burn area transitioning to forest) to Lake Constance at 4,650 ft. From camp, traverse Avalanche Canyon north 0.6 mile, then climb south chute. Total approach: 4-6 hours to base.
routes.descent: Descend via South Chute using plunge stepping down scree, dropping 3,600+ feet back to Avalanche Canyon and Lake Constance. Many parties descend via the Finger Traverse bypass rather than Terrible Traverse. Estimated descent time 3-4 hours to camp, then 2+ hours back to trailhead.
routes.descentText: From summit, descend east-northeast to ridge line and locate the saddle. Two main options: (1) Finger Traverse—cross exposed downsloping ledge with fingers jammed in crack (Class 3-4, high exposure); or (2) Terrible Traverse bypass via gully. Descend into South Chute notch, then plunge step down the broad scree chute (loose rock, Class 3). Cairns mark sections but snow cover can obscure them. Drop 3,600 feet to scree flats below, then re-enter Avalanche Canyon.
routes.bestSeason: July through September offer the most favorable climbing conditions with predominantly dry weather and minimal snow. June can be viable but carries significant avalanche risk from heavy maritime snow. May and earlier should be avoided due to wet, unstable snow conditions on the chutes and traverses. Best climbing is typically mid-July through September.
routes.turnaround: Most climbers establish base camp at Lake Constance and make a single summit day push or take a 2-3 day approach depending on fitness and acclimatization needs. Recommended turnaround decision point is mid-to-late afternoon to ensure descent in daylight; the upper mountain section requires 4-6 hours from Lake Constance in good conditions.
routes.bail: If severe weather is encountered on the upper mountain, the best option is to retreat to Avalanche Canyon and regroup at Lake Constance. The route offers limited natural belay/anchor sites for extended stops due to poor rock quality and terrain type. If unable to cross a traverse section safely, retrace steps back to the previous saddle or descent route.
routes.aspect: Variable; South Chute faces southeast; summit approaches from east and northeast.
routes.commitment: Grade II-III, 7-9+ hours round trip from Lake Constance
routes.fa: Peak first ascended 1922 by R. Schellin and A.E. Smith from the southeast; specific North Chimney route first-ascent history not documented in available sources.
routes.face: Southeast face via South Chute; summit approach from east side
routes.itinerary: Day 1: Hike Dosewallips Road bike-in section (1 hr via bike or 2-3 hrs on foot), then Lake Constance Trail to lake (3-4 hrs); camp overnight with permit. Day 2: Early wake (~4 AM), ascend through Avalanche Canyon and South Chute (2.5-3 hrs), traverse and climb North Chimney (1-2 hrs), summit (6-7 hrs total from camp), descend South Chute and return to camp (3-4 hrs). Day 3: Hike back down Lake Constance Trail and Dosewallips Road to vehicle (3-4 hrs).
knownHazards: Spring avalanche danger in Avalanche Canyon and South Chute from wet maritime snow (May-June)
Exposure on Terrible Traverse and Finger Traverse—fatal falls likely
Poor rock quality (pillow basalt) makes protection difficult and unreliable
Loose scree and unstable terrain throughout
Rapid weather changes common; sudden storms can reduce visibility
objHaz: Exposure to falls on narrow ridges and ledges
routes.proNeeds: Full trad rack with emphasis on slings and wedge protection rather than cams (poor rock quality). Rope recommended for belaying on technical sections and for protection on the Finger or Terrible Traverse.
proTips: Climb in September for the most stable conditions and least snow
Protect the exposed traverses on the ridge with at least one rope setup
The Finger Traverse bypass is easier than the Terrible Traverse for descent
Start very early (4 AM from camp) to ensure daylight descent
Cairns are often covered by snow or scree; careful route-finding required

road.name: Dosewallips River Road
road.status: Washed out beyond mile 8.5; road passable to vehicle by car to approximately mile 8-9, then closed due to 2002 flooding damage
road.driveNote: From US 101 near Brinnon, take Dosewallips Road east. Drive to vehicle parking at road end (~8.5 miles). Many climbers bike or walk the additional washed-out section (~5 miles) to Lake Constance trailhead. Road parking can be congested on weekends. No facilities at road end.

timing.recommendedStart: 4:00 AM from Lake Constance camp for day push; total round trip from car 18-21 hours

watchOut: Avalanche zones must be crossed quickly during warm/wet periods
The Terrible Traverse has killed climbers who fall—rope up or bypass via Finger Traverse
Descend in daylight; the route is complex and route-finding at night is dangerous
Pillow basalt rock is brittle and crumbly—place all protection carefully and test holds
whatToBring: Gloves and hat for early starts
Crampons and ice axe for spring/early season
routes.detailedRack: Bring a slim rack: nuts from small to 0.5-inch, 2-3 cams in the 1-3 inch range, 1-2 hexes, 6-8 quickdraws, 4 x 120cm slings and 6 x 60cm slings (dyneema preferred for weight), 4-6 carabiners (including 2 locking), 70m dynamic rope. Slings are more effective than wedge protection in the poor basalt; plan for natural anchors and piton stations on established lines.

climate.forecastZone: Mountain-forecast.com Mount Constance (2360m elevation)
climate.typical: Maritime Pacific Northwest climate with cool summers and mild, very wet winters. January average low ~28°F, July average high ~50°F. Significant precipitation year-round with heaviest October-March.
climate.spring: May-June: wet and mild with lingering snowpack above 5,000 ft. High avalanche danger from wet maritime snow. Rain and snow frequent; visibility often poor.
climate.summer: July-September: driest and most stable season. Daytime highs 45-55°F, nighttime lows 35-40°F. Morning clouds typically clear by late morning. Occasional fog banks; visibility generally good in afternoons.
climate.fall: September-October: transition to autumn; temperatures 35-65°F. Early snowstorms possible after mid-September. Increasing cloud cover and precipitation.
climate.winter: November-March: very wet with heavy rain and frequent snow at altitude. Temperatures 28-40°F. Rapid weather changes; storms common.

access.landManager: Olympic National Park (NPS)
access.fees: $6 reservation fee plus $8 per person per night for backcountry camping
access.permit: Wilderness backcountry permit required year-round; reserved through Recreation.gov (online June 15-Oct 15) or by phone (360) 565-3100. Groups 7+ must use designated group sites at Lake Constance

emergency.rangerStation: Olympic National Park Ranger Station, Port Angeles, WA
emergency.nearestHospital: Jefferson Healthcare Emergency Room, 834 Sheridan St, Port Townsend, WA 98368; Phone 360-385-2200
emergency.notes: Cell service is unreliable to absent in the Olympic Mountains; satellite communicator (InReach, Garmin, etc.) strongly recommended for emergencies. Helicopter rescue possible in good weather but weather windows in the Olympics are unpredictable. Park rangers monitor popular routes during peak season.

routes.overview: A renowned exposed alternate ridge traverse on Mount Constance, featuring a narrow slanting ledge where finger jams in the crack provide the primary handhold. The Finger Traverse is an alternative to the more serious Terrible Traverse and has become semi-famous among Olympic climbers for its dramatic exposure despite being technically only Class 3.
routes.beta: After summiting via the standard South Chute approach, the Finger Traverse is typically encountered on the descent. From the summit ridge near the Terrible Traverse, locate the downsloping ledge on the eastern side. Place your fingers in the horizontal crack running along the top edge and traverse carefully. The slab below is steep and exposure is extreme—a fall would be fatal. The traverse exits to easier terrain and rejoins the descent route.
routes.approach: Approach via standard Lake Constance trailhead and Avalanche Canyon route. Climb South Chute to summit area. The Finger Traverse section is accessed from the summit ridge during descent.
routes.descent: The Finger Traverse IS part of the descent. After traversing the exposed ledge, descend to easier terrain and continue down to Avalanche Canyon via the South Chute.
routes.descentText: From the exposure of the Finger Traverse, downslope carefully to the next ledge system. Some parties rope up through this section due to the high consequence of a slip. Continue traversing left and down to open scree slopes, then descend the South Chute back toward Avalanche Canyon.
routes.bestSeason: Late June through September; avoid May due to heavy snow coverage on the ledges and higher avalanche danger
routes.turnaround: The Finger Traverse must be crossed during descent; turnaround decisions should be made at the summit or earlier approach sections. If crossing the Finger Traverse is not possible due to snow, ice, or personal comfort, the Terrible Traverse bypass (via gully) is the alternative.
routes.bail: If the Finger Traverse is judged too dangerous (wet conditions, rime ice, high winds), retreat before reaching the ledge and locate the gully bypass that leads around the traverse section via easier terrain.
routes.commitment: Grade III, Class 3-4; 15-20 minutes to traverse (longer if roped or conditions poor)
routes.fa: Specific first ascent of the Finger Traverse route not documented in available sources; became popular as an alternative to the Terrible Traverse
routes.itinerary: The Finger Traverse is a descent feature, typically encountered during the return on the same day as ascent via the South Chute route. Plan 1-2 hours for this section including time for careful route-finding and protection setup if using a rope.
knownHazards: Extreme exposure to falls below the ledge
Downsloping surface with limited friction in wet conditions
Rime ice or light snow can make holds treacherous
Mental challenge of crossing a very exposed, committing feature
objHaz: Wet or icy conditions on the slab
Route-finding on descent (locating the proper descent line)
routes.proNeeds: Most parties cross without protection, relying on friction and finger jams. Roping up is recommended for inexperienced climbers or in wet conditions; pitons or cams can be placed at the start of the traverse for anchor points.
proTips: The handhold crack at the top is bomber and reliable
Foot placements are minimal; trust your friction
Some parties install hand-lines (webbing/rope) through this section for future use
Crossing in dry conditions is much easier; avoid after rain
Mental preparation is as important as technical skill here

road.name: Dosewallips River Road
road.status: Washed out beyond mile 8.5
road.driveNote: Standard access via Dosewallips Road; see North Chimney route for details

timing.recommendedStart: 4:00 AM from Lake Constance for full day push

watchOut: Do not climb this exposed section in wet or icy conditions
A slip or fall from the Finger Traverse will result in death
Carefully test every handhold and foothold before committing weight
Rope up if you are uncomfortable with the commitment level
routes.detailedRack: If roping: 2-3 slings for anchors at the start of the traverse, 4-6 quickdraws, carabiners, and a 70m rope. Many parties traverse unroped but should place protection if their skill level warrants it.

routes.overview: A prominent alpine rock ridge rising directly above Crystal Pass at the head of Avalanche Canyon, climbing approximately 1,100 feet of mostly 4th class and low 5th class terrain to the summit ridge crest of Mount Constance. The West Arête is more technical and exposed than the standard South Chute route, featuring multiple rock pitches with poor-quality basalt rock and dramatic vertical exposure.
routes.beta: From Crystal Pass (base of the West Arête), begin a rising traverse on the right side of the ridge crest at ~10:30 AM (typical timing). Terrain involves fairly broken Class 2 and 3 with occasional 4th or low 5th steps. Bypass towers via a ledge on the right side. Reach the crux pitch on the arête proper—traverse up and right on a groove (5.4), clip existing pitons, move straight up (5.4) then back left to a small alcove below a chimney. Anchor and ascend the chimney leftward to a sloping ledge, then traverse left to easier ground. Continue to ridge crest with talus climbing above, then cross to east side following the standard route descent to summit horn.
routes.approach: From Lake Constance trailhead: Hike Lake Constance Trail 2 miles (3,250 ft gain) to Lake Constance camp (4,650 ft). From camp, traverse through Avalanche Canyon approximately 1.5 miles on scree and talus, ascending slowly toward Crystal Pass. At Crystal Pass (6,200 ft), establish the base of the West Arête. This approach section takes 2.5-3 hours from camp through variable snow, talus, and steep scree.
routes.descent: From the summit, descend via the standard South Chute route or retrace steps back down the West Arête ridge (more difficult for descent). Most parties descend the South Chute on the opposite side of the peak, dropping back through Avalanche Canyon to Lake Constance. The descent typically involves either the Finger Traverse (Class 4, exposed) or the Terrible Traverse alternative.
routes.descentText: Rappel or downclimb back to Crystal Pass via the West Arête is possible but exposed and difficult for descent. Most climbers descend the opposite (south/southeast) side via the established South Chute route to Avalanche Canyon. From summit, descend east and drop down to the south ridge saddle, then locate the South Chute notch, and plunge-step down the scree chute (3,600+ feet of descent).
routes.bestSeason: Late July through September; early June possible with careful planning but snow makes the route finding difficult and travel slower. September is ideal with typical clear skies and stable weather.
routes.turnaround: Most climbers should aim for early afternoon summits on the West Arête to ensure sufficient time for the exposed descent. Turnaround decisions should be made by 2-3 PM to descend in safe daylight conditions.
routes.bail: If the crux section is encountered with poor snow coverage, icing, or deemed unsafe, parties can bypass to the right via easier scree/talus terrain before reaching the main climbing. Downclimbing Class 4-5 terrain in poor conditions is serious; reversing to Crystal Pass and descending the approach route is safer than pressing forward.
routes.aspect: West-facing ridge with variable aspects at different elevations
routes.commitment: Grade III, 5.4 rock, ~1,100 feet of climbing; 5-7 hours from Crystal Pass to summit
routes.fa: Specific first ascent history for the West Arête not documented in available sources; established as a climbing route and described in guidebooks; similar vintage to other Constance routes (early-to-mid 1900s likely).
routes.itinerary: Day 1: Bike/hike Dosewallips Road and Lake Constance Trail to camp (5-6 hours); overnight at Lake Constance with permit. Day 2: Early start (~4 AM), traverse to Crystal Pass base (2.5-3 hrs), climb West Arête (5-7 hrs to summit), descend via South Chute to camp (3-4 hrs); total 12-16 hours from camp. Day 3: Hike down Lake Constance Trail and Dosewallips Road to vehicle (3-4 hours). Climbers attempting this as a single push from the car should expect 20+ hours total.
knownHazards: Poor-quality pillow basalt rock that is brittle and difficult to protect
Significant exposure on the upper ridge—falls would be fatal
Weather-dependent route; fog and rain make climbing and especially descent dangerous
Wet rock from precipitation makes friction and protection unreliable
Descent via South Chute on opposite side requires complex route-finding in variable conditions
objHaz: Weather changes rapidly; storms can move in quickly
Poor rock quality makes protection marginal

pitchDetail.notes: Lower ridge approach, traverse right via ledge on right side of towers
Bypass section around main towers; established traverse route
Crux pitches: traverse up and right on groove clipping existing pitons, straight up then back left to alcove below chimney
Chimney and upper ridge to summit area; talus and scree climb to easier ground

routes.proNeeds: Wedge protection (slings, nuts) more effective than cams in the poor basalt; slings often preferred for speed and protection reliability. Existing pitons mark the crux pitch. Belays on natural anchors and established chock stone positions.
proTips: Climb in September for best weather stability and drying conditions
The 5.4 crux pitch goes quicker than expected; manage time carefully to avoid a slow-paced day
Existing piton marks on the crux pitch—place them early and rap back if the line feels above your level
Avoid this route in wet or icy conditions; the poor rock becomes very dangerous
The descent via the opposite South Chute involves significant route-finding; study the route during ascent
routes.rappels: Possible to rappel the West Arête via existing piton placements and natural anchors, but most parties downclimb or descend the alternate South Chute route.

road.name: Dosewallips River Road
road.status: Washed out beyond mile 8.5 since 2002 flooding
road.driveNote: Drive to vehicle parking at end of maintained road (~8.5 miles from US 101); bike or hike additional ~5 miles to Lake Constance trailhead via washed-out section of road.

timing.recommendedStart: Very early (3-4 AM) from Lake Constance camp

watchOut: Poor-quality basalt rock crumbles easily; test all holds and placements carefully
Exposure on the upper ridge is extreme; concentrate fully on the route and belay secure placement
Descending the South Chute on the opposite side requires careful route-finding; mark the descent line during ascent
Storms develop quickly in the maritime climate; have a clear descent plan
whatToBring: Ice axe and crampons for early/late season
Chalk bag and brush for basalt holds
routes.detailedRack: Slim alpine rack: nuts (1/4 to 0.5 inch sizes), 2-3 cams (1-3 inch range), 1-2 hexes, slings (4 x 120cm dyneema, 6 x 60cm dyneema recommended for weight), carabiners (6-8, including 2 locking), quickdraws (6-8), 70m dynamic rope. Slings and wedges preferred to cams for the poor basalt. Existing pitons on crux can supplement your rack.

routes.overview: The Terrible Traverse is a serious and exposed section on Mount Constance's descent, featuring a steep snow cirque above a massive cliff drop where a fall would certainly be fatal. Named for its extreme exposure and avalanche danger, it is an alternative to the Finger Traverse and presents a committing alpine climbing challenge that demands strong snow travel skills and careful decision-making.
routes.beta: The Terrible Traverse is typically encountered during descent from the summit. Parties locate the Australia-shaped snowfield (exposed ridge line with precipitous drop below). The traverse crosses steep, exposed snow above a massive cliff. Early season parties must be equipped with ice axes and crampons; self-arrest techniques and belay skills are essential. Many climbers place pickets for protection or opt for the Finger Traverse bypass instead.
routes.approach: Approach via standard Lake Constance route and Avalanche Canyon/South Chute ascent to summit area. The Terrible Traverse is a descent feature, not typically an ascent route.
routes.descent: The Terrible Traverse IS the descent section. From the summit ridge, locate the Australia-shaped snowfield. Descend very carefully onto the snow, managing exposure and maintaining control. Cramponed descent or glissading may be possible depending on snow conditions. Cross the exposed section and exit to safer terrain below.
routes.descentText: From the exposure, descend extremely carefully on the steep snow, using ice axe self-arrest skills and possibly placing pickets for protection. The exposure is absolute—a fall is fatal. Below the main traverse section, terrain opens up to scree and talus for plunge-stepping descent. Return to Avalanche Canyon and Lake Constance.
routes.bestSeason: July through September when the snowfield is firm but not icy; June carries excessive avalanche risk from heavy maritime snow. After August, some years may have less permanent snow on the traverse.
routes.turnaround: The Terrible Traverse must be crossed during descent; the summit should only be summited if climbers are confident they can reverse the traverse or successfully descend it. Turnaround decisions must be made early (by 2-3 PM) to ensure descent in daylight.
routes.bail: If the Terrible Traverse is judged too dangerous due to avalanche risk, snow conditions, or personal ability, parties should not summit. The Finger Traverse bypass route (Class 4 exposed ledge to the south) offers an alternative descent, but it is also serious. Retreat to Lake Constance is the safest option if doubt exists.
routes.aspect: North-facing snowfield, variable exposure depending on conditions
routes.commitment: Grade III, steep snow travel; 30-45 minutes to cross depending on conditions
routes.fa: Not a specific first ascent line; named for its characteristics rather than a particular party. The route is the standard/modern line for descent on multiple ascent routes.
routes.face: North-facing snow cirque on summit ridge
gear: Belay/rappel equipment if pickets will be placed
routes.itinerary: The Terrible Traverse is a descent feature typically encountered during the same-day round trip from Lake Constance. Plan 30-60 minutes for this section depending on snow conditions and whether protection (pickets) is placed.
knownHazards: Avalanche danger on the steep snowfield (especially May-June with wet maritime snow)
Exposure is absolute—fatal fall zone
Rime ice coating the snow can make footing treacherous
Cornices can form on the ridge above the traverse
Route-finding difficulty in whiteout or poor visibility
routes.proNeeds: Pickets (snow anchors) are recommended for belaying on the traverse section. Ice axe and crampons are mandatory equipment. Rope is advisable for protection, particularly in early season.
proTips: Climb in mid-to-late July through August for most stable snow conditions
Avoid May and June due to wet, unstable maritime snow and high avalanche risk
Place pickets and run a rope belay if you are uncomfortable with the exposure
Glissading the descent is possible in summer conditions if the snow is consolidated
Many experienced parties choose the Finger Traverse bypass instead of the Terrible Traverse
routes.rappels: Possible to rappel the traverse section if pickets are placed, though most parties downclimb or traverse carefully without rappelling.

road.name: Dosewallips River Road
road.status: Washed out beyond mile 8.5
road.driveNote: Standard access; see North Chimney route

routes.rockGrade: Class 3-4 with significant snow; snow steepness 45-55 degrees

timing.recommendedStart: 4:00 AM from Lake Constance

watchOut: Do not attempt this route in May or June; avalanche danger is extreme
A slip on this section is fatal; full concentration required
Rime ice can form in cold, humid conditions overnight; be prepared for icy snow
Cornices often form on the ridge above the traverse; avoid climbing or traversing directly under them
If the snow feels unstable (warm, heavy, or showing signs of recent slides), retreat
routes.detailedRack: For protection on the traverse: 1-2 snow pickets (or full snow anchor system), carabiners, sling, 50-70m rope. Ice axe and crampons are primary gear.

climate.spring: Wet and mild; significant avalanche danger May-June
climate.summer: July-August: dry, stable weather; September: excellent but cooling trend
climate.fall: Cooling, increasing precipitation
climate.winter: Heavy snow and rain; unstable

access.landManager: Olympic National Park

emergency.rangerStation: Olympic NP

routes.overview: Technically moderate alpine climb accessing Mount Index's main peak from the north side, combining scrambling and technical rock climbing through mixed terrain to the 5,991-foot summit.
routes.beta: The main peak is most commonly accessed via the East Route from Lake Serene, though north-side approaches exist via Proctor Creek drainage. This route involves mixed Class 4-5 scrambling with significant brushy sections and exposure.
routes.approach: From US-2 at Milepost 35.2, turn south on Mount Index Road and drive 0.3 miles to Lake Serene trailhead (600 ft). Hike Lake Serene trail 3.6 miles (1.5-2 hours, 2,000 ft gain) to the lake. For north-side approach: Access via Proctor Creek drainage from the east, a less-traveled approach requiring route-finding skills.
routes.descent: Downclimb via the same route; the descent can be challenging in poor visibility. Single rappels may be needed on steeper sections. Plan for significant time finding the correct descent route.
routes.descentText: Retrace the ascent route carefully, as route-finding on the summit area can be difficult in clouds. Some parties use rappels (30-40 feet) to descend particularly steep sections. The brushy sections below are harder on descent and require caution for rockfall.
routes.bestSeason: June-September, with June-July preferred when snow covers the lower brushy terrain. October can work in dry years. Avoid winter and early spring due to avalanche hazard in the gullies.
routes.fa: First recorded ascent 29 October 1911 by H. B. Hinman, Ernest Martin, Lee Pickett and George E. Wright
gear: Trad rack (single set micronuts to #3 Camalot)
routes.detailedRack: Single set of nuts (micronuts through size 1), single set of cams (micros through #3), 2-4 alpinedraws, 2 slings, rappel anchor webbing. Minimal protection on most pitches.
routes.proNeeds: Moderate alpine rock protection. Most sections use minimal pro; crux may take a few pieces. Good anchor sites can be sparse.
watchOut: Route-finding on descent is very difficult; mark waypoints on ascent
Brushy sections create rockfall hazard; watch for rocks from above
Wet, downsloping slab sections are slippery; socks can wick moisture
Poor visibility often covers this peak; rely on terrain memory
knownHazards: Loose rock and poor rock quality on upper sections
Brushy steep terrain lacking secure protection
Exposure to rockfall from adjacent slopes
Route-finding difficulty especially in poor weather
proTips: Mark waypoints or paint route markers on ascent to aid descent navigation
Start early; the descent is tricky and requires good light
In spring/early season, snow covers brush and makes approach faster
Approach via Lake Serene is more frequently traveled and better marked than north approaches
This route is rarely climbed compared to the East Route; expect minimal established rappel anchors
routes.comms: No reliable cell service at trailhead (600 ft) or on approach; satellite communicator recommended for emergency contact.
routes.turnaround: Turn around by 2 PM if still climbing after 5 hours; summit will not be reached by safe descent time.

timing.recommendedStart: 4:00 AM or earlier to summit by early afternoon
