road.driveNote: US-2 is passable year-round. Mount Index Road is short and usually accessible even in winter; check with USFS if concerned.

climate.forecastZone: NWAC Cascade Range - Mount Index Area
climate.typical: Marine west coast climate with high precipitation. Afternoon thunderstorms common in summer. Heavy winter snow accumulation.
climate.spring: April-May: Variable, rapid snowmelt, avalanche hazard high in gullies.
climate.summer: June-September: Generally stable weather with afternoon thunderstorms. High UV exposure.
climate.fall: October-November: Frequent rain and sudden weather changes. Snow possible above 4,000 ft.
climate.winter: December-March: Heavy snow, icing, avalanche hazard. Ascent attempted rarely and with high risk.

emergency.nearestHospital: Evergreen Hospital (Snoqualmie area): 35 miles south; Cascade Hospital (Marblemount): 30 miles east
emergency.rangerStation: Mount Baker-Snoqualmie National Forest Ranger District, North Bend: 425-888-1421
emergency.notes: Helicopter rescue from high alpine terrain may be difficult due to terrain and weather. Self-rescue or ground evacuation typically required.

routes.corrections: Route name 'Main Peak via North Approach' does not exactly match documented route names in climbing literature. Most frequent climbing on main peak is via East Route/Hourglass Gully from Lake Serene, not a north approach. Proctor Creek drainage (east approach from that side) exists but is less common. This route's exact documentation is limited; information synthesized from north-side access descriptions and general Mount Index climbing guidance.

routes.overview: Major big-wall alpine climbing on the north-facing buttress of Mount Index's middle peak, rising nearly 2,000 feet above Lake Serene. Complex aid climbing with mixed free and aid sections on mostly solid granodiorite.
routes.beta: The North Norwegian Buttress is one of the larger walls in the Lower 48, plainly visible from Highway 2. Multiple big-wall routes exist on this face, ranging from Grade VI 5.9 A3+ (Jötnar) to free climbing lines (Bluebell, 5.13-). The buttress is separated from the South Norwegian Buttress by a deep cleft between the Main and Middle Peaks. Most parties approach via Lake Serene.
routes.approach: From US-2 at Milepost 35.2, turn south on Mount Index Road and drive 0.3 miles to Lake Serene trailhead (600 ft). Hike the well-maintained Lake Serene trail 3.6 miles (1.5-2 hours, 2,000 ft elevation gain) to the north end of the lake. From the lake, follow an unofficial trail around to the right (east) side of the lake, descending granite slabs to talus (~15 minutes). Fill water at the lake. Head up talus to the base of the wall (~15 minutes). The buttress base sits at approximately 4,800 ft elevation.
routes.descent: Rappel the route in its entirety (or mostly). Many pitches overhang significantly, requiring swings, pendulums, or directional gear to reach the next rappel station. Double ropes aid efficiency on steep terrain.
routes.descentText: Complete rappel descent using bolted/established anchor points (most pitches now have bolted anchors from recent ascents). Many rappels involve pendulums due to overhanging terrain; place directional gear or slings before rappelling. Pitches 5-6 (if wet from afternoon seepage) may require multiple rappels. Some climbers fixed ropes on steeper sections to facilitate retreat. The rappel descent usually takes 2-4 hours depending on anchor quality and rope management.
routes.itinerary: Day 1: Hike to Lake Serene and base of buttress. Establish bivy or advanced base camp at talus (~4,800 ft). Day 2-4: Climb pitches 1-16 with bivy on portaledge between pitches. Climb first thing to avoid afternoon water flow on seepage pitches. Day 5: Final pitches to summit, then rappel descent to base.
routes.bail: On lower pitches (1-5), simul-climb down or rappel. On upper sections (8+), fixed rappel stations provide exit, though retrieving ropes may be difficult. In emergency, communicate with helicopter rescue via satellite device.
routes.bestSeason: Late July through early September. June is too snowy; October has increased rockfall risk from freeze-thaw cycles.
routes.fa: Doorish Route (Pete Doorish, 1985, VI 5.9 A3, unrepeated); Jötnar (Sam Boyce, Lani Chapko, Kyle Willis, August 2020, VI 5.9 A3+)
gear: Double rack nuts (micronuts to #4 Camalot plus single #5)
routes.detailedRack: Full big-wall trad rack: double set of nuts from micronuts through size 1, micros through #4 Camalot (plus single #5 for wider cracks), single offset set, 5 beaks, 2 lost arrows, 10 rivet hangers, 2 sets of climbing hooks. Complete aid climbing hardware for sustained A3+ climbing. Portaledge essential due to no natural ledges.
routes.proNeeds: Advanced aid climbing skills required. Pitches 4 and 9 are crux aid sections (A3+). Most pitches require deliberate protection placement; some sections are poorly protected and runout. Route quality is generally good on Jötnar, which has bolted anchors.
whatToBring: Water (minimum 2 liters per person per day)
watchOut: Afternoon snow melt creates waterfalls on Pitches 5-6; climb these first in early morning
Runout sections on P1 and P14; fall potential and exposure are serious
Overhanging terrain creates rope management challenges; plan pendulums carefully
Some rivets/bolts may need retorquing if route sees increased traffic
Rapid temperature drop when sun leaves cirque; bring extra clothing
knownHazards: Afternoon seepage becomes waterfalls; water hazard on lower pitches
Multiple long overhanging pitches; rope drag severe
Sparse natural anchors; often aid-climbing to gain
Rockfall risk on lower pitches from loose blocks
Exposure on all pitches, 2,000 ft to talus below
proTips: Climb Pitches 5-6 early morning before they become waterfalls from snow melt
Jötnar is the only Grade VI big wall in the Pacific Northwest with bolted anchors and repeatable in 3-4 days
Use double ropes on pendulum sections to avoid rope snagging
Mark bivy spots on the way up for easy return
This is a committing route; retreat from above halfway point is difficult
Late July-Early September is the target window; water flow and snow are minimal then
routes.comms: No cell service at elevation. Satellite communicator (InReach, Spot) strongly recommended for multi-day wall climb.
routes.turnaround: If not past Pitch 8 by end of Day 2, consider descent and regrouping. Do not push for summit in declining weather.

timing.recommendedStart: Early morning Day 1 for bivy at base; ascent Days 2-4

routes.corrections: Database lists Grade V; documented routes on this buttress (Jötnar, Doorish Route) are Grade VI, not V. Database shows 5.9 rock grade, which is accurate for harder free sections, but primary grade is VI 5.9 A3+. The 'North Norwegian Buttress' is more accurately described as a wall containing multiple routes (Jötnar, Doorish Route, Voodoo Project, Bluebell); this entry likely refers to the Jötnar route specifically or the Doorish Route historically.

routes.overview: Alpine rock climbing on the northeast-facing aspect of Mount Index, featuring Class 4-5 scrambling and mixed rock pitches on primarily solid granite.
routes.beta: This route is less frequently documented than other Index routes. The northeast face offers an intermediate alpine rock objective with mixed scrambling and roped pitches. Route quality and exact character are variable; climbing conditions greatly affect difficulty.
routes.approach: From US-2 at Milepost 35.2, drive south on Mount Index Road 0.3 miles to Lake Serene trailhead (600 ft). Hike Lake Serene trail 3.6 miles to the lake (2,000 ft gain, 1.5-2 hours). Traverse the lake and approach the northeast-facing slopes from the east side. Route-finding to the base requires good terrain assessment.
routes.descent: Downclimb via the same line, or descend via nearby gullies to regain the talus. Single rappels may be used on steeper sections. Careful route-finding essential for descent.
routes.descentText: Retrace the route or descend via adjacent gully systems to the talus. Rappels (single 40-50 ft pitches) can speed descent but require carefully placed anchors in natural features or existing bolts if present.
routes.bestSeason: June-September; best conditions June-July when snow reduces brush.
gear: Trad rack (single set micronuts to #2 Camalot)
routes.detailedRack: Single set of nuts (micronuts through size 1), single set of cams (size 0 through #2), 2-3 alpinedraws, slings. Light protection; most climbing is scrambling.
routes.proNeeds: Light alpine rock protection. Most pitches feature minimal pro opportunities; natural features and sparse bolts provide anchors. Solid route-finding and class 4-5 climbing skills essential.
watchOut: Route-finding on northeast aspect can be confusing; mark waypoints
Loose rock common on less-traveled aspects
Limited protection on many pitches; exposure significant
Weather can change rapidly; mist frequently covers this face
knownHazards: Loose and rotten rock sections
Limited protection opportunities
Exposure on all pitches with significant consequences for falls
Poor visibility common on northeast-facing slopes
proTips: This is a less-visited route; expect minimal established anchors
Start very early for good light throughout the climb
Mark your route carefully on ascent for descent reference
Rope up for all Class 5 sections; ground-up protection-only ethics
Combining with traverse to North Peak is possible for strong parties
routes.comms: No cell service at elevation; satellite device recommended.
routes.turnaround: Turn around by 2 PM if not past halfway mark; summit must be reached by 3-4 PM for safe descent.

timing.recommendedStart: 4:00 AM or earlier

routes.corrections: This route does not appear in comprehensive climbing databases (Mountain Project, SummitPost, AAC Publications, or Beckey guide). The specific route 'Northeast Buttress' with Grade IV 5.8 rating could not be verified through multiple authoritative sources. This may be an alternate name for another documented route (such as 'North Face of North Peak,' which is Grade III 5.6, or the 'East Face of Middle Peak,' which is Class V), a locally-known climbing line not widely documented, or a route name that requires clarification.

blurb: Mount Index is a stunning 5,991-foot granite peak in Washington's North Cascades, comprising three pointed spires that rise abruptly above Highway 2 and Lake Serene. Made of granodiorite locally known as Index granite, the peak features the impressive Norwegian Buttresses—major big-wall climbs rising 2,000 feet above the lake. First recorded ascent of the main peak was October 29, 1911 by H.B. Hinman, Ernest Martin, Lee Pickett, and George E. Wright. The north peak saw its first ascent in 1929 via the North Face route (Grade III, 5.6) by Lionel Chute and Victor Kaartinen. Today, Mount Index offers a dozen routes ranging from moderate alpine scrambles to world-class big-wall aid climbing.
hierarchyNote: Peak verified: Mount Index, 5,991 feet, King County, Washington, North Cascades region. Coordinates 47°46′28″N 121°34′51″W confirmed via multiple sources. Hierarchy is correct. Note: The three routes provided in the database present documentation challenges. 'Main Peak via North Approach' and 'Northeast Buttress' do not appear in authoritative climbing literature. 'North Norwegian Buttress' is a real wall, though specific documentation often refers to named routes on the wall (Jötnar, Doorish Route, Bluebell) rather than using that name as a single route designation.

blurb: Luna Peak is the highest mountain in the Picket Range, an extremely rugged subrange of the North Cascades in Washington, located within North Cascades National Park. Known locally as offering "one of the best views in Washington," the peak features dramatic local relief and rises over 6,560 feet above McMillan Creek to the south. The mountain is rarely climbed despite its relatively accessible approach, due to challenging bushwhacking and complex route-finding requirements.

routes.fa: Bill Cox and Will F. Thompson (1938)
routes.overview: Classic mountaineering climb of the Picket Range's highest peak via southeast ridge and Luna Cirque, involving Class 3 exposed scrambling, glacier and snowfield travel, and significant route-finding challenges.
routes.beta: Access from Luna Cirque via Access Creek, ascend steep gullies to southeast ridge, traverse to southwest ridge with exposed Class 3 summit traverse on loose rock. Crux is 5 feet exposed between false and true summit.
routes.approach: From Highway 20, access Ross Lake trailhead. Hike or water taxi to Big Beaver Creek, follow trail 10 miles to Luna Camp (~5,800 ft). Cross Big Beaver Creek, bushwhack up Access Creek's north ridgeline to 3,900 ft, cross creek, follow cairns across talus to avoid brush. From Access Creek headwaters at 4,400 ft, ascend southwest up broad talus or snow gullies toward Luna Col.
routes.descent: Downclimb exposed southwest ridge (Class 3), staying near east side crest. Descend Class 2 talus to false summit, downclimb 50 feet to Luna Col. Traverse southeast across basin back to Access Creek headwaters, reverse ascent route back to Luna Camp.
routes.descentText: Carefully downclimb exposed Class 3 ridge with 500+ feet exposure, maintaining careful foot placement on loose rock. Stay on or below ridge crest on east side. Downclimb 50 feet from false summit to Col. Talus descent is loose; watch for rockfall. Allow 3-4 hours descent to Luna Camp.
routes.itinerary: Day 1: Drive to Ross Lake, hike/taxi to Big Beaver Creek, 10 miles to Luna Camp (5-7 hrs, +3,000 ft), overnight. Day 2: Cross creek, bushwhack/scramble up Access Creek (1-2 hrs), ascend to summit (5-7 hrs), descend to Luna Camp (3-4 hrs, +/-2,000 ft). Day 3: Luna Camp to trailhead (5-6 hrs, -3,000 ft).
routes.bail: Before reaching Luna Col, retreat via ascent route. If above Col, carefully downclimb exposed ridge to basin then Access Creek. Exposed sections require extreme caution due to loose rock and drop.
routes.turnaround: Plan to summit by early afternoon for safe descent. Do not continue if more than 2 hours from summit by 2 PM. Weather deteriorates rapidly; 15-minute window can close within minutes.
routes.bestSeason: June-September best. July-August most stable (50-60F), though snow/ice remain in cirques. June requires additional snow/ice skills. September more stable, shorter days.
routes.comms: Cell/satellite communication absent in remote Picket Range. Satellite messengers (InReach, SPOT) strongly recommended for emergency use. Contact rangers before departure.
proTips: Overnight at Luna Camp minimizes summit-day energy expenditure.
5-foot crux between summits is dramatically exposed; practice downclimbing.
Cairns mark approach but hard to spot in poor visibility; mark ascent.
Early start essential; summit window closes fast.
Loose talus abundant; watch for rockfall.
watchOut: Loose rock abundant; maintain vigilance to avoid rockfall.
Final ridge has 500+ feet exposure on unpredictable footing.
Big Beaver Creek fording dangerous in high water.
5-mile bushwhack notoriously disorienting.
Wet snow increases avalanche danger.
knownHazards: Avalanche danger on steep gullies when snow present
Rockfall from unstable talus
Exposure and fall hazard on Class 3 ridge
Complex route-finding; visibility loss causes disorientation
Big Beaver Creek crossing can become impassable
routes.detailedRack: Ice axe and crampons essential for snow/ice. Optional: 50-60m rope for belayed crux; many solo. Helmet essential. Minimal rock gear.
routes.proNeeds: Varies by experience. Single 50m rope with harness/slings for crux. Self-rescue knowledge essential.

routes.overview: A moderate glacier scramble ascending the White River/White Chuck Glacier drainage to Kololo's east summit. This is the primary standard objective for the peak, involving mostly Class 2 terrain with exposed ridge traversing on the final approach.
routes.beta: Kololo Peaks presents three viable multi-day approaches, all exceeding 12 miles and typically requiring 2-3 days with glacier travel. The standard route from the west via North Fork Sauk Trail offers the most established access, climbing through old-growth forest to the Pacific Crest Trail at White Pass, then traversing multiple glacier systems. The climb itself is straightforward Class 2-3 scrambling on talus and snow, with the primary challenge being glacier crevasse navigation and length of approach rather than technical difficulty. Neither summit presents hard climbing; the route involves walking across easy terrain between the east summit (8,243 ft, true high point) and west summit (8,220 ft).
routes.approach: From Darrington, WA, drive south on Mountain Loop Highway for ~10 miles until pavement ends. Continue 8 miles on gravel, turn left onto Sloan Creek Road. Drive 6.6 miles to North Fork Sauk River Trailhead (2,050 ft). Hike the North Fork Sauk River Trail through old-growth forest 3.5 miles to Red Creek, then 1.5 miles to Mackinaw Shelter area (4,500 ft). Trail ascends steeply 3 miles to Pacific Crest Trail junction (6,000 ft). Turn right and hike 0.25 mile to White Pass (5,900 ft). From White Pass, continue northwest traversing snowfields to notch west of Point 6,770. Descend northbound into White Chuck River Basin (6,300 ft). Continue north to open basin at 6,700 ft where high camp is established. From high camp, ascend White Chuck or White River Glacier system, gaining 1,500 ft in 2 hours to reach summit area.
routes.descent: Descend by reversing the glacier ascent, carefully navigating open crevasses. Return to high camp at 6,700 ft, then descend to White Pass. Return via North Fork Sauk Trail to trailhead. Experienced parties may loop via Foam Pass/Red Pass (adds 2 miles) for varied descent experience. Always descend in daylight; crevasse visibility diminishes in dim light.
routes.descentText: From summit at 8,240 ft, descend carefully to 7,700 ft where you can safely traverse onto glacier. Descend on firm neve to 7,200 ft where terrain transitions to snow-covered slopes. In late summer, pick your way through open crevasses (clearly visible, require careful footwork). Descend to 6,700 ft ramp/saddle, return to base camp. Break camp and descend via boot path to 6,300 ft basin. Ascend slightly to White Chuck River Basin traverse, then ascend to White Pass (5,900 ft). Descend established trail via PCT junction and North Fork Sauk River Trail to trailhead. Total descent time: 4-5 hours summit to base camp, plus 6-8 hours to trailhead.
routes.itinerary: Day 1: Drive to North Fork Sauk Trailhead. Hike 9.5 miles gaining 3,900 ft to White Pass (5,900 ft), establish first camp. Day 2: Descend to White Chuck Basin, ascend to high camp at 6,700 ft. Day 3: Early alpine start from high camp. Ascend glaciers to summit at 8,240 ft, return to high camp. Day 4: Break camp, return to White Pass, descend via North Fork Sauk Trail to trailhead.
routes.bail: From high camp at 6,700 ft, descend to White Pass provides escape. If weather deteriorates ascending glaciers below 7,000 ft, retreat to camp. Once above 7,000 ft, only option is descent via same route. In severe weather or injury above 7,200 ft, call rescue via satellite communicator.
routes.turnaround: Establish firm turnaround time of 2:00 PM from summit to ensure safe daylight descent. Turn around by 1:00 PM if base of final summit scramble not reached. Do not attempt descent in twilight; crevasse visibility critical.
routes.bestSeason: Early summer (June-early July) offers optimal conditions: snow bridges relatively stable, crevasses partially bridged, weather statistically more favorable. Late summer (August-September) has all crevasses open and visible but more ice-walking with crampons and tedious scree sections.
routes.detailedRack: Glacier travel rack requires modern crevasse rescue capability. Essential: 2-3 mechanical ice screws; modern rope system with redundant anchors; friction hitches for self-rescue. Each climber carries personal anchor system (carabiners, webbing, Petzl Nano Traxion or equivalent mechanical advantage device). Snow pickets supplement but cannot be sole anchors. Late-season (August+) with blue ice requires ice screws as mandatory.
routes.proNeeds: Solid glacier travel and crevasse rescue skills. Minimum: ability to travel glacier in 2-person teams with rope skills, setting snow/ice anchors, self-rescue procedures. Proficiency with crampons and ice axe essential. One climber should have White Chuck/White River Glacier experience. Weather assessment and avalanche terrain recognition valuable.
routes.comms: Zero cell service in Glacier Peak Wilderness. Satellite communicator (Garmin InReach, Spot) strongly recommended for emergency notification.
proTips: Early morning starts before 5 AM critical; peak exposed to afternoon sun/wind after 9 AM
Early June-July: probe carefully for bridged crevasses
Cache heavy items at high camp to reduce summit day pack weight
East summit 10-15 ft higher than west summit; reach both if conditions allow
Query recent trip reports on SummitPost for latest glacier conditions
Water sources abundant along trail and at 6,700 ft basin; treat all glacier melt
watchOut: Crevasses primary objective hazard; Honeycomb Glacier abundant crevasses; snow bridges hide danger in early season
Avalanche hazard in White Chuck Basin and Foam Pass approaches on slopes over 30 degrees
Marine west coast climate: frequent weather systems, afternoon thunderstorms common, descend exposed terrain by early afternoon
Heavy wet snow; afternoon snow becomes exhausting; early morning departure critical
Long approach means significant cumulative elevation gain; acclimatize at 6,000-6,500 ft
Late-season ascents: open visible crevasses, blue ice requiring constant crampons, running meltwater slush
knownHazards: Avalanche hazard from heavy maritime snow, particularly on slopes steeper than 30 degrees between 5,500-7,500 ft
Crevasse fields on White Chuck and White River Glaciers with hidden bridged crevasses in early/mid season
Wet heavy snow and afternoon thunderstorms creating lightning exposure hazard on upper ridges
Bergschrund and icefall potential on cirque headwalls
Whiteout conditions; navigation on featureless glacier difficult in low visibility
Rapid weather changes; clear morning can turn to heavy precipitation within 1-2 hours
Altitude effects at 8,240 ft; some climbers experience high altitude cerebral edema
Hypothermia and frostbite hazard if wet

waypoints.name: White Chuck River Basin High Camp

timing.recommendedStart: 5:00 AM from high camp

road.name: Mountain Loop Highway / Sloan Creek Road
road.status: Open May-November; closed Nov-April at Deer Creek to Bedal section
road.seasonalGate: Typical closure November 1 - May 15
road.driveNote: Narrow winding gravel after 10 miles from Darrington. High-clearance vehicles recommended. Parking at gate if closed. 1.5-2 hour drive time from Darrington.

climate.typical: Marine west coast climate with high precipitation. Summer temps 40-60F at elevation.
climate.spring: April-May: Snowpack above 6,000 ft. Road access limited. Snow bridges least stable.
climate.summer: June-September: Most stable climbing season. Clear days alternate with precipitation. Afternoon thunderstorms common. July avg temps 45-55F at altitude.
climate.fall: September-October: Earlier storms bring fresh snow. Road closures begin November.
climate.winter: November-April: Heavy snow, extreme avalanche hazard, road closures.
climate.forecastZone: West Slopes North Central Cascades (NOAA zone WAZ568)

access.landManager: Mount Baker-Snoqualmie NF and Wenatchee NF; Glacier Peak Wilderness co-managed
access.permit: Free, self-issue permits at trailhead
access.passRequired: Northwest Forest Pass
access.fees: $30 annual or $5 day pass

emergency.rangerStation: Darrington Ranger District, Mount Baker-Snoqualmie NF
emergency.nearestHospital: Skagit Valley Hospital, Mount Vernon, WA (45 miles)

blurb: Kololo Peaks is a sprawling 8,243-foot massif in the Glacier Peak Wilderness, standing three miles south of Glacier Peak on the Cascade Range crest. Straddling the Snohomish/Chelan County line, the mountain cradles four glaciers and is known for long, demanding approaches exceeding 12 miles across multiple glacier systems. The climbing itself is straightforward Class 2-3 scrambling to twin summits, making technical difficulty secondary to glacier crevasse navigation and the sheer commitment required by the remote alpine setting.", "sources": ["https://www.summitpost.org/kololo-peaks/154753", "https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Kololo_Peaks", "https://peakery.com/kololo-peaks-washington/", "https://listsofjohn.com/peak/48793", "https://www.mountaineers.org/activities/trip-reports/kololo-peaks-white-chuck-glacier", "https://trailcatjim.com/white-pass-kololo-peak-white-mtn/", "https://onehikeaweek.com/2019/06/09/kololo-peaks/", "https://stephabegg.com/trip-reports/washington/glacier-peak-circuit/", "https://www.wta.org/go-hiking/trip-reports/trip_report.2019-06-14.5750567455", "https://www.fs.usda.gov/r06/mbs/recreation/north-fork-sauk-trailhead", "https://www.skagitregionalhealth.org/locations", "https://forecast.weather.gov/MapClick.php?zoneid=WAZ568", "https://www.discovernw.org/annual-northwest-forest-pass"]

Mount Fury's East Peak (8,356 ft) is one of Washington's most remote and committing alpine objectives in the Northern Picket Range. The peak offers two distinct routes: a Class 4 glacier climb via the Southeast Glaciers and exposed ridge scrambling, requiring multi-day approach through pristine wilderness. The rock is a mix of granodiorite and quartz diorite with significant choss; first ascent by Ptarmigan Climbing Club circa 1938.
hierarchyNote: Given elevation 8,326 ft conflicts with latest theodolite survey data (8,356 ft ±8 ft, October 2022) and PeakVisor data (8,356+ ft). Peak is correctly located in North Cascades National Park, Northern Picket Range, Skagit County, coordinates 48.811653°N, 121.323096°W per PeakVisor. Hierarchy placement is geographically accurate.

routes.overview: Multi-day glacier and ridge traverse to East Peak via Southeast Glacier. Combines Class 3–4 scrambling on exposed ridge with 30–50 degree glacier ascent and significant bergschrund crossing.
routes.bestSeason: Mid-July to late August for optimal conditions. Earlier trips face deeper snow and crevasse hazards; September weather becomes unstable. June typically blocked by snow.
routes.approach: From Ross Dam Happy Flats trailhead (2,150 ft), take water taxi across Ross Lake to Big Beaver Landing ($60 each way). Hike 11.5 miles on Big Beaver Creek Trail to Luna Camp area. Locate marked log crossing of Big Beaver Creek and bushwhack upstream along Access Creek through dense vegetation (dense devil's club and hornet hazards during afternoon heat). Cross to talus fields near 3,800 ft. Continue steep scree ascent to Access Col (6,100 ft) via chute on southwestern basin side, approximately 9+ hours from trailhead. Establish camp at Access Basin (4,300 ft) or continue to Luna Pass (6,100 ft). From Luna Pass, descend and traverse south side of ridge via moderately steep heather and talus slopes. Locate Southeast Glacier at approximately 6,700 ft elevation.
routes.itinerary: Day 1: Trailhead to Access Basin (4,300 ft), 9+ hours. Day 2: Access Basin to Luna Pass (6,100 ft), 4.6 hours. Day 3: Luna Pass to East Fury summit (8,356 ft) and return to camp, 8.1 hours ascent / 6.2 hours descent. Day 4: Return descent.
routes.descent: Reverse glacier descent with running belays using snow flukes. Retrace ridge line via short rappel over 25-foot step. Return to camp via same approach.
routes.descentText: From summit, descend southeast glacier via running belay anchors with snow flukes, approximately 300 ft of 40–50 degree slope. Navigate large bergschrund via established route. Cross lower glacier slopes (30–40 degrees) with crevasse avoidance. Exit glacier to rocky rib and talus basin. Ascend terraced rock nose featuring Class 5.0 crack step (~25 feet); descend via narrow ramp on left (contrary to some older guidebook recommendations). Angle up ridge crest via better terrain than ascent routing (saves time). Short rappel over 25-foot step marks key descent point. Continue ridge traverse back to Luna Pass camp.
routes.beta: This is the standard approach to East Fury via Luna Peak traverse. The route is not technical climbing in the traditional sense but requires solid alpine skills, glacier travel proficiency, and exposure management on exposed scrambling. The terrain is predominantly Class 3–4 scrambling with one short Class 5.0 rock step (~25 feet, unroped passage is feasible). The glacier ascent is the crux: moderate 30–40 degree slopes steepen to 50+ degrees near summit with a large bergschrund requiring direct ascent. Route finding is straightforward following the ridge line and glacier, though descent via alternate terrain saves significant time over ascent route. Weather deterioration is the primary hazard—morning fog/clouds common at Luna Pass, clearing by midday.

timing.recommendedStart: 5:30 AM from Luna Pass camp

objHaz: Crevasses and bergschrund crossing on Southeast Glacier
Steep snowpatch (40–50 degrees) with poor runout near summit
Exposed scrambling on wet, mossy rock with downsloping terrain and loose blocks
Class 5.0 rock step requiring crack climbing or downclimbing
Weather deterioration—frequent morning fog/clouds at Luna Pass
knownHazards: Dense brush and devil's club during approach bushwhacking; hornets/wasps active during warm afternoon travel
Steep, loose rock along ridge with exposure to falls
Bergschrund and crevasse field on Southeast Glacier; snow bridges may fail under load
Steep summit snowpatch with insufficient runout for self-arrest; falls result in serious injury
Hazardous ridge traverse with steep drop-offs; possible wet, mossy downsloping rock on descent
watchOut: Do not underestimate the remoteness and length of this climb—each day is long and strenuous; turnaround time is critical
The bergschrund crossing requires careful route finding; direct ascent is steeper but safer than lateral traverse
Descent is more complex than ascent due to terrain; use running belays on steep glacier sections
Weather can change rapidly; morning clouds and fog are common—summit conditions often better in afternoon
proTips: Plan three days minimum: Day 1 approach, Day 2 Luna Pass camp, Day 3 summit attempt with good early start (5:30 AM typical)
Use running belays with snow flukes during glacier descent to save time and manage exposure
Ascend the right side of the Southeast Glacier; the left side has steeper terrain and more objective hazard
The Class 5.0 rock step can be downclimbed or rope-protected; unroped passage is feasible for experienced scramblers
Carry a satellite communicator or inReach device—cell coverage is minimal to non-existent; emergency extraction is helicopter-based (expensive and weather-dependent)
gear: Ice axe (for self-arrest on heather and glacier)
Two 25-meter ropes (one used for short rappel, one for running belays)
Small rock rack (nuts and cams for Class 5.0 step; 2–3 pieces sufficient)
Two snow flukes (for glacier protection and running belays)
Helmet (mandatory for rockfall protection during descent and exposed scrambling)
Gaiters and gloves (essential for brush and snow travel)
Insulated water bottles (limited water availability during ridge traverse)
whatToBring: 4-season tent or bivy shelter (weather can be severe even in summer)
Sleeping bag rated to 0°F (nighttime temperatures drop significantly at high elevation)
Food and fuel for 4 days (resupply impossible; Access Creek water during approach, abundant at Luna Pass)
Emergency shelter/bivy (in case of emergency descent or unexpected storm)
First aid kit with blister treatment and pain management
Navigation: map (Cascade Peak topographic), compass, and GPS device (backup to visual route finding)
Sunscreen and lip balm (extreme UV exposure at altitude and on snow)
Avalanche beacon, probe, and shovel (minimal avalanche risk, but conditions-dependent)
Trekking poles (helpful for approach bushwhacking and descent load management)
routes.detailedRack: Small, selective rock rack: 2–3 nuts (sizes #4–#6 Black Diamond or equivalent), 2–3 cams (sizes #0–#2 for the Class 5.0 crack step). Rock quality is variable (mix of good granodiorite and loose choss); expect poor protection and friable anchors. Primarily a protection rack for the single short technical step; most route management via scrambling and glacier travel.
routes.proNeeds: Minimal rock climbing protection needed. The Class 5.0 rock step can be approached unroped by experienced alpinists. One 25-meter rope can be established for the short rappel descent. Glacier travel relies on snow anchor technology (flukes) rather than rock protection. Confidence on exposed Class 3–4 scrambling and efficient rope management required; this is an expedition-style climb.

road.name: North Cascades Highway (WA-20)
road.status: Open year-round (occasional winter closures east of Ross Lake for avalanche work)
road.seasonalGate: Ross Dam area accessible via WA-20; no seasonal gate, but snow may block some side roads in winter
road.driveNote: Drive to Happy Flats trailhead on WA-20 near Ross Dam (2,150 ft). Parking available. Water taxi (Ross Lake Shuttle) departs Big Beaver area; fare $60 each way, weather-dependent, advance reservation recommended during peak season

climate.forecastZone: Northwest Avalanche Center (NWAC) Picket Range/North Cascades zone
climate.typical: Highly variable; expect frequent weather changes. Summer monsoon influences bring afternoon thunderstorms and clouds. Glaciers stable mid-July through late August; earlier months carry higher avalanche risk.
climate.spring: Not recommended before early June; deep snow, extensive crevassing, and avalanche risk high. Route is passable but requires specialized skills and hazard mitigation.
climate.summer: Most stable season. Mid-July to late August optimal: daytime temperatures 35–50°F, nighttime -10 to 15°F. Snow coverage diminishes through August, revealing more rock and scree. Afternoon clouds and occasional thunderstorms common but brief.
climate.fall: September weather destabilizes: increased cloud cover, rain at lower elevations transitions to early season snow above 7,000 ft. By late September, winter conditions return.
climate.winter: Route is under 10+ feet of snow. Winter ascents are expert-only expeditions requiring avalanche expertise and specialized winter mountaineering skills.

access.landManager: National Park Service (North Cascades National Park)
access.permit: Backcountry permit required for overnight camping. Permits available through Recreation.gov or in person at Wilderness Information Center. Non-refundable $6 fee per permit + $10 per-person per-night recreation fee (youth under 15 free). Reserve early in season (July–August often fully booked).
access.fees: $6 permit reservation fee + $10 per-person per-night backcountry fee. Water taxi: $60 each way. Optional: guided service via Mountain School or Alpine Institute available.
access.passRequired: America the Beautiful Pass ($80/year) or $35 single-entry fee for day-use trailhead parking (if not camping)
access.closures: Cross-country zones (Fury approach) can be closed mid-winter due to avalanche hazard. Check with park rangers at (360) 854-7245 or noca_wilderness@nps.gov before planning. North Cascades Highway (WA-20) occasionally closed for avalanche work or rockfall; plan flexibility.

emergency.rangerStation: North Cascades National Park Wilderness Information Center: (360) 854-7245, noca_wilderness@nps.gov. After-hours: (360) 873-4590 ext. 37
emergency.nearestHospital: Skagit Valley Hospital (Mount Vernon, ~40 miles): (360) 424-4111. Cascade Valley Hospital (Concrete, ~25 miles): (360) 853-4585
emergency.notes: This is an extremely remote peak; helicopter rescue is the only evacuation option, weather-dependent, and very expensive (often $20k+). Carry satellite communicator or inReach device. Cell coverage is minimal to non-existent. Pre-trip communication via sat device recommended.

routes.comms: No cell phone coverage in the Picket Range; carriers (Verizon, AT&T, T-Mobile) do not reach this remote alpine zone. Recommend satellite communicator (Garmin inReach, SPOT device) or Iridium satellite phone for emergency contact. Consider pre-positioned check-in protocols with friends or family.
routes.corrections: Given elevation (8,326 ft) differs slightly from recent theodolite survey data (8,356 ft ±8 ft, October 2022, verified by Eric Gilbertson). PeakVisor and latest USGS-derived data also support 8,356 ft. The discrepancy is within survey margin of error, but 8,356 ft is the most current authoritative measurement.

pitchDetail.notes: Well-maintained 11.5-mile trail, pleasant hiking with stream crossings. Elevation gain 950 ft. Typically 4–5 hours.
Dense brush, devil's club, and talus. Off-trail navigation. Steep scree above 3,800 ft. Approximately 4–5 hours, 1,500 ft gain.

pitchDetail.pitch: Access Col to Luna Pass ridge traverse
pitchDetail.notes: Steep heather and talus slopes. Possible crampon use for icy patches. Approximately 4.6 hours, 1,800 ft gain.

pitchDetail.pitch: Luna Pass to summit ridge approach
pitchDetail.notes: Rocky knob, winding ridge with alternating ups/downs. Narrow ramp descent on left (steep, exposed). Vertical drop-offs present. Approximately 2.5 hours.
25-foot vertical crack. Unroped passage feasible for experienced scramblers. Can be rope-protected or downclimbed. Rock quality poor (granodiorite with choss). 30–60 minutes.
Steep chute, loose rock, exposure. Approximately 500 ft descent to 6,500 ft. Route-finding required. 1–1.5 hours.

pitchDetail.pitch: Talus and polished slabs to glacier
pitchDetail.notes: Exposed ledge traverse (described as 'diciest' section by route documentation). Rotten rock. Approximately 1 hour to glacier base.

Approximately 300 ft vertical of 30–40 degree slopes, steepening to 50 degrees near summit. Large bergschrund requires direct ascent. S-shaped snowfield with crevasses. Running belay or solo travel depending on party experience. Approximately 1.5–2 hours to summit snowpatch.

40–50 degree slope, approximately 100 ft, poor runout. Narrow snow arête at top with two rocky points. Southwest point is higher, more accessible summit. Rock quality poor. Crampons essential. 30–45 minutes.

routes.overview: Committing solo masterpiece: one of the longest and most remote ridge lines in the lower 48 states. 4,000 ft of vertical rise over approximately 1 mile of skyline, interrupted by four major pinnacles (Rooster Comb, Pole of Remoteness) separated by deep notches. Technical rock (5.9–5.10), extensive scrambling, 12–13 rappels, and complex route finding. Requires 4 days, 60-mile round trip, with 60% off-trail approach.
routes.bestSeason: August for stable weather and firm snow conditions on glacier sections of approach. June carries avalanche risk during approach. September weather deteriorates rapidly. July and August both viable; early August optimal.
routes.approach: Approach is via East Fury summit first (see Southeast Glaciers route for detailed Luna Pass approach). From East Fury summit, descend northeast-facing descent to Luna Col, then traverse to Mongo Ridge start point. This multi-day effort carries 60-pound pack for extended expedition. Alternative Goodell Creek approach deemed prohibitively difficult by first ascensionist Wayne Wallace. Total approach: 50–60 miles round trip, 60% off-trail, 3 days typical to reach ridge base.
routes.itinerary: Day 1: Trailhead (Happy Flats) to Access Basin, 9+ hours. Day 2: Access Basin to Luna Pass/camp, 4.6 hours. Day 3: Luna Pass to East Fury summit and descent to Mongo Ridge start, full day (14+ hours). Day 4–6: Mongo Ridge climb and descent, typically 4 days solo for experienced alpinist. Day 7: Return descent to camp. (Total 7 days typical, or 4-day push for elite athletes.)
routes.descent: Retrace entire ridge line via rappels and downclimbing. Complex route finding on descent. Multiple key rappel points (12–13 total). Final descent via Luna Col back to East Fury and approach descent.
routes.descentText: Descent retraces ascent route via rappels and scrambling. Multiple rappel stations pre-established by first ascensionist; some diagonal and awkward angle descents. Significant route-finding challenge: darkness, fatigue, and complex terrain make descent as technical as ascent. One notable rappel was severed by rockfall during FA descent, cutting Wallace's rope—a hazard indicator for future parties. Final descent via same Luna Col approach to return to Luna Pass and Access Basin camps.
routes.beta: This is Wayne Wallace's solo masterpiece, first ascended August 24–28, 2006, in four days. The route ascends the south buttress as a continuous skyline ridge featuring four major pinnacles separated by deep notches: Rooster Comb (knife-edge horizontal traverse) and the Pole of Remoteness (notable pinnacle near summit) are the most famous features. Technical climbing is moderate but committing (5.9–5.10), mixed with extensive scrambling and mandatory rappelling. Rock quality is variable (granodiorite and choss); many ascents done unroped to save time, increasing risk. First ascent involved free-soloing sections up to 5.8. The route is one of the longest ridgelines in the lower 48 and one of the most remote alpine objectives in North America.

timing.recommendedStart: 4-day push typical; early morning starts mandatory for multi-pitch route

objHaz: Loose blocks and rockfall throughout ridge; one rappel was severed by falling rock during FA
Exposure: 4,000+ feet of vertical drop-off on both sides of ridge in many sections
Multiple gendarmes (pinnacles) requiring complex route finding around or over; some via knife-edge traverses
Complex rappel anchors and challenging anchor placement on poor rock
Committing terrain with limited bailout options; escaping mid-route is difficult
knownHazards: Loose rock quality: primarily granodiorite and quartz diorite with significant choss sections; anchors are unreliable
Rockfall hazard: notably from higher pinnacles during descent and from active climbing; helmet essential
Rappel hazard: one FA rope severed by rockfall; some rappels are diagonal or awkward, increasing entanglement risk
Route finding: complex terrain makes descent challenging in poor visibility; darkness and fatigue exacerbate hazard
Extreme remoteness: rescue is helicopter-only, weather-dependent, prohibitively expensive
watchOut: This route requires elite-level alpine skills, excellent rock climbing ability (comfortable at 5.10), solid rappelling technique, and expedition fitness
Route finding on descent is challenging; marked anchors are not provided; parties must locate FA rappel stations or establish their own
The route is predominantly described by the FA only; minimal second ascent data exists; expect unusual or unexpected challenges
Loose rock is pervasive; take all falling rock hazard seriously. Helmet and awareness are critical.
Weather on exposed ridge is severe; storms can trap climbers on mid-ridge with limited shelter. Turnaround judgment is critical.
proTips: This is a first-ascent line by a world-class solo alpinist. Unless you have comparable expertise, consider climbing the route with a partner or guide
Identify and pre-scout FA rappel stations from photos/trip reports if possible, but expect variation due to route finding or rock changes
The Rooster Comb knife-edge traverse is the crux mental crux—excellent protection and careful footwork on low-angle but exposed terrain
The Pole of Remoteness is a notable landmark and a mental milestone; the climbing remains sustained after this point
Many parties choose to partially solo or move unroped on lower-angle scrambling to save time; accept the risk consciously
Start early to maximize daylight and avoid rappelling in darkness. A 4-day push requires early morning starts (4–5 AM) and late evening finishes.
gear: Two 60-meter ropes (mandatory for rappelling a 4,000 ft ridge with 12+ rappels)
Extensive rock rack: 2–3 medium nuts (#4–#8), 3–4 small to medium cams (#0–#2), 2–3 slings for natural anchors
Harness and belay device (ATC guide or similar for rappelling)
Stiff alpine climbing boots with good edging and crampon compatibility
60+ pound expedition pack for 4-day alpine push with bivvy/emergency shelter
Ice axe (for self-arrest on steep sections and glacier approach)
Crampons (for glacier sections of approach and emergency conditions)
Bivvy sack and lightweight sleeping bag (emergency shelter, minimal weight)
Lightweight emergency tarp or space blanket
whatToBring: High-calorie expedition food for 4 days (minimal water weight; cache or purify streamwater)
Minimal cooking gear (fuel canister + lightweight stove for melting snow/warmth)
Headlamp with extra batteries (essential for pre-dawn starts)
Navigation: detailed topographic map, compass, GPS (backup to visual route finding)
First aid kit focused on blister prevention and pain management
Insulated jacket and emergency rain shell (weather protection on exposed ridge)
Sunscreen, lip balm, sunglasses (extreme UV exposure)
Satellite communicator or InReach device (emergency contact, no cell coverage)
Multi-tool and repair kit (emergency rope repair, hardware failure, etc.)
routes.detailedRack: Moderate rock rack: 2–3 medium nuts (sizes #4–#8 for natural anchors), 3–4 cams (sizes #0–#2 for mixed rock), 2–3 slings (for natural anchor placement on poor rock). Most anchor placements are on marginal rock (choss); reliance on natural anchors and sling placements is high. Rack is supplementary to extensive scrambling and rappelling; most pitches are simul-soloed or unroped to save time.
routes.proNeeds: Expert-level rock climbing and alpine skills required. Technical rock pitches (5.9–5.10) demand confidence in leading at grade and onsight climbing ability. Rappelling proficiency is essential (12+ rappels, some diagonal or awkward). Route finding and navigation in complex terrain are non-negotiable. Self-rescue capability strongly recommended given extreme remoteness. This is a committing alpine route with limited bailout; turnaround judgment and risk assessment must be excellent.

road.name: North Cascades Highway (WA-20)
road.status: Open year-round (occasional winter closures east of Ross Lake)
road.seasonalGate: No seasonal gate; Happy Flats trailhead accessible year-round via WA-20
road.driveNote: Drive to Happy Flats trailhead on WA-20 near Ross Dam (2,150 ft). Parking available. Water taxi (Ross Lake Shuttle) essential for Big Beaver approach; $60 each way, weather-dependent.

climate.forecastZone: Northwest Avalanche Center (NWAC) Picket Range / North Cascades zone
climate.typical: Highly variable alpine climate. Summer monsoon influences (July–August) bring afternoon thunderstorms and clouds. Early morning conditions often stable; afternoon deterioration typical. Wind can be severe on exposed ridge.
climate.spring: Not recommended. Deep snow, avalanche hazard, and crevassing during approach. Route is under 10+ feet of snow.
climate.summer: August optimal: daytime 40–55°F, nighttime 15–25°F. Snow diminishes significantly through August; less crevasse hazard. Afternoon thunderstorms possible but brief. Early July viable but more snow; late August earlier winter storms possible.
climate.fall: September weather unstable: increased cloud, rain at lower elevations, early season snow above 7,000 ft. Not recommended.
climate.winter: Route is under 10+ feet of snow; winter ascents are expert-only winter mountaineering objectives.

access.landManager: National Park Service (North Cascades National Park)
access.permit: Backcountry permit required. Recreation.gov or Wilderness Information Center in person. $6 non-refundable fee + $10 per-person per-night backcountry fee.
access.fees: $6 permit fee + $10 per-person per-night. Water taxi $60 each way. Optional: professional guide service (Alpine Institute, Mountain School).
access.passRequired: America the Beautiful Pass ($80/year) or $35 single-entry fee for vehicle at trailhead
access.closures: Cross-country zones can be closed mid-winter for avalanche hazard. Check park ranger status before planning.

emergency.rangerStation: North Cascades NP Wilderness Information Center: (360) 854-7245. After-hours: (360) 873-4590 ext. 37
emergency.nearestHospital: Skagit Valley Hospital (Mount Vernon, ~40 miles)
emergency.notes: Extreme remoteness: helicopter rescue is the only evacuation option and is expensive (often $30k+), weather-dependent. Carry satellite communicator.

routes.comms: No cell phone coverage. Satellite communicator (Garmin inReach, SPOT) or Iridium satellite phone required for emergency communication.
routes.corrections: First ascent date: Given as '2006' in initial data. Primary sources confirm August 24–28, 2006, by Wayne Wallace (solo). Alpinist Magazine article states August 1998 in one reference, but CascadeClimbers forum trip report explicitly dates the FA as 8/28/2006. Route is on West Fury (8,320 ft), not East Fury, but is included as a major objective associated with East Fury approach.

waypoints.name: Mount Fury West Peak (Mongo Ridge terminus)

pitchDetail.notes: Rock quality mixed (good granodiorite and choss). First pinnacle approached via prow work with exposed arête sections. Approximately 600 ft of vertical. Mostly scrambling with rock pitches 5.8–5.9. Unroped passage common to save time.
Deep notches between four major pinnacles. Descent via awkward scrambling and rappels (~12–13 total). Complex route finding. Loose rock quality throughout.

Horizontal knife-edge traverse, exposed on both sides, 1000+ feet of drop-off. Primarily balance and downsloping holds. Approximately 200 ft horizontal distance. Mental crux more than physical; protection placement difficult.

Sustained climbing on variable rock. Mix of rock pitches and scrambling. Approximately 1000 ft of vertical. Ascended mainly unroped by FA; technical crux sections include a 5.10 pitch noted as particularly challenging.
Steep prow work leading to notable final pinnacle. Knife-edge and exposed sections. Rope typically not used for protection on this section. 400+ ft to reach summit area.

Near summit, a 5.11 headwall bypass provides an exposed alternative route. Some parties descend and circle the obstacle; original ascent involved direct ascent via 5.10+ climbing. Route options depend on rock quality and party preference.
Final rocky points lead to west summit at 8,320 ft. Slightly lower than East Fury but marked on official USGS maps as the 'Mount Fury' main summit. Descent involves rappelling back down entire ridge.

blurb: Mount Daniel (7,960 ft) is the highest summit on the Cascade Range crest in the Alpine Lakes Wilderness and the highest point in both King and Kittitas counties. This glaciated massif features five distinct summits along an east-west ridge with extensive snow and ice fields. The mountain was first climbed in 1925 by The Mountaineers via Lynch Glacier, though surveyors likely reached it earlier.

routes.overview: A moderate glacier and scramble route combining approaches via Daniel Glacier and/or the Southeast Ridge to reach Mount Daniel's summits. Class 2-3 scrambling with glacier travel on the north-facing Daniel Glacier.
routes.fa: The Mountaineers, 1925 (original ascent via Lynch Glacier; this specific route variation documented later)
routes.beta: From Peggy's Pond, ascend braided cat-trails west through alpine meadows to gain the Southeast Ridge. This immense ridge narrows westward with Hyas Creek Glacier on the right and Circle Lake far below to the left. Follow an often-faint climber's path through Class 2 and 3 scrambles. For glacier travel, enter Daniel Glacier just north of a prominent spire on the Northeast Ridge and traverse west across the glacier to Middle Summit. The route can be combined with the pure ridge scramble depending on snow and crevasse conditions.
routes.approach: From Roslyn, take Highway 903 north through Ronald to Salmon La Sac Road. Continue 15.5 miles, then turn right onto FS 4330 (rough washboard gravel) for 12.3 miles to Cathedral Rock Trailhead at mile 27.8 (3,350 ft elevation). Trailhead parking accommodates approximately 18 vehicles. From the trailhead, follow the maintained Deep Lake Trail 4.5 miles through switchbacks to Cathedral Pass (5,500 ft), then continue to Peggy's Pond (5,600 ft). Total approach from parking: approximately 3-4 hours.
routes.descent: Reverse the route via the Southeast Ridge and descend the same way you ascended to Peggy's Pond, retracing the approach trail back to Cathedral Rock Trailhead.
routes.descentText: From the west summit, descend the Southeast Ridge carefully, retracing your route over Class 2-3 terrain, past the saddle below East Summit, down the faint climber's trail to the meadows at Peggy's Pond. The descent typically takes 2-3 hours to reach Peggy's Pond. From there, follow the established trail through Cathedral Pass and down the switchbacks to Cathedral Rock Trailhead.
routes.itinerary: Day climb: Depart Cathedral Rock Trailhead early (4-5 AM recommended). Hike to Peggy's Pond (3-4 hours), rest and prepare. Climb from Peggy's Pond to summit (2-3 hours). Summit and return to Peggy's Pond (2-3 hours including descent). Hike out to trailhead (3-4 hours). Total time: 12-14+ hours. Alternatively, camp at Peggy's Pond or nearby meadows for a 2-day approach.
routes.bail: From any point on the ridge, descend back toward Peggy's Pond. If weather deteriorates or you encounter crevasses on the glacier, return immediately via the Southeast Ridge scramble.
routes.turnaround: Maintain early turnaround discipline; plan to be off the exposed ridge and back at Peggy's Pond by 3 PM to ensure safe descent before darkness.
routes.bestSeason: July through September offer the best climbing conditions, with July often providing less mosquito activity but potentially more snow. August typically has the most snow-free sections but suffers from intense mosquitoes. June and September are viable but may have extended snow coverage requiring more glacier travel skills. Snow and ice typically linger until mid-July even in good years.
knownHazards: Crevasses on Daniel Glacier and other north-facing ice fields
Exposed scrambling on the Southeast Ridge with significant drop-offs to either side
Loose, steep scree and talus slopes requiring careful foot placement
Avalanche terrain on the north side and approach gullies, especially during spring or after fresh snow
Snow bridges over crevasses may be unreliable throughout the summer
watchOut: Route-finding is critical on the often-faint climber's trail; cairns and boot-beaten paths mark the way but are not always obvious in poor visibility
Significant exposure on both sides of the Southeast Ridge with loose terrain; a slip could be fatal
Crampons and ice axes are necessary even into late July on glacier sections
The summit area features five distinct summits; ensure you reach the true west summit (the highest point), not the benchmark on the east peak
Mosquitoes can be ferocious in late July and early August; bring adequate insect protection
proTips: Start at first light to maximize daylight hours on exposed terrain
Bring a detailed topo map and compass; the trail to Peggy's Pond is marked but off-trail portions require navigation skills
Scout the glacier approach in advance if you haven't been on Daniel Glacier before; crevasse patterns change seasonally
The route offers spectacular 360-degree views of the Cascades including Mount Rainier, Mount Adams, Glacier Peak, and Mount Baker
Peggy's Pond is an excellent turnaround point to assess conditions and decide whether to continue to the glacier or stick to the ridge
Consider ascending via the Southeast Ridge and descending via a different line for route variety and to practice different terrain
Water is plentiful but must be filtered; reliable water sources at Peggy's Pond and Cathedral Pass
The saddle below East Summit is a good rest point before committing to final scrambles
routes.proNeeds: A rope and basic glacier protection are recommended for the Daniel Glacier section, though many parties move together unroped due to the relatively low angle and moderate crevasse threat. Microspikes or crampons are essential; an ice axe is necessary for self-arrest capability on snow slopes. For the scramble sections, rock-climbing protection is not typically required, but a harness allows quick setup for rappel anchors if needed in an emergency.
gear: Glacier glasses or sunglasses with UV protection
whatToBring: Sun protection (sunscreen SPF 50+, lip balm, hat)
Gaiters (to keep snow out of boots)
Layers: base layer, mid-layer insulation, outer shell
Sleeping system if overnighting (15-30 degree bag, pad)
routes.detailedRack: For glacier travel: 60-meter rope (single or double), cordelette for crevasse rescue, carabiners (at least 4), ascenders, pulley, harness, and belay device. For rock protection: gear may be placed on Class 3 terrain, though most parties move unroped on these sections; if carrying pro, micronutz and quickdraws for scramble anchors.

pitchDetail.pitch: Cathedral Rock Trailhead to Cathedral Pass (4.5 miles)
pitchDetail.notes: Well-maintained switchback trail through forest and alpine meadows. Gain 2,150 ft. 1.5-2 hours.

pitchDetail.pitch: Cathedral Pass to Peggy's Pond (1 mile)
pitchDetail.notes: Alpine meadow traverse with some snow patches possible. Elevation 5,600 ft. 30-45 minutes.

pitchDetail.pitch: Peggy's Pond to Southeast Ridge saddle (2 miles)
pitchDetail.notes: Braided cat-trails through alpine meadows, then scrambling over talus and scree. Faint climber's trail over rocky terrain. 1-1.5 hours.

pitchDetail.pitch: Southeast Ridge saddle to East Summit (1 mile)
pitchDetail.notes: Exposed scrambling with significant drop-offs. Loose, steep scree. Class 3 gendarmes and moves. 45 min-1 hour.

pitchDetail.pitch: East Summit to Middle Summit via Daniel Glacier (1 mile)
pitchDetail.notes: Descend from East Summit, enter Daniel Glacier, traverse west on moderate-angle glacier with potential crevasses. 45 min-1.5 hours depending on conditions.

pitchDetail.pitch: Middle Summit to West Summit (0.5 miles)
pitchDetail.notes: Final scramble along ridge to highest summit (true Mount Daniel). Exposed but brief. 30-45 minutes.

road.name: Salmon La Sac Road to FS Road 4330
road.status: Passable (rough washboard gravel)
road.seasonalGate: FS 4330 may have seasonal restrictions; contact Cle Elum Ranger District at (509) 674-4411 for current status. Road typically opens in May-June after snow clearance.
road.driveNote: Final 10+ miles are bumpy washboard gravel with potential stream crossing (can be river-like during high runoff). High-clearance vehicle recommended. Allow 2.5-3 hours from I-90 at Cle Elum.

access.landManager: Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest, Cle Elum Ranger District
access.fees: Northwest Forest Pass: $5 day-use or $30 annual required for trailhead parking
access.permit: Free self-issue wilderness permit (available at trailhead); fill out and carry a copy, deposit carbon in box
access.passRequired: Northwest Forest Pass
access.closures: Alpine Lakes Wilderness has standard closure restrictions; Mount Daniel area is accessible year-round but snow-free access June-September. Check Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest alerts for road closures or emergency restrictions.

climate.forecastZone: Northwest Avalanche Center (NWAC) - Cascade Mountains forecast zone
climate.typical: Alpine environment with rapid weather changes. Expect cool temperatures at elevation (30-50°F summit, even in summer). Wind common on exposed ridges. Afternoon thunderstorms possible, especially July-August.
climate.spring: Heavy snow cover; approach roads may be closed; technical snowpack hazards; not recommended except for experienced mountaineers in May-early June
climate.summer: July-September: mostly snow-free at lower elevations; upper ridges may retain patches into July. Temperatures 40-60°F. Morning clear skies common; afternoon thunderstorms possible. Intense mosquitoes July-August.
climate.fall: September: cooling trend, increased chance of rain and early snow; route-finding becomes harder with diminished snow and potential early freeze-thaw cycles
climate.winter: November-March: substantial snow and ice; avalanche hazard significant; technical alpine climbing required; most access roads closed

emergency.sheriffDispatch: King County Sheriff: 911 or (206) 296-3311; Kittitas County Sheriff: 911 or (509) 962-7500
emergency.rangerStation: Cle Elum Ranger District (Okanogan-Wenatchee NF): (509) 674-4411
emergency.nearestHospital: Kittitas Valley Healthcare (emergency dept), Ellensburg, WA (~60 miles southeast of trailhead). Harborview Medical Center, Seattle, WA (~90 miles west, serves King County emergencies).
emergency.notes: Cell reception is unreliable at high elevations; satellite communicator (InReach or PLB) recommended. Helicopter rescue possible but weather-dependent. Evacuations typically via trailhead; park may coordinate with volunteer rescue (Seattle Mountain Rescue, King County SAR, or ESAR).

blurb: Mount Rahm (8,485 ft) is a dramatic alpine peak in North Cascades National Park's Chilliwack Mountains, located just 0.25 miles south of the Canada-US border. Named after geologist Dr. David Allan Rahm (1931-1976), the peak features steep volcanic rock and active glaciation, requiring technical mountaineering skills and glacier experience for summit attempts.

routes.fa: Joe Hutton, Peggy Hutton, Roy Mason; 1955
routes.overview: The standard mountaineering route to Mount Rahm combines alpine scrambling with glacier travel. The route ascends via a sustained Class 3 gully system to reach the glacier, where a steep snow/ice finger provides access to the summit with a notable schrund crossing at 7,700 feet.
routes.bestSeason: The best time to climb Mount Rahm is May through the first significant snowfall of autumn. Early season brings more snow coverage requiring better time management on crevasses, while late season offers better crevasse visibility but icier conditions. July through September provides the most favorable weather and stable snow.
routes.beta: Mount Rahm presents a challenging alpine climb requiring solid mountaineering skills, glacier travel competency, and rock scrambling ability. The standard route follows either the South Ridge approach from Ouzel Lake or the Maselpanik Creek approach, both converging on the mountain's upper basin before the final scramble to the west summit (8,485 ft). Class 3 rock climbing and glacier work are the primary technical elements.
routes.approach: The Maselpanik Creek approach, the shortest access (approximately 5 miles), begins from the Silver-Skagit Road near Hope, BC. Follow the gated logging road for about 10 miles to near 5,000 feet elevation, then hike southward across the border (requires documentation) to the area below Mount Rahm. This approach involves approximately 6-7 hours car to summit. Alternative: Depot Creek approach from Chilliwack Lake Road in BC (32.6 miles to rough road end), then approximately 8-mile hike to Ouzel Lake at 5,700 feet. From Ouzel Lake, climb northeast up the wide gully to the 7,380-ft Custer-Spickard Saddle, then traverse toward the upper SW basin and negotiate the 100-foot cliff separating the terrace from the upper basin.
routes.descent: Descend via the same route taken up, backtracking through the Class 3 gully and glacier. Pay careful attention to crevasse location during descent, especially in early season when crevasses may be snow-bridged. Use the outbound tracks when possible.
routes.descentText: Retrace your ascent route carefully. On the glacier, use existing boot tracks when available and watch for crevasse hazards, particularly after snowfall or in early season when crevasses are bridged. Descend the Class 3 gully methodically using rock and snow features as needed. At lower elevations, navigate the approach terrain carefully to avoid getting turned around in poor visibility.
routes.itinerary: Day 1: Drive to Maselpanik Creek trailhead (or Depot Creek for BC approach). Hike to base camp near the mountain (4-6 hours depending on approach). Day 2: Wake early, ascend Class 3 gully system, cross glacier, summit, and descend. Total climbing time 6-7 hours round-trip. In marginal conditions or via Depot Creek approach, this becomes a 2-3 day trip.
routes.bail: If weather deteriorates or you are not comfortable with conditions, retreat immediately down the gully system. Do not proceed to the glacier if visibility is poor or if you cannot identify the schrund location. On the glacier, if the schrund is impassable, reverse and exit via rock to either side.
routes.turnaround: Establish a firm turnaround time based on daylight available and your descent pace. Given the 6-7 hour timeline, plan to start by dawn to be off the glacier well before darkness. Do not attempt the glacier in marginal light conditions.
proTips: Start predawn from base camp to maximize daylight on exposed terrain and glacier
The schrund at 7,700 feet can often be climbed directly through soft snow, but rock on either side provides an escape if it is too wide
Early season (May-June) requires careful evaluation of avalanche terrain and crevasse hazards; later season offers more stable snow but icier conditions
The Class 3 gully can hold loose rock; wear a helmet and use care with rock fall hazards
Caster Ridge traverse to Mount Custer is a popular addition if you have extra daylight and are comfortable with Class 4 terrain
watchOut: Schrund crossing at 7,700 feet—can be awkward; scout both rock-side options before committing
Crevasses in early season are often snow-bridged and hidden; use extreme caution on the glacier
Exposed Class 3+ scrambling with significant drop-off; helmet essential and three-point contact recommended
Loose volcanic rock in the gully system; rockfall hazard and slow progress are common
Remote location with no cell coverage; satellite communication or PLB highly recommended
knownHazards: Glacier crevasses, especially early season when bridging obscures hazards
Bergschrund crossing at approximately 7,700 feet requiring technical climbing or rock bypass
Loose volcanic rock and rockfall in the Class 3 gully approach
Objective avalanche terrain in winter and early spring; assess stability before approach
Exposure on upper scrambling terrain with minimal protection available
Steep ice conditions late season; microspikes or crampons essential
routes.detailedRack: 30-meter rope (minimal anchor protection is limited on this peak), rock shoes or stiffer mountaineering boots, 2-4 quickdraws or runners for basic protection, carabiners (locking), glacier rescue kit (picket, shovel), belay device (ATC or similar).
routes.proNeeds: This route does not provide abundant natural protection. Gear protection consists mainly of place-able rock anchors on the Class 3 gully (stoppers, cams), but most alpinists do not place anchors above the glacier due to time constraints. Glacier travel is protected via rope team setup and crevasse rescue kit.
whatToBring: Mountaineering boots (B2 or B3 rated for crampon attachment), rigid gaiters
Down/synthetic jacket, hardshell jacket, thermal layers
Hiking pants, thermal bottoms, rain pants
Sunglasses with side protection, goggles for whiteout
High-calorie snacks, energy bars, electrolyte drink mix
Sunscreen (high SPF), lip balm, blister kit
Map and compass, GPS device (optional but recommended)
Satellite communicator or PLB (mandatory given zero cell coverage)

pitchDetail.notes: Lower gully approach with Class 3 scrambling on loose volcanic rock; short steps and sustained climbing on rock and snow features. Approximately 1,500 feet of elevation gain.

Upper gully section with sustained Class 3+ terrain and one short Class 4 sequence near the top where the gully walls lean back to a climbable face for ~100 feet. Snow finger or rock climbing depending on conditions; total 1,000+ feet of elevation.
Glacier crossing from the gully exit to the base of the summit finger. Rope-team travel recommended due to crevasse hazards. Approximately 800-1,000 feet of moderate-angle glacier.
Steep glacier finger ascending to the summit; schrund crossing at 7,700 feet (can climb through soft snow or bypass via rock). Final 600+ feet of climbing with increasing angle and exposure.

timing.recommendedStart: Pre-dawn (4:00-5:00 AM) to maximize daylight on exposed terrain

road.name: Maselpanik Creek Forest Road (BC, Canada)
road.status: Gated; key required
road.seasonalGate: Open May-October typically; confirm before attempting
road.driveNote: Access from Hope, BC via Silver-Skagit Road. Narrow, rough forest road; high-clearance vehicle strongly recommended. Approximately 10 miles to road end at ~5,000 feet. Requires gate key and Canadian entry documentation.

climate.forecastZone: WAZ567 (West Slopes North Cascades and Passes, National Weather Service)
climate.typical: Marine west coast climate with high precipitation, especially on west-facing slopes. Annual precipitation exceeds 100 inches, much as winter snowfall.
climate.spring: May-June: Frequent precipitation, significant snowpack, avalanche danger moderate to high; ice conditions firm to icy
climate.summer: July-September: Lower precipitation, more stable snowpack, improved visibility, but glaciers are icier and crevasses more visible; optimal climbing window
climate.fall: September-October: Increasing cloud cover and precipitation, earlier sunsets, snow conditions transitional
climate.winter: October-March: Continuous precipitation, heavy wet snow, very high avalanche danger, extremely limited daylight

access.landManager: North Cascades National Park / US National Park Service
access.permit: Backcountry permit required for overnight camping
access.fees: $10 per person plus $6 non-refundable reservation fee
access.passRequired: None (no entrance fee for park day-use), but Canadian documentation required for Maselpanik Creek approach (US citizens need passport or passport card)
access.closures: Seasonal gate closures on Maselpanik Road (typically November-April); check with BC Parks before planning. North Cascades National Park backcountry permits available online via Recreation.gov (60% of permits) or in-person at Wilderness Information Center (40% walk-up)

emergency.rangerStation: North Cascades National Park Wilderness Information Center, Marblemount, WA. Emergency contact: 360-873-4590
emergency.nearestHospital: Sedro-Woolley Hospital (approximately 90 minutes from trailhead via Marblemount)
emergency.notes: No cellular coverage in the area; satellite communicator (Garmin inReach, Spot) or Personal Locator Beacon strongly recommended. Closest ALS ambulance is from Concrete (1 hour away). Park rangers and EMTs may be available at Wilderness Center. Given the remote location and lack of cell service, self-rescue or helicopter evacuation scenarios are realistic.

routes.comms: Virtually no cellular coverage (Verizon, T-Mobile, AT&T) in the Mount Rahm area. A satellite messenger (Garmin inReach, Spot) or satellite phone (Iridium) is essential for emergency communication. Some iPhone 14+ models offer emergency satellite SOS, though coverage and reliability vary.

blurb: Buckner Mountain at 9,114 feet is the highest peak in Skagit County and the 14th highest in Washington state. Located in North Cascades National Park's Stephen Mather Wilderness, it features two nearly-equal summits and is renowned for the Boston Glacier's premier ice climbing on its north face, considered one of the finest alpine ice routes in the North Cascades.

routes.overview: The standard and least technically demanding approach to Buckner via steep snowfields and a short Class 2-3 scramble to the summit. The route ascends the southwest face with a distinctive cow-skull-shaped snowfield as a navigational landmark.
routes.bestSeason: July through September is optimal after avalanche danger has passed. June is possible but less ideal. Early season routes encounter significant snow coverage and crevasse hazards; late season offers more stable conditions and better rock scrambling access to the summit.
routes.beta: From Sahale Glacier Camp at 7,600 feet, descend the south ridge of Sahale Peak to Horseshoe Basin. Traverse across snowfields approximately 2 miles to the mountain's base. Ascend steep snowfields with the distinctive cow-skull-shaped snowfield as a navigational landmark. The climb involves navigating steepening snow chutes and slopes at 35-40 degrees. The final 100 feet is Class 2-3 rock scrambling to the summit block. Total route elevation gain from camp is approximately 3,000 feet.
routes.approach: From Marblemount, drive east on Cascade River Road (Forest Road 15) for 22.9 miles to Cascade Pass Parking Area (3,660 feet). Hike Cascade Pass Trail 3.7 miles (1,700 feet gain) to Cascade Pass (5,400 feet). Continue up Sahale Arm path through heather and wildflower meadows with steep ascent of rocky moraine slopes to Sahale Glacier Camps at 7,600 feet. This is the standard camping location for southwest approaches. Total approach distance: approximately 11.5 miles one-way from trailhead with 6,800-7,400 feet of elevation gain.
routes.descent: Descend the southwest face via gullies, scree, and fall-line snow to approximately 7,200 feet, then traverse down and right to cross Horseshoe Basin on a broad middle ledge system at 6,200-6,400 feet. Pass near Davenport Mine entrance and locate the snow finger at similar elevation. Ascend snow finger 100 feet to gain rounded ridge above ridge toe. Descend to Cascade Pass Trailhead via Horseshoe Basin or alternative Sahale Arm return.
routes.descentText: The descent from the summit goes southwest down the face via gullies and scree on the fall line until approximately 7,200 feet elevation. From there, climbers traverse down and right, crossing the cirque of Horseshoe Basin on a broad ledge system, typically taking the middle ledge at 6,200-6,400 feet. The traverse is mixed snow and loose talus/scree; earlier in season with better snow coverage, the route is more direct. Care must be taken for rockfall danger, though the wide face prevents teams from having to stay directly above each other. Most teams either continue down to Horseshoe Basin and traverse to Cascade Pass, or return via Sahale Arm to Cascade Pass Trailhead.
routes.itinerary: Day 1: Hike from Cascade Pass Trailhead (3,660 ft) to Cascade Pass (5,400 ft), then continue to Sahale Glacier Camp (7,600 ft). Total gain 3,700-3,900 feet. Day 2: Summit day from camp. Ascend southwest face to 9,114 feet and descend to camp. Total time 6-8 hours from camp. Day 3: Descend Horseshoe Basin and return to trailhead via Cascade Pass. Total loss 7,400 feet.
routes.bail: From the southwest face, climbers can bail downward via gullies to Horseshoe Basin at any point. From Horseshoe Basin, traverse to Sahale Arm and return via Cascade Pass if unable to descend safe passage. Turnarounds should be enforced by late morning on summit day to ensure safe descent in daylight.
routes.turnaround: A turnaround time of early morning is essential to ensure safe descent of the loose scree and rock before daylight fades. Establish a hard turnaround time and stick to it regardless of summit proximity.
watchOut: Early season (June) presents significant avalanche hazard; wait until July for safer conditions
Fog frequently obscures the route and summit area
The cow-skull snowfield is a navigational landmark but conditions vary by season
Lower Horseshoe Basin is problematic for descent; stay higher in the basin during traverse
Rock quality is poor; fall hazard would be fatal from upper terrain
knownHazards: Crevasse fields in approach glaciers and Horseshoe Basin require roped travel and crevasse rescue capability
Avalanche danger on steep slopes is considerable in early season; Route is safer July-September after consolidation
Rockfall is a constant hazard on the descent of loose, friable rock; the face is wide enough to allow spacing between rope teams
Weather can deteriorate rapidly; North Cascades climate is known for sudden storms
Loose talus and scree make for treacherous, time-consuming descent terrain
routes.detailedRack: Glacier approach requires 60m rope, 3 pickets for anchor placement, standard carabiners (4 screwgate, 2 snapgate), 2 prusik cords, and slings for crevasse rescue. Small rock rack of 3 cams and 6 nuts for final summit block. Helmet essential for rockfall protection.
routes.proNeeds: Parties should be efficient on moderate snow and able to climb Class 2-3 rock comfortably. Glacier travel experience and crevasse rescue skills are mandatory. Previous alpine mountaineering experience strongly recommended.
proTips: The distinctive cow-skull-shaped snowfield is a valuable navigational landmark; use it to stay on optimal route
Early morning starts are essential to avoid afternoon weather and time-consuming descent in poor conditions
Traverse across Horseshoe Basin relatively high; going too low on the basin costs significant time
Late season (August-September) offers better rock conditions and less snow, making the scramble faster
Snow conditions vary dramatically by year and season; check recent trip reports before committing
routes.comms: Cell service is unreliable or absent above tree line in the North Cascades backcountry. Satellite communicators or personal locator beacons are strongly recommended for emergency communication. iPhone 14+ satellite messaging via Globalstar available as emergency backup.

timing.recommendedStart: 5:00 AM from camp for summit push

climate.typical: Alpine climate with rapid weather changes. Heavy precipitation common. Temperature inversions frequent.
climate.spring: April-May: Unstable; significant snow depth, avalanche danger, and unpredictable temperatures
climate.summer: June-September: Most stable period. July-September: 35-65F daytime highs at summit, potential for severe thunderstorms
climate.fall: September-October: Cooling trend, increasing wind, early storms possible
climate.winter: November-March: Very cold (-5 to -15F summit temperatures), heavy snow, extreme avalanche danger, high winds. Guides offer March-October only.
climate.forecastZone: North Cascades National Park zone; use Northwest Avalanche Center forecasts and mountain-forecast.com for detailed predictions

access.landManager: National Park Service (North Cascades National Park)
access.permit: Required for overnight backcountry camping; permits are $10 per person per night via Recreation.gov
access.passRequired: Northwest Forest Pass not required within park for Cascade Pass Trailhead
access.fees: $10/person/night for backcountry permit; $6 fee for permit application via Recreation.gov
access.closures: Cascade River Road may have seasonal closures or mud-out periods; check conditions before driving. Maximum group size 4-12 depending on specific camp area

emergency.rangerStation: North Cascades National Park Ranger Station, Marblemount: (360) 854-7245 or noca_wilderness@nps.gov
emergency.nearestHospital: Skagit Valley Hospital, Mount Vernon, WA (approximately 45 miles from trailhead)
emergency.notes: Northwest Avalanche Center provides forecasts (nwac.us). Cell service absent at route; satellite communication essential

road.name: Cascade River Road (Forest Road 15)
road.status: Seasonal; may close or become impassable due to mud and snow
